summary: Adversarial JS↔C++ parity re-audit of hyperdht-cpp: fresh finders diff every subsystem against the JS reference, skeptics refute each finding, synthesis cross-references tonight fixes to catch regressions and blind spots
logs: Adversarial JS-parity sweep over 16 subsystems (all agents on opus 4.8; 6 hardest at xhigh effort).
91 findings survived adversarial verification. Cross-referencing against tonight's fixes.

confirmed.title: Eviction pings send once (retries=0) where JS retries 3× — comment claims JS 'only sends once'
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:826-872 (reping_and_swap, retries=0 at 855; false comment 853-854), 877-911 (check_node, retries=0 at 894; false comment 874)
confirmed.divergence: JS _check and _repingAndSwap issue their PING via _request, which uses the Request default retries=3 (io.js:366). On timeout, oncycle resends until sent>retries, i.e. up to 4 transmissions before onerror/ontimeout fires. C++ check_node and reping_and_swap explicitly pass retries=0, so a single request timeout is final. The C++ comments ('JS _repingAndSwap only sends once', 'Sends a single PING') are inaccurate.
confirmed.scenario: A well-established routing-table node drops ONE PING packet (transient loss). JS: PING retried up to 3 more times (~4s window at 1000ms timeout); node responds on a retry and is kept. C++: first timeout → check_node onerror → table_.remove(), or reping_and_swap ontimeout → node evicted and swapped. C++ churns the routing table on single-packet loss where JS tolerates 3 losses.
confirmed.consequence: C++ routing table is far more aggressive at evicting live-but-lossy nodes; poorer table quality under normal internet packet loss, more swap traffic.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: io.js:366 sets Request.retries=3; io.js:595-606 oncycle resends until sent>retries (up to 4 transmissions before onerror). index.js _repingAndSwap and _check call this._request -> io.createRequest without overriding retries, and the only retries overrides in JS (index.js:370 opts.retry===false, query.js:380-382) do not apply to these paths — so eviction pings get 3 retries in JS. C++: src/rpc.cpp:855 (reping_and_swap) and :894 (check_node) pass retries=0; rpc.cpp:570 uses the same sent>retries comparison, so retries=0 means one transmission and the first timeout is final, triggering table_.remove()/swap (rpc.cpp:867-871, 906-910). The comments at rpc.cpp:853-854 and :874 claiming JS sends once are factually wrong. Divergence is observable: C++ evicts an established node on a single lost PING where JS tolerates 3 losses. Working tree still has retries=0 at both sites, so not fixed. MED severity: routing-table churn under transient loss, self-healing, not a protocol or connectivity break.

confirmed.title: Per-peer adaptive request timeout is always on; JS uses a fixed 1000ms timeout by default
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/lib/io.js:78 (_adt = adaptiveTimeout ? ... : null), 457-459 (timeout = req.timeout || _adt?.get() || 1000); hyperdht/index.js:32 (super passes opts, never sets adaptiveTimeout); dht-rpc/index.js:26-30 (DEFAULTS has no adaptiveTimeout)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:1240-1282 (record_rtt/timeout_for; comment 1243-1252 claims 'JS uses the adaptive-timeout package'), 337-339 (send_now uses timeout_for)
confirmed.divergence: In JS dht-rpc/hyperdht the AdaptiveTimeout (_adt) is only constructed when opts.adaptiveTimeout is passed; neither DHT.DEFAULTS nor HyperDHT sets it, so _adt is null and every request timeout is the fixed 1000ms (unless req.timeout is overridden, e.g. DELAYED_PING). C++ always computes a per-peer EMA timeout of 2×smoothedRTT clamped to [200ms,5000ms] after the first response from a peer. The rpc.cpp comment asserting JS models this package is false for the default configuration.
confirmed.scenario: Node repeatedly pings a low-RTT peer (RTT≈50ms). After the first response C++ records rtt and subsequent pings use timeout=max(200, 2·50)=200ms. If that peer later answers in 300ms (jitter), C++ times out at 200ms; combined with tick-1 (retries=0) it evicts the peer. JS uses 1000ms and keeps it.
confirmed.consequence: Different retry/eviction timing than JS; on fast links C++ times out ~5× sooner, amplifying tick-1's table churn; on slow links C++ waits longer. Observable via routing-table membership and query fan-out timing.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: io.js:78 constructs AdaptiveTimeout only when opts.adaptiveTimeout is truthy, else _adt=null; io.js:458 with this.timeout=0 (io.js:368) falls through to fixed 1000ms. dht-rpc/index.js DEFAULTS (lines 26-30) and hyperdht/index.js:32 super() never set adaptiveTimeout (grep over both packages shows zero callers), so default JS timeout is a fixed 1000ms. C++: rpc.cpp:744-747 records RTT unconditionally on every response; rpc.cpp:337-339 send_now always uses timeout_for() (no disable option); rpc.cpp:1272-1281 returns 2×EMA clamped to [200,5000] once a peer has one recorded RTT. Observable divergence: low-RTT peers get 200ms timeouts in C++ vs 1000ms in JS, causing spurious timeouts under jitter. The rpc.cpp:1243-1252 comment claiming JS-parity via the adaptive-timeout package is wrong for the default config. Not fixed in working tree. MED: heuristic timing divergence with real eviction-pressure consequences, not a protocol break.

confirmed.title: Node with no bootstrap seeds never becomes bootstrapped, so the background tick does nothing forever
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:379-404 (_bootstrap always runs the walk and sets bootstrapped=true / emits ready, even with an empty bootstrap list)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/dht.cpp:279-281 (start_bootstrap_walk called only if !opts_.bootstrap.empty()); src/dht_network.cpp:53-109 (set_bootstrapped(true) fires only in the walk's on_done); src/rpc.cpp:942 (background_tick returns early while !bootstrapped_)
confirmed.divergence: JS _bootstrap unconditionally performs a background FIND_NODE walk and, regardless of whether any seed exists, flips bootstrapped=true and emits ready. C++ only starts the walk when opts_.bootstrap is non-empty, and set_bootstrapped is reachable only from the walk's completion. With an empty bootstrap list the RpcSocket's bootstrapped_ stays false, so background_tick's early return at line 942 permanently disables ping_some, refresh, NAT recheck, and the persistent transition.
confirmed.scenario: Construct a HyperDHT with bootstrap:[] (seed/first node, or the bootstrapper which does opts.bootstrap.clear() in dht.cpp:380). JS: node becomes ready and participates. C++: node never becomes bootstrapped; it never pings, refreshes, or transitions to persistent — it is an inert DHT participant.
confirmed.consequence: C++ seed/isolated nodes and self-run bootstrappers hang unbootstrapped and never advertise an id or maintain their table.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:379-402 — _bootstrap unconditionally runs _backgroundQuery(this.table.id) (empty seed list just finishes immediately) and then sets this.bootstrapped=true and emits 'ready' with no seed-count gate; _ontick:775 gates on bootstrapped, so the tick body always goes live. C++ side (working tree with uncommitted changes): src/dht.cpp:279-281 starts the bootstrap walk only if !opts_.bootstrap.empty(); grep confirms set_bootstrapped(true) has exactly one call site (src/dht_network.cpp:87, inside the walk's on_done), and bootstrapped_ defaults false (rpc.hpp:440); src/rpc.cpp:942 (background_tick) and rpc.cpp:799 both early-return while !bootstrapped_. So with bootstrap:[] (seed node, or the bootstrapper factory which clears the list at dht.cpp:380) the C++ node never becomes bootstrapped: no check_persistent, no ping_some, no refresh, no health/eviction — permanently, exactly as claimed. Divergence is real and observable. Severity corrected to MED: default clients/servers using the 3 public seeds are unaffected; only seed/first-node and bootstrapper deployments hit it (the bootstrapper also sets ephemeral=false up front, so it escapes the missed persistent transition, but its table maintenance is still fully dead).

confirmed.title: Persistent transition derives node id/port from the CLIENT socket's external port, not the SERVER socket's (JS rebuilds the sampler from PING_NAT replies)
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:801-875 (_updateNetworkState: natSampler=new NatSampler at 818, id=peer.id(natSampler.host,natSampler.port) at 831, this._nat=natSampler at 842), 916-963 (_checkIfFirewalled samples natSampler from PING_NAT replies whose `to` carries our server port at 929/940 and verifies natSampler.port===serverSocket port at 954)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:725 (nat_sampler_ fed from responses = client-socket external addr while firewalled), 1159-1200 (finish_firewall_probe: port check compares client_external vs client_local at 1185-1196, never server port), 1219-1238 (do_persistent_transition: id = compute_peer_id(nat_sampler_.host():nat_sampler_.port()) at 1225-1232)
confirmed.divergence: JS runs _checkIfFirewalled with a fresh NatSampler that is populated only by the PING_NAT replies, whose `to` field carries our SERVER-socket external address (the remote sets req.from.port to the value we sent = server port). It then computes the persistent id and replaces this._nat from THAT server-derived sampler. C++ keeps the single nat_sampler_ that (while ephemeral/firewalled) was filled from ordinary responses to client_socket_ requests — i.e. the CLIENT socket's external port. do_persistent_transition computes the id from nat_sampler_.port() (client external), and the firewall-probe port-preservation check only validates the client port; the server-port PING_NAT replies are dropped by nat_sampler_'s visited_ dedup because those relay nodes were already sampled.
confirmed.scenario: On a port-preserving NAT where the probe passes (client_external==client_local), client and server sockets still have different local→external ports. C++ goes persistent with table id = peer.id(host, clientExternalPort) but then sends all DHT traffic from server_socket_ (serverExternalPort). Peers run validateId = peer.id(source_host, source_port)=peer.id(host, serverExternalPort) ≠ our advertised id → they reject our id, drop us from their tables and null our id on responses.
confirmed.consequence: After a successful ephemeral→persistent transition the C++ node advertises an id/port that does not match the socket it transmits from, defeating the purpose of going persistent (peers won't store it, announces/lookups keyed on its id misbehave).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: dht-rpc/index.js:818 creates a FRESH NatSampler when firewalled, _checkIfFirewalled (916-963) populates it exclusively from PING_NAT pongs' res.to (our server-socket external address, since remotes reply to the server port we sent at 929) and requires natSampler.port === serverSocket.address().port (954); index.js:831 then derives the persistent id from that server-derived sampler and 842 installs it as this._nat. C++ side verified: src/rpc.cpp:725 feeds the single long-lived nat_sampler_ from ordinary responses, which while firewalled all leave client_socket_ (so samples = client external port); finish_firewall_probe (1159-1201) checks only client_external vs client_local (1185-1196); PING_NAT replies arriving on server_socket_ only bump probe_replied_hosts_ (608-616) and their nat_sampler_.add calls are dropped by visited_ dedup (src/nat_sampler.cpp:99-104) because those nodes already contributed samples (and ≤5 server-port samples could not outrank the dominant client-port sample anyway, since port() = samples_full_[0] by hits). do_persistent_transition (1219-1236) computes new_id from nat_sampler_.port() = client external port, then active_socket() (rpc.hpp:258-264) switches all traffic to server_socket_, and outgoing responses carry table().id() (rpc_handlers.cpp:200,290,545,592,650). Peers recompute peer.id(source_host, source_port) (JS io.js:619, C++ rpc.cpp:728-737) — source port is the server socket's external port, so the advertised id never matches and peers null/drop it. The divergence is observable and applies not only to port-preserving NATs but to ANY host going persistent (client and server sockets always have different ports), so every persistent C++ node advertises an id that fails validateId — it cannot participate as a routing-table member. Not fixed in the working tree; the comment at rpc.cpp:1179-1188 documents the client-port check as intentional, misreading the JS. HIGH severity is correct.

confirmed.title: opts.ephemeral is never applied and the `adaptive` gate is missing from tick/wakeup
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:53 (adaptive = typeof opts.ephemeral!=='boolean' && opts.adaptive!==false), 76 (_stableTicks = adaptive?STABLE_TICKS:0), 777 (_ontick gate: adaptive && ephemeral && --_stableTicks), 560-570 (_onwakeup reverts ephemeral only if adaptive), 104-120 (bootstrapper: ephemeral:false)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:946 (background_tick gate: only `ephemeral_ && --stable_ticks_`), 994-996 (do_wakeup reverts ephemeral_ unconditionally); src/dht.cpp:379 (bootstrapper sets opts.ephemeral=false but nothing applies it — grep shows no set on socket, only set_firewalled(false) at 387)
confirmed.divergence: JS gates both the persistent-transition countdown (_ontick) and the wakeup ephemeral-revert on `this.adaptive`, and adaptive is false whenever ephemeral is explicitly set. It also honors opts.ephemeral (bootstrapper forces ephemeral:false and the bootstrap loop transitions it to persistent immediately). C++ has no `adaptive` concept and never applies opts.ephemeral to the RpcSocket: ephemeral_ is hardcoded true at construction and can only change via the firewall-probe path, and do_wakeup always reverts to ephemeral.
confirmed.scenario: (a) C++ bootstrapper: opts.ephemeral=false is ignored, ephemeral_ stays true; nat_sampler seeded with one self-sample stays firewall=UNKNOWN so check_persistent never even starts the probe → the bootstrapper never becomes persistent, never advertises its id, and replies id-less (JS bootstrapper is persistent and advertises table.id). (b) A user requesting forced-ephemeral (JS: never persistent) instead auto-transitions in C++.
confirmed.consequence: Forced-persistent (bootstrapper/server-with-ephemeral:false) and forced-ephemeral configurations both diverge; a C++ bootstrap node is effectively non-functional as a DHT id-holder.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: dht-rpc/index.js:53 (adaptive = typeof opts.ephemeral!=='boolean' && opts.adaptive!==false), :71 (_forcePersistent = opts.ephemeral===false), :76 (_stableTicks = adaptive?STABLE_TICKS:0), :777 (_ontick gates countdown on adaptive && ephemeral), :560-570 (_onwakeup reverts ephemeral only if adaptive), :104-120 + :379-398 + :801-874 (bootstrapper: ephemeral:false/firewalled:false → _bootstrap with _forcePersistent → _updateNetworkState skips probe since firewalled already false, sets ephemeral=false and rebuilds table with peer.id(host,port) immediately). C++ side verified: src/rpc.cpp:946 gate is only `ephemeral_ && --stable_ticks_` (no adaptive), src/rpc.cpp:996-998 do_wakeup reverts ephemeral_ unconditionally, rpc.hpp:416 hardcodes ephemeral_=true, and the full HyperDHT ctor (src/dht.cpp:81-198) never reads opts_.ephemeral — dht.cpp:379 sets it and ffi_core.cpp:97 plumbs it, but nothing consumes it; force_check_persistent (rpc.hpp:318) is test-only (grep: only test files call it). Scenario mechanics confirmed: bootstrapper's single self nat-sample keeps firewall UNKNOWN (nat_sampler.cpp:111 requires sampled_>=3) so check_persistent (rpc.cpp:1089) never starts the probe at startup → C++ bootstrapper stays ephemeral and replies id-less (rpc_handlers.cpp:190) where the JS bootstrapper is persistent immediately; and an explicit ephemeral:true user auto-transitions in C++ where JS (adaptive=false) never does. Divergence is real and unfixed in the working tree. Downgraded to MED because the default adaptive client/server path is behaviorally identical, the affected modes (bootstrapper deployment, forced-ephemeral) are niche, and the bootstrapper can eventually self-heal via response samples (rpc.cpp:725) once refresh traffic flows.

confirmed.title: NAT sampler is fed only by responses (and holepunch), never by incoming DHT requests, and never re-samples known peers
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:632-635 (_onrequest → _addNodeFromNetwork(!external, req.from, req.to)), 705-707 (_onresponse), 483-521 (_addNodeFromNetwork: existing node re-samples when sampled===0 or _tick-sampled>=OLD_NODE, at 497-501), 463-472 (_natAdd)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:697-717 (handle_message REQUEST_ID branch: adds node but makes NO nat_sampler_.add call), 725 (only the RESPONSE_ID branch samples NAT); src/nat_sampler.cpp:97-104 (visited_ permanently dedups a source, never re-samples)
confirmed.divergence: JS samples our external address (`to`) from BOTH inbound requests (when req.from.id!==null and !external) and responses, and re-samples an already-known node whenever its last sample is ≥OLD_NODE (30 min) old, so _nat tracks external-address changes over time. C++ samples the DHT-level sampler only on responses (rpc.cpp:725); the REQUEST_ID path decodes req.to but never feeds the sampler, and nat_sampler_'s visited_ set never expires, so a peer contributes at most one sample ever.
confirmed.scenario: A mostly-receiving node (established peer that others FIND_NODE/PING but which sends few outbound requests): JS derives NAT samples from those inbound requests; C++ derives none. Separately, a long-lived node whose NAT remaps its external port: JS re-samples known peers after 30 min and updates _nat / emits nat-update; C++ never re-samples known peers, so the remap is invisible to the DHT layer.
confirmed.consequence: Slower/incomplete NAT classification for receive-heavy nodes and no detection of NAT/external-address changes over the node's lifetime.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side, all read directly: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:632-634 _onrequest calls _addNodeFromNetwork(!external, req.from, req.to) whenever req.from.id !== null (io.js:89 sets external = socket !== expectedSocket, so the normal case is external=false → sample=true); index.js:705-707 _onresponse does the same. _addNodeFromNetwork:497-501 re-samples a known node when oldNode.sampled===0 or _tick - oldNode.sampled >= OLD_NODE (360 ticks ≈ 30 min, index.js:24) and calls _natAdd → nat.add + 'nat-update' emit (index.js:462-471); a brand-new node samples immediately in _addNode:531-534. So JS feeds _nat from BOTH inbound requests and responses, and refreshes each node's contribution every 30 min. C++ side, all read directly: src/rpc.cpp handle_message REQUEST_ID branch (rpc.cpp:697-717) calls only add_node_from_network(expected, req.from.addr) — grep confirms nat_sampler_.add appears exactly once in the file, at rpc.cpp:725 inside the RESPONSE_ID branch; add_node_from_network (rpc.cpp:769-791) touches only the routing table, no sampler, and Node has no to/sampled fields. src/nat_sampler.cpp:96-104: NatSampler::add dedups by source ref via visited_ (returns false if seen before), visited_ is only cleared in reset(), and a MAX_VISITED=1024 cap makes add() return false for ALL new sources once 1024 unique peers have ever been sampled. The code comment at nat_sampler.cpp:93-95 explicitly marks the dedup as a deliberate deviation, but it lacks JS's 30-min expiry, so the observable behaviors genuinely diverge: (1) a mostly-receiving C++ node collects no DHT-level NAT samples from inbound requests, and (2) a long-lived C++ node never re-observes a NAT external-port remap (and after 1024 unique sources, sampling halts permanently), whereas JS re-samples every 30 min and emits nat-update. Not fixed in the working tree. Severity MED rather than HIGH: initial classification and connect still work (outbound query/ping responses from not-yet-visited peers feed the sampler, and table churn supplies fresh sources for a while), but long-lived deployments (the nospoon VPN case) will carry a stale external address after a NAT remap until restart.

confirmed.title: Bootstrap lacks the quick-firewall PING_NAT heuristic and the second NAT pass (single-pass walk)
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:379-433 (_bootstrap: up to 2 passes, ondata fires PING_NAT on the first responder, sets testNat, then _updateNetworkState(onlyFirewall)); 389-399 (first/testNat loop)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/dht_network.cpp:40-51 (comment acknowledging the gap), 53-109 (start_bootstrap_walk: single Query walk, no quick-NAT PING_NAT, no second pass, no _updateNetworkState during bootstrap)
confirmed.divergence: JS's _bootstrap sends a PING_NAT to the first responder during the bootstrap walk and, on success, runs _updateNetworkState(onlyFirewall=true) to clear the `firewalled` flag early, and loops the walk up to twice. C++ does a single FIND_NODE walk with no quick-firewall probe and no in-bootstrap network-state update; the earliest firewall determination happens only via the stable-tick check_persistent path.
confirmed.scenario: A non-firewalled node comes up: JS clears its `firewalled` flag within the bootstrap window (seconds) and starts using its server socket; C++ leaves firewalled_=true (traffic on client socket) until the first STABLE_TICKS expiry (240 ticks ≈ 20 min) triggers the firewall probe.
confirmed.consequence: Firewall status / socket-selection optimization is delayed by up to ~20 minutes relative to JS for non-firewalled nodes (documented as a known follow-up, but still an observable behavioral divergence).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS (.analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:379-433): _bootstrap loops the walk up to 2 passes; ondata sends PING_NAT (server port as uint16) to the FIRST responder when firewalled && _quickFirewall (default ON, index.js:70) && !_forcePersistent; a reply sets testNat, then _updateNetworkState(onlyFirewall=true) (index.js:800-824) runs _checkIfFirewalled and clears this.firewalled=this.io.firewalled=false within the bootstrap window. C++ (src/dht_network.cpp:53-109): start_bootstrap_walk is a single FIND_NODE query with no PING_NAT, no second pass, no network-state update — lines 47-50 explicitly acknowledge this as a "§15 follow-up". The only production firewall-probe trigger is background_tick (src/rpc.cpp:946-950) gated on stable_ticks_ starting at STABLE_TICKS_INIT=240 (rpc.hpp:49, 240×5s=20 min); force_check_persistent() is test-only (all callers in test/). So a non-firewalled C++ node stays firewalled_/ephemeral on the client socket for ~20 min where JS flips in seconds — real, observable, not fixed in tree. Severity corrected to MED: JS's own source comment calls quick-firewall failure "nbd" because the onstable hook (the same 20-min path C++ relies on) picks it up; the gap is a missing acceleration with a bounded, self-healing 20-minute window, not a correctness failure.

confirmed.title: Tick and refresh counters are not randomly offset at startup (JS randomOffset)
confirmed.divergence: JS seeds _tick to a random value in (50,100] and _refreshTicks to a random value in [30,60] specifically to de-synchronize network ticks across nodes ('make sure to random offset all the network ticks'). C++ starts tick_ at 0 and refresh_ticks_ at the fixed 60, so the (tick&7)/(tick&63) ping/refresh phases and the first refresh are deterministic.
confirmed.scenario: Many C++ nodes started near-simultaneously all reach (tick_&7)==0 and their first refresh on the same ticks, firing ping_some/refresh in lockstep; JS spreads these across the interval.
confirmed.consequence: Thundering-herd synchronization of periodic DHT maintenance traffic across a fleet, and first-refresh timing fixed at 60 ticks vs JS 30–60. Low correctness impact; matters at scale.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS index.js:74-75 seeds _tick=randomOffset(100) (range (50,100]) and _refreshTicks=randomOffset(60) (range (30,60]) via randomOffset(n)=n-((Math.random()*0.5*n)|0) at index.js:1046-1048, explicitly to de-sync network ticks. C++ rpc.hpp:437 has tick_=0 and :415 refresh_ticks_=REFRESH_TICKS(60) fixed; grep of src/ and include/ shows no random seeding anywhere (only wake-up refresh_ticks_=1 at src/rpc.cpp:990 and post-refresh reset at :1047, both JS-parity). Phase logic is otherwise identical ((tick&7) ping at src/rpc.cpp:956 vs index.js:786, (tick&63)/--refreshTicks at :962-963 vs :791-792), so simultaneously-started C++ nodes fire ping_some/refresh in lockstep where JS spreads them. Minor correction: stable_ticks_ (rpc.hpp:418) is NOT part of the divergence — JS _stableTicks is also fixed (index.js:76). Severity LOW: fleet-level thundering-herd nicety, no per-node correctness or wire-protocol impact.

confirmed.title: Invalid-token requests are silently dropped instead of getting an INVALID_TOKEN error reply
confirmed.divergence: JS validates the token at the IO layer for EVERY request that carries one (before dispatch): if it matches neither current nor previous secret it calls req.error(INVALID_TOKEN, {token:true}) — sending an error response with code=2 AND a freshly-issued valid token (io.js:99, error() uses token===true → true, closerNodes default true). C++ handle_message performs NO token check; each write handler (announce/unannounce/mutable_put/immutable_put) validates the token itself and on failure just returns with no reply at all. C++ also has no INVALID_TOKEN error code defined (messages.hpp only has SEQ errors).
confirmed.scenario: A client announces/puts with a token that has aged past the ~15s two-window overlap (token rotated out). JS server replies error=2 plus a brand-new token so the client can immediately re-announce. C++ server answers with nothing → the client's request times out (4×1000ms), the whole query re-runs, and the client never receives a recovery token from the C++ node.
confirmed.consequence: Announce/put refresh against a C++ node stalls on a full RPC timeout+re-query instead of a fast INVALID_TOKEN round-trip; a JS peer probing token validity observes a dead node instead of a protocol error.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS io.js onmessage (lines 94-101) validates req.token against token(from,1) and token(from,0) before onrequest and on mismatch calls req.error(INVALID_TOKEN, {token:true}); error() (io.js:419-423) sends _sendReply(2, null, true, true, ...) — an error reply carrying a fresh valid token plus closer nodes; INVALID_TOKEN=2 at errors.js:16. C++ working-tree RpcSocket::handle_message (src/rpc.cpp REQUEST_ID branch) has no token check — it validates the sender ID and dispatches directly to on_request_. All four write handlers in src/rpc_handlers.cpp (handle_announce ~526, handle_unannounce ~615, handle_mutable_put ~669, handle_immutable_put ~789) do a bare `return` when token_store().validate() fails, sending no reply; grep confirms no INVALID_TOKEN constant exists in src/ or include/ (messages.hpp has only ERR_NONE/ERR_SEQ_REUSED/ERR_SEQ_TOO_LOW). Divergence is real and observable: stale-token clients get an instant error=2 + recovery token from JS but a full 4-attempt timeout from C++. Severity corrected to MED: exploiting the gap requires a token older than the 7.5-15s two-secret window, which is rare in practice since writes normally follow token issuance within milliseconds; impact is degraded recovery latency, not broken functionality or security.

confirmed.title: Unknown / ungated commands get no UNKNOWN_COMMAND error reply
confirmed.divergence: JS dht-rpc _onrequest replies req.sendReply(UNKNOWN_COMMAND=1, ...) for any unrecognized INTERNAL command (index.js:679), and for external commands the app declines it replies UNKNOWN_COMMAND (index.js:684-685). HyperDHT's onrequest returns false while ephemeral (index.js:404 `if (this._persistent === null) return false`), so an ephemeral node still emits an UNKNOWN_COMMAND reply for storage commands. C++ hits `default: break;` (no reply) for unknown internal commands, and `if (socket_.is_ephemeral()) return;` (no reply) for storage commands while ephemeral.
confirmed.scenario: A JS client issues a LOOKUP/FIND_PEER/ANNOUNCE against a C++ node that is still ephemeral, or sends an internal command the C++ build doesn't implement. JS ephemeral node replies error=1 (node is alive but can't serve); C++ node stays completely silent.
confirmed.consequence: The querying peer times out on a live C++ ephemeral node (mis-reads it as dead/unresponsive, may evict it) instead of receiving the UNKNOWN_COMMAND signal JS provides; query dynamics and remote routing-table health diverge.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js:679 — after the internal switch (PING/DELAYED_PING/PING_NAT/FIND_NODE/DOWN_HINT all `return`), any unrecognized internal command falls through to `req.sendReply(UNKNOWN_COMMAND, null, false, req.target !== null)`; index.js:684-685 — `if (this.onrequest(req) === false) req.sendReply(UNKNOWN_COMMAND, ...)` for external commands. errors.js:15 confirms UNKNOWN_COMMAND = 1. .analysis/js/hyperdht/index.js onrequest: PEER_HANDSHAKE/PEER_HOLEPUNCH return true unconditionally, then `if (this._persistent === null) return false` — so an ephemeral JS node replies error=1 (with closerNodes when target present, since the 4th sendReply arg is `req.target !== null`) for FIND_PEER/LOOKUP/ANNOUNCE/etc.; the function also ends with `return false` for unknown external commands. C++ side verified in working-tree src/rpc_handlers.cpp RpcHandlers::handle(): internal switch ends `default: break;` with no reply; external path handles PEER_HANDSHAKE/PEER_HOLEPUNCH, then `if (socket_.is_ephemeral()) return;` (silent drop, comment even cites the JS line but omits that the dht-rpc layer above still sends UNKNOWN_COMMAND), and the persistent storage switch also ends `default: break;` with no reply. grep for UNKNOWN_COMMAND/error=1 across src/ and include/hyperdht/ finds no error-reply mechanism — not fixed in tree. Observable divergence is real: a JS client hitting an ephemeral C++ node with LOOKUP/FIND_PEER gets a timeout (and no closerNodes to advance the query) instead of an immediate error=1 reply, which slows queries and can get the C++ node marked down. Severity MED: wire-observable and hits a common early-lifecycle path, but the ephemeral window is bounded and no crash/data loss results.

confirmed.title: Request retry timeout is per-peer adaptive in C++ but flat 1000ms in real JS (adaptive-timeout not enabled)
confirmed.divergence: JS only builds an AdaptiveTimeout when the `adaptiveTimeout` option is passed to IO (io.js:78). Grep confirms hyperdht never passes it, so this._adt is null and every send uses `req.timeout || 1000` = a flat 1000ms (io.js:458). C++ send_now always uses timeout_for(peer): 2×EMA-RTT clamped to [200ms,5000ms] (rpc.cpp:1272-1282), seeded to 1000ms only for a peer with no prior sample. The C++ comment at rpc.cpp:1243-1252 asserts "JS uses the adaptive-timeout package" as if for parity, but the deployed JS path is flat 1000ms.
confirmed.scenario: After one successful exchange with a nearby peer (RTT≈50ms), a follow-up request loses a packet. C++ retransmits after 200ms and exhausts 4 attempts in ~800ms; JS waits 1000ms per attempt, ~4000ms total. Conversely for a 600ms-RTT peer C++ waits 1200ms vs JS 1000ms.
confirmed.consequence: Different retransmit cadence and ~5× faster give-up on known-fast peers → premature duplicate requests and earlier failure declaration than a JS node; observable timing/traffic divergence and comment overstates parity.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: io.js:78 only constructs AdaptiveTimeout when the adaptiveTimeout option is passed; dht-rpc index.js:40 spreads opts into IO, but grep of the whole .analysis/js/hyperdht tree shows the option is never set, so _adt is null and _sendNow (io.js:454-459) uses `this.timeout || null || 1000` = flat 1000ms per attempt. C++: rpc.cpp:337-342 send_now always calls timeout_for(req->to); rpc.cpp:747 feeds record_rtt on every response, so after one exchange timeout_for (rpc.cpp:1273-1282) returns 2×EMA-RTT clamped [200,5000] instead of DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS=1000 (rpc.hpp:40). The parity comment at rpc.cpp:1240-1252 cites the JS adaptive-timeout package as justification, but that code path is dead in deployed hyperdht. Observable divergence: for a ~50ms-RTT peer C++ retries after 200ms and exhausts attempts ~5x faster than JS; a 400ms-jittered reply is a timeout in C++ but on-time in JS, so liveness marking and query failure timing differ. Timing-only, not wire-breaking — MED.

confirmed.title: PING and FIND_NODE replies carry a token in C++ but never in JS
confirmed.divergence: JS PING replies `sendReply(0,null,false,false)` and FIND_NODE replies `sendReply(0,null,false,true)` — both pass token=false, so neither response sets the RESP_FLAG_HAS_TOKEN bit or emits the 32 token bytes. C++ make_query_response unconditionally sets resp.token; handle_ping only clears closer_nodes and handle_find_node clears nothing, so both C++ replies include the token flag + 32 bytes.
confirmed.scenario: Any JS peer that PINGs or FIND_NODEs a C++ node receives a response with the token flag set and 32 extra bytes that a JS node would never send.
confirmed.consequence: Wire-format divergence on the two most common message types (extra 32 bytes/token flag per reply). Non-breaking (announce/put derive tokens from LOOKUP/GET/ANNOUNCE query replies, which legitimately carry tokens on both sides), but a strict decoder or byte-exact cross-test will differ.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/index.js _onrequest — PING replies `req.sendReply(0, null, false, false)` and FIND_NODE replies `req.sendReply(0, null, false, true)`; the third arg is `token` (io.js:447 `sendReply(error, value, token, hasCloserNodes)`), and io.js:485-513 `_sendReply` only sets flag bit 2 and encodes the 32-byte token when `token` is truthy. So JS PING/FIND_NODE responses carry no token flag and no token bytes. C++ side verified in working tree: src/rpc_handlers.cpp:202 `make_query_response` unconditionally sets `resp.token = socket_.token_store().create(...)`; handle_ping (224-228) clears only closer_nodes and handle_find_node (257-269) clears nothing, and src/messages.cpp:74/84/103 sets RESP_FLAG_HAS_TOKEN + encodes 32 bytes whenever resp.token has a value. The wire divergence is real and observable (flag bit 2 set, 32 extra bytes). Severity LOW, not higher: the JS decoder handles the token flag generically so nothing breaks, the token is a valid host-bound token the peer could obtain anyway via any query command (FIND_PEER/LOOKUP use req.reply with token defaulting to true, io.js:416), so there is no security or interop failure — only a wire-fingerprint/parity mismatch. Fix is trivial: clear resp.token in handle_ping and handle_find_node (make_query_response's unconditional token is correct for the other query commands).

confirmed.title: rpc::Session cannot cancel congestion-queued requests and double-counts congestion differently
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:399-409 (pending not added to inflight_), 263-268 (find_inflight scans inflight_ only), 1288-1328 (Session::request/destroy)
confirmed.divergence: In JS a request is added to io.inflight immediately in createRequest (io.js:337) even when it is congestion-queued in _pending, so session.destroy()/req.destroy() finds and removes it. In C++ a congestion-full request goes ONLY into pending_ (rpc.cpp:407) and is not in inflight_; Session::destroy → cancel_request → find_inflight scans inflight_ only, so a still-pending request is never cancelled — it is later drained and sent, and its wrapped callback dereferences the Session's captured `self` (rpc.cpp:1303/1309). Additionally JS session.destroy calls congestion.recv() once itself plus once inside req.destroy (two recv per req, session.js:43 + io.js:480); C++ does a single recv in destroy_request.
confirmed.scenario: A Session issues enough parallel requests to exceed the congestion window (some land in pending_), then the Session is destroyed. C++ leaves the pending ones queued: they still transmit, and when a reply arrives the erase-on-self lambda runs against a Session whose lifetime may have ended (use-after-free); JS cancels them cleanly.
confirmed.consequence: Extra network traffic + late callbacks after Session teardown, potential UAF, and a small congestion-count mismatch. Currently only reachable via tests (rpc::Session is instantiated only in test/test_rpc_handlers.cpp, not production), so impact is latent.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: io.js createRequest pushes every request into io.inflight and session._attach BEFORE the congestion check; a congestion-full send() only additionally queues it in _pending. session.js:39-46 destroy() therefore finds and destroys queued requests too (req.destroy removes from io.inflight, marks destroyed; _sendNow no-ops on destroyed), and does two congestion.recv() per request (session.js:43 + io.js destroy). C++: src/rpc.cpp:398-409 puts a congestion-full request ONLY into pending_, not inflight_; cancel_request→find_inflight (rpc.cpp:253-268) scans inflight_ only, so Session::destroy (rpc.cpp:1319-1327) silently fails to cancel it; nothing else can set req->destroyed on a pending request (timer never started, socket destroy() at 505-516 frees pending_ without the flag), so drain_pending (345-357) later sends it and the wrapped callbacks (rpc.cpp:1297-1313) dereference the captured raw `self` Session pointer — the safety comment at 1292-1295 is false for this path, giving a genuine latent use-after-free (~Session at rpc.hpp:533). The single-vs-double congestion.recv() difference is also textually real. Divergence CONFIRMED and not fixed in the working tree. Severity corrected to MED: rpc::Session has zero production callers (grep hits only rpc.hpp comments and test_rpc_handlers.cpp, whose tests never exceed the congestion window), so the UAF is latent in a public class rather than reachable from any current pipeline.

confirmed.title: NAT sampler is fed only from responses, not from incoming requests
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:697-717 (REQUEST path — no nat_sampler_.add), 718-738 (RESPONSE path — only sampler feed)
confirmed.divergence: JS _onrequest calls _addNodeFromNetwork(sample=!external, req.from, req.to), and _addNodeFromNetwork feeds the `to` field (how the requester observed our external address) into the NAT sampler via _natAdd — so BOTH incoming requests and responses drive NAT/external-address discovery. C++ handle_message calls nat_sampler_.add only in the RESPONSE_ID branch (rpc.cpp:725); the REQUEST_ID branch never samples the request's `to` field.
confirmed.scenario: A C++ node that is queried frequently (many FIND_NODE requests arrive, each carrying a `to` address) but issues few outbound queries converges on its external host/port more slowly than a JS node in the same position.
confirmed.consequence: Slower/less-complete external-address sampling → delayed or differently-timed ephemeral→persistent transition and firewall probe relative to JS.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: index.js:632-634 _onrequest calls _addNodeFromNetwork(!external, req.from, req.to); _addNodeFromNetwork feeds `to` into _natAdd both on the known-node refresh path (index.js:495-501, gated on sample && staleness) and unconditionally on new-node insertion via _addNode (index.js:531-534). io.js:89 defines external = socket !== expectedSocket, so requests arriving on the sampler's socket do drive NAT sampling in JS. C++: src/rpc.cpp:697-717 REQUEST branch calls add_node_from_network(expected, req.from.addr) and never touches nat_sampler_; the decoded req.to (messages.hpp:81) is discarded; rpc.cpp:725 in the RESPONSE branch is the only nat_sampler_.add in handle_message; add_node_from_network (rpc.cpp:769-791) does no sampling. Divergence real and observable (slower external-address convergence/remap detection on serve-heavy nodes). Severity MED not HIGH: the sampler is still fed by the node's own outbound traffic (bootstrap, pings, announces), so this degrades freshness rather than breaking discovery. Any fix must replicate JS's !external gating since nat_sampler_ tracks a single socket's mapping.

confirmed.title: alloc_tid can return 0, colliding with the request() failure sentinel
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/lib/io.js:318-320 (tid 0 is a normal value; failure signaled by null req, not 0)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:247-251 (alloc_tid), 165 (next_tid_ seeded rd()&0xFFFF), 376/405 (request returns 0 on failure), 1314 (Session tracks only tid!=0)
confirmed.divergence: C++ uses tid 0 as the 'request failed / queued-cap / closing' sentinel returned by request(). alloc_tid avoids 0 in steady state, but when next_tid_ is seeded to 0 by std::random_device (constructor), the first alloc_tid returns 0 for a genuine, fully-inflight request. JS assigns tid via _tid++ where 0 is a valid transaction id and never overloads it as a failure signal (createRequest returns the req object / null).
confirmed.scenario: 1-in-65536 process starts seed next_tid_=0; the first request gets tid 0, is created/sent/inflight, but request() returns 0. Callers that treat 0 as failure (and Session::request, which only tracks tid!=0 at rpc.cpp:1314) mis-handle a live request — it becomes untracked/uncancellable while its callbacks still fire.
confirmed.consequence: Rare (startup, 1/65536) but real: a valid inflight request is reported as failed and, through a Session, is silently untracked.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS io.js:315-320 verified: createRequest returns null on destroy and assigns tid via `if (this._tid === 65536) this._tid = 0; const tid = this._tid++` — tid 0 is an ordinary value, failure is out-of-band. C++ verified: rpc.cpp:164-165 seeds next_tid_ = rd()&0xFFFF (0 with p=1/65536); alloc_tid (rpc.cpp:247-251) does `tid = next_tid_++; if (next_tid_ == 0) next_tid_ = 1` — the 0-skip runs after handing out the tid, so a 0 seed yields tid 0 for a real, fully-inflight request; request() (rpc.cpp:376/405/413) returns 0 as its failure sentinel and msg.tid on success, making success-with-tid-0 indistinguishable from failure; Session::request (rpc.cpp:1314) only tracks `tid != 0`, so the tid-0 request is untracked/uncancellable while its callbacks (capturing `self`, dereferencing self->tids_ at 1303/1309) still fire — Session::destroy (1324-1327) cannot cancel it, so a Session destroyed mid-flight yields a use-after-free, contradicting the safety comment at 1291-1295. Divergence is real and unfixed in the working tree. Severity corrected to LOW: trigger requires a 1/65536 startup seed, affects exactly one request per process lifetime, and the UAF additionally requires the Session to die while that request is inflight; fix is a one-liner in alloc_tid.

confirmed.title: Query walk uses RPC default retries=3 (4 sends); JS uses 5 (6 sends)
confirmed.divergence: JS sets req.retries = this.retries, whose default for any non-DOWN_HINT query is 5 (query.js:28-29). io.js oncycle resends until sent > retries, i.e. sends at sent=1..6 then gives up → 6 transmissions per node. C++ Query::visit calls the 2-arg socket_.request(), which hard-codes retries = DEFAULT_RETRIES = 3; on_request_timeout gives up when sent > retries → only 4 transmissions. C++ Query exposes no way to raise per-visit retries. hyperdht/index.js lookup/findPeer/announce/immutable*/mutable* do NOT override retries (only connect.js does), so every dht_ops walk diverges 5 vs 3.
confirmed.scenario: A FIND_PEER/LOOKUP/ANNOUNCE walk hits a reachable-but-lossy node on a congested path. JS retransmits up to 6 times before declaring it DOWN; C++ gives up after 4. Under moderate loss C++ marks more nodes DOWN, accrues more errors_, and is likelier to trip the successes_<k/4 table-retry and to commit to a smaller closest set.
confirmed.consequence: Fewer successful node contacts per query on lossy networks → weaker announce/put coverage and earlier (spurious) node eviction than the JS reference.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: .analysis/js/dht-rpc/lib/query.js:28-29 sets `this.retries = opts.retries === 0 ? 0 : opts.retries || (this.internal && command === DOWN_HINT ? 3 : 5)` — default 5 for every non-DOWN_HINT query. query.js:380-383 (_visit) assigns `req.retries = this.retries`. io.js: `this.sent = 0` (line 365), `this.sent++` on each send (line 453), and oncycle (line 595-606) destroys only when `req.sent > req.retries` — with retries=5 the request is transmitted at sent=1..6, i.e. 6 sends. Of the hyperdht callers, only connect.js:347 overrides retries (1 or 3); lookup/findPeer/announce/mutable/immutable use the default 5. C++ side: src/query.cpp:257 (Query::visit) calls the 2-arg `socket_.request(req, on_response, on_timeout)`, which src/rpc.cpp:361-365 forwards with `DEFAULT_RETRIES` (include/hyperdht/rpc.hpp:39 = 3). src/rpc.cpp:328 increments `sent` per send and rpc.cpp:570 gives up when `sent > retries` — same counting semantics, so 4 sends vs JS's 6. No per-visit retries override exists on Query; the working tree even documents the gap itself (src/query.cpp:159-161: "JS retries failed visits up to opts.retries (default 5). C++ relies on the RpcSocket-level retry policy"). Divergence is real and observable: under packet loss C++ declares nodes DOWN after 4 transmissions where JS persists to 6, inflating errors_ and shrinking closest sets. Severity MED — no wire-format or correctness break, but it degrades query resilience on lossy paths for every dht_ops walk. Not fixed in tree (the comment acknowledges it as a known diff, no compensating code).

confirmed.title: Commit phase never signals failure: empty-closest, all-timeout, and tokenless-only all report SUCCESS
confirmed.divergence: JS _endAfterCommit: (a) if the commit set is empty (no closestReplies) it destroy()s the query with 'Too few nodes responded'; (b) each commit promise settles, and if the LAST one settles with success===0 it destroy()s with the error; (c) autoCommit rejects for a reply with no token, so tokenless replies count as failed commits. immutablePut/mutablePut await query.finished(), so all three destroy() paths REJECT to the caller. C++ do_commit only collects replies with a token (query.cpp:531-534), so tokenless replies are dropped from the set; if the resulting target set is empty it fires on_done as success (536-540); and the per-commit finish() always calls fire_done_once() when commit_inflight_ hits 0 regardless of commit_success_ (549-555). commit_success_ is incremented but never used. The C++ OnDoneCallback has no error parameter, so failure is unrepresentable.
confirmed.scenario: announce()/immutable_put()/mutable_put() to a target while the network path is down so every store request times out (or the walk reached zero closest nodes). JS: query.finished() rejects → caller's put throws / announce errors. C++: on_done_ fires with the closest vector and no error → caller believes the announce/put succeeded when nothing was stored.
confirmed.consequence: Silent false-success on failed announce/put: a node reports it announced its address when no peer actually stored it, so remote peers cannot find it, with no error surfaced.
confirmed.verify_reasoning:
JS side, read directly: dht-rpc/lib/query.js:211-223 `_flush` builds one commit promise per closestReplies entry; :225-229 `_endAfterCommit` destroys with `new Error('Too few nodes responded')` when the set is empty; :238-247 the settle handlers destroy(err) when the last settle arrives with success===0 (any success → push(null)); :393 `autoCommit` rejects with 'No token received for closest node' for tokenless replies; :82-105 `finished()` rejects on destroy-with-error. hyperdht/index.js:297 (immutablePut) and :387 (mutablePut) `await query.finished()` — so all three destroy paths reject to the caller.

C++ side, read directly in the current working tree: src/query.cpp:531-534 do_commit collects only replies with a token (tokenless silently dropped); :536-540 empty target set → `done_=true; fire_done_once()` which invokes on_done_ with closest_replies_ — indistinguishable from success; :549-555 per-commit `finish` fires done when commit_inflight_ hits 0 regardless of commit_success_, which is written at :523/:550 and read nowhere else in src/ or include/ (grep confirmed). include/hyperdht/query.hpp:53 `OnDoneCallback = std::function<void(const std::vector<QueryReply>&)>` — no error parameter, so failure is structurally unrepresentable. Downstream confirms no recovery channel: dht_ops.cpp announce/immutable_put/mutable_put just forward on_done; dht.hpp:613/650 ImmutablePutResult/MutablePutResult have no error field; ffi_storage.cpp:16/38/57 hardcode `cb(0, userdata)` (status 0 always).

Observable divergence is real: with all store requests timing out (or zero closest replies), JS immutablePut/mutablePut/announce throw; C++ reports success at every layer down to the FFI. HIGH because put/announce are one-shot API calls whose false-success means data silently not stored, and the error is unrepresentable in the whole callback chain, so no caller can work around it.

confirmed.title: Error responses (m.error != 0) are surfaced to on_reply; JS never emits them as data
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/query.cpp:292-293,343-352; include/hyperdht/query.hpp:37-43 (QueryReply has no error field)
confirmed.divergence: JS _onvisit returns early after `if (m.error !== 0) { this._readMore(); return }`, so map()/push() (the query's data stream, i.e. the consumer callback) only ever sees error===0 replies. C++ on_visit_response computes is_success but calls on_reply_(reply) unconditionally for every response, error or not, and QueryReply carries no error field so the consumer cannot filter. (closerNodes and success/error counters are handled correctly on both sides; only the reply-surfacing gate differs.)
confirmed.scenario: During a LOOKUP/FIND_PEER walk a node replies with error=UNKNOWN_COMMAND (or any error!=0) plus closerNodes. JS never yields it to the lookup/findPeer consumer; C++ dht_ops::lookup/find_peer forward it straight to the caller's on_reply. Value-less error replies make value-checking handlers (immutable_get/mutable_get) no-op, but generic lookup consumers observe spurious replies they cannot distinguish from real ones.
confirmed.consequence: Consumers of on_reply for lookup/find_peer receive error replies the JS reference filters out, and have no error field to reject them.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS .analysis/js/dht-rpc/lib/query.js _onvisit: after counters/closerNodes handling it has `if (m.error !== 0) { this._readMore(); return }` immediately before `const data = this.map(m); if (!data || this.push(data) !== false) ...` — error replies never reach the consumer stream. C++ src/query.cpp on_visit_response: computes is_success (gates push_closest and successes_/errors_ correctly) but calls on_reply_(reply) unconditionally, and QueryReply (include/hyperdht/query.hpp:37-43) has fields from_id/from_addr/token/value/closer_nodes only — no error field, so consumers cannot filter. src/dht_ops.cpp lookup/find_peer (lines 47-80) forward the caller's on_reply unfiltered; immutable_get/mutable_get (lines 211-212, 339-341) bail on missing value so they are unaffected in practice. Divergence is real and unfixed. Severity LOW: closest-replies list, commit phase, and slowdown counters all match JS; error replies normally carry no value so value-parsing consumers already ignore them — only generic on_reply observers see spurious replies. Fix is a one-line is_success gate before the on_reply_ dispatch.

confirmed.title: DOWN_HINT emission and rate-limiting entirely absent from the query walk
confirmed.js_ref: dht-rpc/lib/query.js:140-169 (_seen refs),279 (ref=m.from),298-332 (_onerror + _downHint); dht-rpc/index.js:86-88,797 (rate limit + per-tick reset)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/query.cpp:131-142 (add_pending tracks no refs),329-335 (closerNodes loop passes no ref),375-386 (on_visit_timeout marks DOWN, emits nothing)
confirmed.divergence: JS tracks, per seen address, the list of nodes that referred it (refs), marks an address DOWN on REQUEST_TIMEOUT, and fires a DOWN_HINT RPC to every referrer (and again whenever a DOWN address is re-encountered with a ref), subject to dht._downHintsRateLimit (default 50) reset each tick. C++ Query keeps only a PENDING/DONE/DOWN enum with no ref list, and on_visit_timeout marks DOWN but sends no DOWN_HINT; the rate-limit machinery does not exist here.
confirmed.scenario: A node A is offered as a closerNode by referrer B, then times out during the walk. JS sends a DOWN_HINT to B so B can re-verify/evict A; C++ sends nothing.
confirmed.consequence: Dead-node gossip is missing, so stale nodes linger in peers' routing tables longer than the JS reference maintains. Wire-safe (omitting packets cannot break peers). Documented gap.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: query.js:140-167 _addPending tracks per-address refs arrays and fires _downHint(ref, node) on DOWN re-encounter (line 152); query.js:279 passes m.from as ref; query.js:298-310 _onerror on REQUEST_TIMEOUT marks DOWN and downHints every ref; query.js:318-332 enforces dht._downHintsRateLimit (default 50, index.js:86-88) reset per tick (index.js:797). Checked the potential refutation at index.js:452 (_sendDownHints gate): default is TRUE (opts.sendDownHints !== false, index.js:85) and hyperdht never sets it, so JS really does send. C++ side verified in current working tree: src/query.cpp add_pending (lines ~141-152) keeps only addr→PENDING/DONE/DOWN enum, no refs; on_visit_timeout (lines ~375-386) marks DOWN, bumps errors_, sends nothing; comments at query.cpp:128 and :367 explicitly document 'no DOWN_HINT yet'. Grep shows C++ implements only the RECEIVE side (rpc_handlers.cpp:282 handle_down_hint + rpc.cpp ping-check); no emission path exists. Divergence is real and on-the-wire observable. Severity corrected to LOW: missing DOWN_HINT emission is altruistic network-hygiene gossip with zero impact on the local node's query results, connectivity, or holepunch — JS even offers sendDownHints:false as a supported no-consequence opt-out.

confirmed.title: _slow oncycle counter (additive concurrency + slow-completion early flush) not ported
confirmed.divergence: JS req.oncycle fires on each retry cycle, incrementing this._slow. Effective concurrency is (slowdown?3:concurrency)+_slow, so retrying requests WIDEN the parallelism; and read_more can enter the flush/commit path early when `_slow === inflight && closestReplies.length >= k` (all remaining in-flight requests are slow and a full result already exists). C++ has neither: effective_concurrency is slowdown?3:concurrency with no +_slow, and it only flushes when inflight_ == 0.
confirmed.scenario: Under packet loss several visits enter retry. JS opens extra concurrency and, once it has k closest with only slow requests left, commits without waiting for them. C++ keeps static concurrency and blocks the flush until every in-flight request fully times out.
confirmed.consequence: Slower query completion under loss, and a potentially different (more-complete but later) committed closest set. Result eventually matches; latency/timing only. Documented gap.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS verified: query.js:179 adds _slow to effective concurrency; query.js:196-199 enters flush when _slow===inflight && closestReplies.length>=k; _oncycle (query.js:312-316) increments _slow and is fired per retry cycle by io.js:459/595-598 via req.oncycle set in _visit (query.js:381); _dec (query.js:250-257) balances the counter. C++ verified: src/query.cpp:181 uses slowdown_?3:concurrency_ with no additive term; lines 206-215 flush only on inflight_==0, with a comment explicitly admitting the _slow gap; no oncycle/per-retry hook exists in rpc.hpp/rpc.cpp/query.hpp. Divergence is real and observable — under packet loss C++ blocks flush until all retry chains exhaust while JS widens concurrency and commits early — but it is latency-only (C++ comment "final result matches" is essentially correct; JS's early flush actually drops late replies via the _commiting guard). Deliberate documented deferral, not an accidental bug. MED because every query type is affected under loss and queries feed time-bounded holepunch flows, but no wrong results.

confirmed.title: opts.onlyClosestNodes not implemented
confirmed.cpp_ref: include/hyperdht/query.hpp / src/query.cpp (no such flag; add_pending always proceeds)
confirmed.divergence: JS _addPending returns false immediately when _onlyClosestNodes is set, so a query with opts.onlyClosestNodes visits ONLY its seeded nodes and never expands the frontier from responses. C++ Query has no such option; add_pending always merges closerNodes.
confirmed.scenario: connect.js sets onlyClosestNodes when reconnecting with a cached closestNodes set (paired with retries:1). C++ neither exposes the flag nor implements the relay-address cache that triggers it, so the confined-walk behavior is unavailable.
confirmed.consequence: No divergence on the currently-ported paths (the only JS caller is the unported cached-reconnect path); a latent gap if that reconnect optimization is later added. LOW.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: dht-rpc/lib/query.js:41 initializes _onlyClosestNodes=false, line 69 sets it from opts.onlyClosestNodes AFTER the seed loop (lines 51-67), and line 141 makes _addPending return false immediately when set — so _addFromTable/_open add nothing and _onvisit's merge loop (line 279, `if (!this._addPending(node, m.from)) break`) never expands the frontier. connect.js:346 sets `onlyClosestNodes: closestNodes !== null` where closestNodes comes from opts.relayAddresses, dht._relayAddressesCache, or the persistent router route (connect.js:320-333). C++ side verified: include/hyperdht/query.hpp:71-137 exposes no such option (only set_concurrency/set_commit/set_internal/add_seed_node); src/query.cpp:131-142 add_pending has only seen-set + MAX_SEEN + filter checks, and line 334 unconditionally merges closerNodes from responses. So the confined-walk capability genuinely does not exist in C++ — the finding stands as a missing feature. Severity corrected to LOW, not higher: no C++ code path could set the flag today even if it existed, because all three JS triggers are absent (no relayAddresses connect opt, no _relayAddressesCache — explicitly listed in CLAUDE.md's TODO "Missing JS Parity Features" — and no persistent router-route shortcut in connect). There is no wrong behavior in any currently-reachable path; the gap only blocks the reconnect fast-path optimization, and implementing the flag is a trivial bool gate in add_pending once the relay-address cache lands. Not fixed in the working tree — grep for only_closest/onlyClosest in include/ and src/ returns nothing.

confirmed.title: No relay→direct upgrade: onsocket/changeRemote replaced by single-shot first-wins complete()
confirmed.divergence: JS onsocket (453-487) branches on rawStream.connected: on first call it connect()s + encryptedSocket.start(); on a LATER call (already connected via relay) it calls rawStream.changeRemote() to migrate the live UDX stream from the relay to the punched direct address. isDone() (138-153) deliberately returns false while a relaySocket is up and the puncher is still running, so relay + holepunch COEXIST and the puncher keeps going after the relay has connected. C++ has no changeRemote at all (grep of src/ and include/ finds none) and state->complete() (126-147) is a one-shot guarded by `completed` — the first path (relay OR holepunch OR direct) to finish wins permanently and every other path is dropped by the `if (state->completed) return` guards.
confirmed.scenario: Client sets relayThrough to a non-holepunchable server. Relay pairing completes before the holepunch lands. JS: connection comes up on the relay immediately, then when the puncher succeeds onsocket() does changeRemote() and the UDX stream migrates to the direct path. C++: relay wins, state->completed=true, the holepunch success callback (773) hits the `if (state->completed) return` guard and is discarded.
confirmed.consequence: C++ connection stays pinned to the relay forever (relay bandwidth cost + extra latency), never upgrading to the direct path JS would switch to. Symmetric risk: if holepunch is the slower path, C++ forfeits the fast relay connectivity JS gets first. This is the documented relay→direct upgrade the branch is named for; it is not implemented in these files.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides. JS connect.js:453-487 — onsocket branches on rawStream.connected: first call connect()+start(), later call rawStream.changeRemote() migrates the live relayed UDX stream to the punched direct address, then destroys the puncher; isDone() (138-153, specifically 148-150) returns false while relaySocket is up and the puncher is still running, so relay and puncher coexist and a late punch upgrades the connection via firewall()→onsocket(). C++ src/connect.cpp — complete() (126-147) is one-shot (`if (completed) return` at 127); the holepunch success callback (772) and every other path carry `if (state->completed) return` guards; relay pair success (965) locks in the relay socket permanently. grep of src/, include/, wrappers/ finds zero uses of changeRemote/udx_stream_change_remote. Divergence is real and observable: relay-first connections in C++ never upgrade to direct and the successful punch is discarded. Not fixed in the working tree: connect.cpp is unmodified on branch fix/relay-direct-upgrade; only the design doc docs/RELAY-UPGRADE-PORT.md exists, and CLAUDE.md line 300 still lists changeRemote as a missing parity feature. HIGH: permanent relay-path traffic (latency, relay load, relay-dies-connection-dies) is the documented root cause of the nospoon connected-no-data issue this branch was created to fix.

confirmed.title: maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket guards missing: holepunch/NO_ADDRESSES/passive-timeout abort kills an in-flight relay
confirmed.divergence: JS maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket (718-725) returns early — i.e. does NOT destroy the socket — when `c.relaySocket` is set (relay pending) or `c.puncher && !c.puncher.destroyed` (puncher pending). So CANNOT_HOLEPUNCH, HOLEPUNCH_ABORTED, probe failure, etc. become no-ops while the relay is still pairing. C++ error completions call state->complete(err) with no such guard: holepunch failure → complete(HOLEPUNCH_FAILED) (774), no-holepunch/empty-addresses → complete(NO_ADDRESSES) (649), OPEN-firewall passive-wait timeout → complete(HOLEPUNCH_TIMEOUT) (691). complete() sets completed=true, and the relay pair-success callback then bails at `if (state->completed || ...) return` (920).
confirmed.scenario: relayThrough set; server IS holepunchable so holepunch_connect() runs in parallel with start_relay_path(). The holepunch probe loop fails (double-random NAT / probe timeout) before the relay finishes pairing. C++: holepunch callback fires complete(HOLEPUNCH_FAILED) → completed=true → the still-pairing relay is abandoned. JS: the same holepunch failure routes through maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket, which is a no-op because c.relaySocket is set, so the relay proceeds and wins.
confirmed.consequence: relayThrough connections fail in C++ in exactly the case relaying exists for (holepunch unreliable), whereas JS falls back to the relay and succeeds.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: connect.js:718-725 — maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket returns early when c.relaySocket is set (721) or puncher live (722); every holepunch error path (onabort 223-226 incl. passive timeout 229, LAN ping fail 245, CANNOT_HOLEPUNCH 254, probe error 273, abort() 743) routes through it, so a pending relay survives punch failure. relayConnection sets c.relaySocket (762) at handshake completion (489-491), before holepunch(c, opts) runs (193), so the two genuinely race and the relay wins on punch failure. C++: src/connect.cpp — start_relay_path launched at 602, then error completions at 649 (NO_ADDRESSES), 691 (HOLEPUNCH_TIMEOUT, guarded only by !completed), and 774 (HOLEPUNCH_FAILED) call state->complete(err) with no relay/puncher guard; complete() (126-128) sets completed=true and fires on_done(err); the relay connect callback (839) and pair-success callback (920) both bail on state->completed, abandoning the in-flight relay after the user already got a failure. Observable divergence: with relayThrough set and an unpunchable NAT, JS connects via relay, C++ fails the connect. connect.cpp is unmodified on branch fix/relay-direct-upgrade and no guard exists in the working tree. HIGH because it defeats the relay fallback exactly when holepunching cannot succeed.

confirmed.title: reusableSocket entirely ignored: handshake flag hardcoded false, route cache read+write ungated
confirmed.divergence: JS gates all three reusableSocket behaviors on the flag: retryRoute only runs `const route = c.reusableSocket ? _socketPool.routes.get(...) : null` (177); the handshake advertises `udx.reusableSocket = c.reusableSocket` (406); routes.add only runs under `if (c.reusableSocket && payload.udx.reusableSocket)` (473). C++ ignores opts.reusable_socket completely (grep finds no `reusable` in connect.cpp or dht.cpp): peer_handshake hardcodes UdxInfo{...,false,...} (peer_connect.cpp:336 & 431); complete() adds the route whenever result.udx_socket!=nullptr with no flag/echo gate (138-142); the route shortcut reads get_route(remote_pk) unconditionally (474-475).
confirmed.scenario: App calls connect() twice for the same peer WITHOUT reusableSocket. JS: nothing cached, second connect goes straight to findPeer. C++: connect #1 caches a route; connect #2 fires a route-shortcut PEER_HANDSHAKE to the (possibly stale) cached address before findPeer. Separately, an app that DOES request reusableSocket never has it advertised to the server, so the feature never engages.
confirmed.consequence: Extra/incorrect handshake traffic on every reconnect (not just reusableSocket peers); reusableSocket optimization is non-functional end-to-end.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides. JS connect.js:177 gates the route-cache read on c.reusableSocket (`c.reusableSocket ? routes.get(...) : null`), :405 advertises `reusableSocket: c.reusableSocket` in the udx payload, :473 gates routes.add on `c.reusableSocket && payload.udx.reusableSocket` (local flag AND server echo). C++ working tree: peer_connect.cpp builds `payload.udx = UdxInfo{1, false, our_udx_id, 0}` in both peer_handshake variants (~336, ~431) — reusable hardcoded false despite the codec supporting the field. connect.cpp complete() (136-142) calls add_route whenever err==0 && result.udx_socket!=nullptr with no flag or echo gate; the route shortcut (473-478) calls get_route unconditionally (only gated on socket_pool_ non-null) and fires a PEER_HANDSHAKE to the cached address before findPeer. ConnectOptions::reusable_socket exists in dht.hpp:205 but is never read on the client connect path (grep: no 'reusable' in connect.cpp/dht.cpp; only server-side plumbing uses it). Divergence is real and observable both ways: flag-off C++ clients cache+retry stale routes JS never would (extra handshake latency), and flag-on clients never advertise the feature so it can never engage. MED not HIGH: shortcut failure falls through to findPeer, so no connection failure or crash — cost is latency and unrequested caching behavior.

confirmed.title: Route-shortcut handshake omits our firewall + addresses (JS builds one payload for all relays)
confirmed.divergence: JS computes the noisePayload once in connectThroughNode under `if (!c.requesting)` (387-411) — including `firewall` (OPEN if remoteAddress() is set) and addresses4 (public + LAN) — and reuses that same c.request for every relay attempt, including the route path. C++ fire_handshake recomputes firewall+addresses4 (188-206), but the route-shortcut call passes `peer_connect::FIREWALL_UNKNOWN, {}` (484-486) — no firewall, no addresses.
confirmed.scenario: An OPEN/public client reconnects and the route shortcut is taken (which C++ does ungated, see connect-3). C++ tells the server firewall=UNKNOWN and advertises no address, so the server cannot send data to us directly and cannot fast-path — it must fall through to holepunch. JS advertises OPEN + public address on the route path too, enabling the direct fast path.
confirmed.consequence: Route-shortcut reconnects for public clients are degraded to holepunch instead of a direct connect.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: connectAndHolepunch (connect.js:176-184) takes retryRoute first; retryRoute (155-174) calls connectThroughNode(c, route.address, ...), and connectThroughNode (386-411) builds the noise payload on first call with firewall = remoteAddress() ? OPEN : UNKNOWN, addresses4 = public addr + LAN addrs, and relayThrough — the identical payload reused for all subsequent relay attempts. C++ side verified: fire_handshake (src/connect.cpp:188-206) correctly computes our_fw (FIREWALL_OPEN when nat_sampler host/port set, not firewalled, port matches) and our_addrs (public + validated LAN), but the route-shortcut call (src/connect.cpp:484-487) hardcodes peer_connect::FIREWALL_UNKNOWN, {} (empty addresses) and std::nullopt (no relayThrough). Divergence is real and observable: an OPEN client reconnecting via the cached route tells the server UNKNOWN/no-addresses, so the server cannot direct-connect to the advertised address or take the LAN shortcut and must fall through to holepunch; JS enables the direct fast path on the same reconnect. Severity MED rather than HIGH: connections still generally succeed via holepunch (slower reconnect, lost LAN path), and only the reusable-route reconnect path is affected; on failure C++ falls back to findPeer with the correct payload. Bonus: the same call also drops relayThrough, which JS includes.

confirmed.title: Direct-connect path lacks bogon filtering and serverAddress fallback
confirmed.divergence: JS getFirstRemoteAddress (196-203) returns the first NON-bogon address in addresses4 and falls back to serverAddress (the relay's fresh observation, hs.peerAddress||to) when none is usable; it drives the `payload.firewall===OPEN || (relayed && !remoteHolepunchable)` direct path (212-221). C++ try_direct_connect uses addresses4[0] with no isBogon skip (holepunch.cpp:245-247), and the no-holepunch branch uses addresses4[0] with no bogon skip and NO serverAddress fallback — empty addresses4 goes straight to complete(NO_ADDRESSES) (connect.cpp:648-649).
confirmed.scenario: Server is relayed and not holepunchable (no holepunch relays) and advertises empty addresses4 (behind NAT, localConnection off / no LAN addrs). JS falls back to serverAddress and attempts a direct connect to the relay-observed address. C++ fails immediately with NO_ADDRESSES. Secondary case: server advertises a private/bogon address first followed by a public one — JS skips to the public one, C++ dials the bogon.
confirmed.consequence: C++ fails (or dials an unroutable address) on peers that JS connects to via the serverAddress/first-non-bogon fallback.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS connect.js:196-203 getFirstRemoteAddress skips isBogon hosts and falls back to serverAddress (always set on the relayed path via router, line 132/447); lines 212-221 use it for the FIREWALL.OPEN || (relayed && !remoteHolepunchable) direct path, so empty/bogon-only addresses4 still connects to the relay-observed serverAddress. C++ src/holepunch.cpp:239-254 try_direct_connect takes addresses4[0] with no bogon check and returns false on empty; src/connect.cpp:629-651 no-holepunch branch takes addresses4[0] with no bogon skip and completes with NO_ADDRESSES when addresses4 is empty — hs.server_address is in scope but only used later on the holepunch path (hp_peer). grep confirms no bogon filter exists anywhere in src/ or include/ (only a comment in rpc.hpp:70). connect.cpp is not among the uncommitted modified files and holepunch.cpp's uncommitted changes don't touch try_direct_connect, so the tree does not fix this. Divergence is observable (hard failure vs successful connect; dialing a private address vs the public one). Downgraded to MED because the triggering configurations — a relayed server with zero holepunch relays, or a bogon-first addresses4 list — are off the mainline connect path, though real on LAN/CGNAT setups.

confirmed.title: LAN same-NAT shortcut runs in PARALLEL with holepunch (JS is exclusive) and uses a different trigger
confirmed.divergence: JS holepunch() (234-251): when `c.lan && relayed && clientAddress.host===serverAddress.host` and non-reserved server addresses exist, it commits EXCLUSIVELY to the LAN path — ping, on success onsocket()+return, on failure maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket(HOLEPUNCH_ABORTED)+return; the holepunch engine is never started. C++ (697-766) runs the LAN ping AND holepunch_connect concurrently (explicit comment 762-764 "we do NOT return here"), and on LAN ping failure just `return`s with no abort (741-744). The trigger also differs: JS keys on clientAddress.host (res.to) == serverAddress.host and requires `relayed = !!hs.peerAddress`; C++ keys on nat_sampler().host()==addresses4[0].host (check A) plus a subnet match (check B), with `relayed = addresses4[0]!=relay_addr` (707).
confirmed.scenario: Two peers behind the same NAT. JS establishes a pure LAN connection and emits no public holepunch traffic. C++ additionally fires holepunch probes onto the public internet in parallel; whichever finishes first wins, so C++ may bring the connection up over the public/holepunched path instead of the LAN path and always spends the extra probe traffic.
confirmed.consequence: Extra public NAT-probe traffic in the LAN case and non-deterministic LAN-vs-public path selection vs JS's LAN-exclusive behavior.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS connect.js 233-251: exclusive LAN commit — `if (c.lan && relayed && clientAddress.host === serverAddress.host)` then `await ping`; success → onsocket+return, catch → maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket(HOLEPUNCH_ABORTED)+return; Holepuncher never started. C++ connect.cpp ~697-766: LAN ping runs concurrently with holepunch_connect (explicit "we do NOT return here" comment), ping failure falls through with no abort; triggers widened (nat_sampler host match + subnet-match check B; relayed = addresses4[0] != relay_addr). Divergence is real and observable (same-NAT connects emit public holepunch probes; punched path can win the race; C++ survives LAN ping failure where JS aborts). HOWEVER, JS line 233 carries the TODO "would be better to just try local addrs in the background whilst continuing with other strategies..." — the C++ code deliberately implements that TODO. Behavior is a strict superset of JS (never loses a connection JS would make; recovers where JS destroys). Downgraded to LOW: intentional, benign-to-better deviation, not a correctness bug; cost is minor extra probe traffic on same-NAT connects.

confirmed.title: opts.holepunch client veto callback never invoked
confirmed.divergence: JS calls `opts.holepunch(remoteFirewall, nat.firewall, remoteAddresses, nat.addresses)` after the probe round and aborts with HOLEPUNCH_ABORTED('Client aborted holepunch') if it returns false (296-307). C++ declares ConnectOptions::holepunch_veto (dht.hpp:210-213) but it is dead — grep shows it is never read, and holepunch_connect (holepunch.hpp:453-463) takes no veto parameter, so on_handshake_success invokes holepunch with no veto hook.
confirmed.scenario: Caller supplies a holepunch veto (e.g. to refuse RANDOM-NAT punches). JS honors it and aborts; C++ ignores it and proceeds to punch.
confirmed.consequence: Client-side holepunch policy control present in JS is silently missing in C++.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: read .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/connect.js around lines 296-307 — after the probe round and the optional serverAddress rewrite, JS runs `if (opts.holepunch && !opts.holepunch(c.puncher.remoteFirewall, c.puncher.nat.firewall, c.puncher.remoteAddresses, c.puncher.nat.addresses))` and aborts with HOLEPUNCH_ABORTED('Client aborted holepunch') before roundPunch. C++ side: include/hyperdht/dht.hpp:208-213 declares ConnectOptions::holepunch_veto with the matching signature, but `grep -rl holepunch_veto` across the whole working tree hits ONLY dht.hpp — no .cpp reads it. connect.cpp:355-371 copies fast_open, local_connection, relay_keep_alive, relay_through, relay_token from opts into ConnState but never the veto; the holepunch call at connect.cpp:769 (`holepunch::holepunch_connect(*state->socket, hs, hp_relay, hp_peer, hp_info.id, fw, addrs, ...)`) matches the holepunch.hpp:453-463 signature, which has no veto parameter, and src/holepunch.cpp contains no veto logic either. The veto that IS implemented (server.cpp:1065-1070 via Server::set_holepunch) is the server-side opts.holepunch (server.js:544), a different hook — it does not cover the client connect path. So a caller supplying ConnectOptions::holepunch_veto (e.g. to refuse RANDOM-NAT punches) is silently ignored in C++ while JS honors it: real, observable divergence, not fixed in tree. Severity MED, not HIGH: it only manifests when a caller opts in by setting the callback; the default connect path behaves identically to JS. It is still a silent public-API contract violation (dead declared option), so not LOW.

confirmed.title: Handshake reply under-validated: no mode===REPLY, no from-address match, no missing-udx rejection; retries on server error
confirmed.divergence: JS peerHandshake throws BAD_HANDSHAKE_REPLY unless `hs.mode===REPLY && to.host===res.from.host && to.port===res.from.port && hs.noise` (router.js:63-71); connectThroughNode then rejects with SERVER_INCOMPATIBLE if payload.version!==1, SERVER_ERROR if payload.error!==NONE, and SERVER_ERROR if `!payload.udx` (connect.js:425-436), all terminal via maybeDestroy. C++ only checks that resp.value and hs_resp.noise are non-empty and that noise_ik completes; success is just `remote_payload.error==ERROR_NONE` (peer_connect.cpp:389/474). It never checks mode, never checks the reply source, and never rejects a reply lacking udx. And because a decrypted error payload only sets success=false, C++ try_next_relay() retries other relays where JS treats a decoded SERVER_ERROR as terminal.
confirmed.scenario: Server replies error=NONE but omits the udx block. C++ proceeds with remote_udx_id left at 0 → the subsequent UDX connect targets stream id 0 and fails; JS fails cleanly at SERVER_ERROR. A stray/off-path reply with a non-REPLY mode but decryptable noise is also accepted by C++.
confirmed.consequence: Malformed/incompatible server replies produce a broken UDX connection instead of a clean, correctly-coded failure; weaker reply authentication than JS.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Verified both sides line-by-line. JS: router.js:63-71 rejects unless mode===REPLY && to.host/port===res.from.host/port && hs.noise; connect.js:425-436 makes version!==1 (SERVER_INCOMPATIBLE), error!==NONE (SERVER_ERROR), and !payload.udx (SERVER_ERROR) terminal via maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket (connect.js:718-725, terminal at handshake time since rawStream/relaySocket/puncher are unset); only thrown per-relay errors are swallowed (connect.js:361 .then(signal,signal)).
