C++: peer_connect.cpp:366-389 (and 455-474 overload) checks only non-empty value/noise + noise completion — hs_resp.mode is never read, no from-address comparison (rpc.cpp:740 matches inflight by tid only), no version check, no udx check; success=(error==ERROR_NONE). connect.cpp:238-240 routes !hs.success to try_next_relay, ending in generic PEER_CONNECTION_FAILED (connect.cpp:257-264), so a server-signaled error (reachable with a real JS server replying TRY_LATER) causes C++ to retry all relays then misclassify, where JS fails fast and terminally. A udx-less error=NONE payload yields success=true with remote_udx_id left 0 (connect.cpp:449-451) and a doomed UDX connect, vs JS clean SERVER_ERROR. Divergence is real and not fixed in the working tree. Severity MED not HIGH: Noise IK authentication means the missing mode/from checks are defense-in-depth (off-path replies cannot produce a decryptable noise blob), the udx-omission requires a nonconforming server, and the one path reachable with stock JS peers (server error → retry vs terminal) affects only failure latency and error classification, not success cases or security.

confirmed.title: findPeer query not seeded with closestNodes / onlyClosestNodes / retries
confirmed.divergence: JS findAndConnect seeds findPeer with closestNodes (opts.relayAddresses → _relayAddressesCache → persistent router route), sets onlyClosestNodes when a set is present, uses retries=1 for a seeded query vs 3 otherwise, and runs up to `tries=2` query passes (318-348). C++ find_peer (dht_ops.hpp:31-39) accepts none of these; start_find_peer runs a single unrestricted query and instead fires direct PEER_HANDSHAKEs at cached relays (connect.cpp:504-518). There is also no equivalent of the persistent `_router.get(target).relay` shortcut.
confirmed.scenario: Reconnect with cached relays. JS issues a targeted, low-retry query restricted to those nodes; C++ issues a full unrestricted query plus out-of-band direct handshakes. Different node set queried and different retry aggressiveness.
confirmed.consequence: Different query fan-out/retry behavior and wire traffic vs JS on seeded reconnects.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS connect.js:318-348 verified: findAndConnect seeds findPeer with closestNodes (opts.relayAddresses -> _relayAddressesCache -> persistent _router.get(target).relay), sets onlyClosestNodes when seeded, retries=1 seeded vs 3 unseeded, and runs tries=2 passes (closestNodes nulled after pass 1, line 364). dht-rpc query.js:28-29,47,69,141 confirms these are real query options (onlyClosestNodes suppresses frontier expansion at :141). C++ verified: dht_ops.cpp:45-60 find_peer takes no such options; query.cpp:159-160 explicitly documents per-query retries as an unimplemented follow-up; query.hpp has add_seed_node (closestNodes plumbing exists) but no only_closest_nodes mode and find_peer never uses it. connect.cpp:504-522 on cache hit fires direct PEER_HANDSHAKEs at cached relays AND always runs an unrestricted start_find_peer (:293-334); the §AUDIT-6 route shortcut (:472-500) is the JS retryRoute (connect.js:177-183) equivalent, not the persistent _router.get(target) seed. Divergence is real: different node set queried, different messages (direct handshake vs restricted FIND_PEER), different retry policy on reconnect. Severity lowered to LOW: no failure scenario — C++ still reconnects (direct handshake at cached relay is at least as fast), the cost is a redundant full query walk and non-matching wire traffic, not broken behavior. Partial mitigations already in tree (relay cache §AUDIT-3, route shortcut §AUDIT-6, add_seed_node) but the seeded/restricted/low-retry query itself is not implemented.

confirmed.title: Relay pairing 15s timeout is a no-op (no teardown / no abort)
confirmed.divergence: JS c.relayTimeout=setTimeout(onabort,15000) (765); onabort destroys the relaySocket, nulls relaySocket/relayToken, and calls maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket(RELAY_ABORTED) (787-794). C++ starts an equivalent 15s timer but its callback only logs and increments relay_stats().aborts (825-832) — it neither tears down the relay resources nor calls state->complete(), so a stalled relay pairing is never actively aborted.
confirmed.scenario: Relay node accepts the connection but pairing never completes and no other path is outstanding (e.g. holepunch already abandoned). JS destroys the encrypted socket with RELAY_ABORTED at 15s; C++ leaves the relay state alive with no completion.
confirmed.consequence: Latent hang / lingering relay resources when the relay path stalls; currently masked only because other unguarded completes (see connect-2) usually fire first.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS connect.js:765 arms setTimeout(onabort,15000); onabort (787-794) destroys relaySocket, nulls relaySocket/relayToken, and calls maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket(RELAY_ABORTED) — and maybeDestroyEncryptedSocket (718-725) returns early while relaySocket exists, so this 15s abort is JS's only terminator for a stalled relay. C++ src/connect.cpp:825-832 timer callback only DHT_LOGs and increments relay_stats().aborts — verified no teardown and no state->complete(); the pair-error callback (971-976) is equally log-only, complete() (126-147) does not reset state->relay, and the BlindRelayClient's stored pair callbacks capture shared_ptr<ConnState> creating a cycle (ConnState→RelayState→client→callback→ConnState) so a stalled pairing leaks the relay DHT connection with an active SecretStream keepalive indefinitely. Severity capped at MED rather than HIGH because on_handshake_success always runs a parallel terminating path (direct connect, passive 10s HOLEPUNCH_TIMEOUT timer, or holepunch_connect whose callback calls complete on failure), so the user's connect callback still fires — the observable divergence is the never-emitted RELAY_ABORTED (wrong error code/timing vs JS) plus the leaked, keepalive-active relay connection per stalled pairing, not a permanent hang.

confirmed.title: Blind-relay client id uses the relay node's pubkey instead of our own
confirmed.divergence: JS creates the relay client as `relay.Client.from(c.relaySocket, { id: c.relaySocket.publicKey })` — relaySocket.publicKey is our LOCAL static noise key on that relay connection. C++ builds the mux channel id from `relay_result.remote_public_key` (the relay node's key) (900-904).
confirmed.scenario: Any blind-relay pairing where the relay or the peer keys off the client id carried in the pairing/channel handshake.
confirmed.consequence: Potential blind-relay pairing/identity mismatch. Consequence is unverified — relay-connection.js is absent in this JS snapshot and blind_relay.cpp channel-id semantics were not fully traced, so the observable impact is uncertain.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: connect.js:762-764 does `c.relaySocket = c.dht.connect(publicKey)` then `relay.Client.from(c.relaySocket, { id: c.relaySocket.publicKey })`. connect.js:41 shows the encrypted socket is created with `publicKey: keyPair.publicKey` and secret-stream/index.js:27,192 confirm `.publicKey` is the LOCAL static key of the noise handshake — so the JS blind-relay channel id is OUR OWN key, not the relay's. Same pattern on the JS server side (server.js:643-645). C++ side verified: src/connect.cpp:900-904 builds `channel_id` from `relay_result.remote_public_key`, which is the handshake result of connecting TO the relay (set from hs.remote_public_key, e.g. connect.cpp:555/616) — i.e. the RELAY node's static key. The identical bug also exists on the server relay path at src/server.cpp:752-756 (not cited by the finder). This is observable, not cosmetic: protomux matches channels strictly by (protocol, id) — .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:608 `this._get(protocol, id)` on incoming open, with the only fallback being a registered null-id pair notify (line 684). blind-relay's Server (index.js:48-90) creates its session channel via `server.accept(stream, { id })` with a fixed id chosen before the client's open arrives and registers no null-id notify; the only id a relay daemon can know is `stream.remotePublicKey` (= JS client's local key), which matches JS clients but NOT the C++ client's id (= relay's own key). Result: against a stock JS blind-relay node, the C++ open message carries a non-matching id, info.outgoing is empty, _requestSession finds no notify, and the session is rejected — the entire blind-relay fallback fails (15s relayTimeout abort). Severity HIGH: complete failure of the relay fallback path against JS relay infrastructure. Not fixed in the current working tree (both connect.cpp and server.cpp still use remote_public_key). Fix: use our local static key on the relay connection (the keypair used for the relay handshake) as the channel id in both files.

confirmed.title: Firewall-rejected handshake sends an ERROR_ABORTED Noise reply; JS sends nothing (presence leak + false parity comment)
confirmed.divergence: JS _addHandshake awaits the firewall (server.js:251); if it rejects (hs.firewalled true) it calls _clearLater and `return null` at 258-261 BEFORE any handshake.send — so no Noise msg2 is ever produced and router.js onpeerhandshake (line 99 `if (!reply || !reply.noise) return`) sends zero packets. C++ finalize_handshake maps firewall_rejected to has_error + ERROR_ABORTED (server_connection.cpp:164-168), builds a full Noise msg2, and on_handshake_result unconditionally sends it (server.cpp:596) before the `if (conn.has_error) return` at 598. The comment at server_connection.cpp:143-144 claims this 'encodes the same outcome as the sync callback returning true ... the client sees a clean refusal instead of a hang' — that is a false parity claim: JS produces a hang (silence), never a clean ABORTED refusal.
confirmed.scenario: A server with a firewall/allowlist callback that rejects public key K. A blocked peer sends PEER_HANDSHAKE. Against a JS server the peer receives NOTHING and its connect() times out with no evidence the server exists. Against this C++ server the peer receives a completed Noise handshake carrying ERROR_ABORTED — revealing the server's presence and completing key agreement with a peer it means to block, and making connect() fail immediately instead of by timeout.
confirmed.consequence: Firewall/allowlist feature leaks server existence and completes a Noise handshake with rejected peers; observable wire + timing difference for every rejected connection.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS server.js _addHandshake: `if (hs.firewalled) { this._clearLater(hs, id, k); return null }` runs before the error computation and before any handshake.send; router.js onpeerhandshake then hits `if (!reply || !reply.noise) return` — no packet. JS's ERROR.ABORTED/VERSION_MISMATCH replies are only for peers that passed the firewall. C++ src/server_connection.cpp:164-168 maps firewall_rejected to has_error+ERROR_ABORTED but Step 8 still builds/encrypts Noise msg2 and returns a valid ServerConnection; src/server.cpp on_handshake_result calls reply_fn(reply_noise) unconditionally before the `if (conn.has_error) return`, on both the sync and async firewall paths. So a blocked peer gets a completed Noise handshake with ERROR_ABORTED (presence leak + key agreement) vs JS silence. The parity comment at server_connection.cpp:143-147 is false. Downgraded HIGH→MED: only servers with a custom (non-default) firewall are affected, allowed-peer behavior is unchanged, and the fix is a trivial early-return before the send while keeping replies for the version/udx error paths JS does answer.

confirmed.title: Holepunch reply committed (ERROR_NONE, punching) before the veto / punch-start decision — server cannot signal ABORTED
confirmed.divergence: JS builds and returns the PEER_HOLEPUNCH reply only at the very end (586-599), AFTER the holepunch veto (`if (!this.holepunch(...)) return _abort(h)`, 544-546) and after `const punching = await p.punch(); if (!punching) return _abort(h)` (576-578). Both failure paths return _abort, which encrypts an ERROR.ABORTED payload instead of ERROR.NONE. C++ sends the reply (`reply_fn(std::move(reply.value))`) at server.cpp:1028 — carrying error=ERROR_NONE, punching=true — BEFORE the veto (1066-1074, which just clear_session()s and returns with no further reply) and before `conn.puncher->punch()` at 1196 whose bool return (false for unpunchable / RANDOM+CONSISTENT-with-no-pool / puncher-level throttle) is ignored.
confirmed.scenario: (a) A server with set_holepunch() returning false for this punch: JS replies ERROR.ABORTED; C++ has already told the client 'ERROR.NONE, I am punching' then silently clear_session()s — client waits for a punch that never comes. (b) Both peers behind symmetric NAT (RANDOM+RANDOM) so Holepuncher::punch() returns false: JS replies ABORTED so the client can fall back / give up; C++ replies ERROR.NONE, punch() returns false and is ignored, session lingers to the 45s backstop.
confirmed.consequence: On veto or unpunchable NAT the client receives a positive 'punching' signal and hangs instead of getting a clean ABORTED that lets it fall back to relay / fail fast.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified in .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/server.js _onpeerholepunch: the holepunch veto (`if (!this.holepunch(...)) return this._abort(h)`) and `const punching = await p.punch(); if (!punching) return this._abort(h)` both execute before the single ERROR.NONE reply built at the function's end; _abort encrypts ERROR.ABORTED. C++ side verified: src/server_connection.cpp:414-421 encodes error=ERROR_NONE, punching=should_punch (true when client punching) into reply.value; src/server.cpp:1027-1029 sends it via reply_fn BEFORE the veto (~1066-1074, which only clear_session()s and returns — no ABORTED path exists) and before `conn.puncher->punch();` (~1196) whose bool return is discarded. src/holepunch.cpp:322-387 confirms punch() returns false for RANDOM+RANDOM, missing pool/verified-remote, and puncher throttle — all silent in C++. Only the DHT-level random throttle matches JS (TRY_LATER swapped in inside handle_holepunch before send). Divergence is real and observable: JS client gets ABORTED and fails fast; C++ client is told "NONE, punching" and stalls to its own timeout while the server session lingers to the 45s backstop. Severity MED: no crash/security impact, but a genuine protocol-signaling divergence that delays client failure/fallback in the veto and unpunchable-NAT cases.

confirmed.title: Handshake dedup is not synchronous on the async-firewall path — duplicate noise creates multiple sessions/replies
confirmed.divergence: JS _onpeerhandshake stores the in-flight Promise into _connects synchronously (467-473) — before _addHandshake's first `await handshake.recv` — with the explicit comment 'The next couple of statements MUST run within the same tick to prevent a malicious peer from flooding us with handshakes.' C++ only writes handshake_dedup_[noise_key] in on_handshake_result (server.cpp:879), which on the async-firewall path runs inside the user completion callback (dispatched at 522-547, function returns at 547 with no dedup entry set). During the async firewall window the dedup lookup at 407-419 misses, so each duplicate allocates a new hp_id, re-decodes Noise, and invokes the firewall callback again.
confirmed.scenario: Server configured with set_firewall_async (e.g. DB/remote allowlist). Client (or attacker) sends the same Noise msg1 bytes N times while the first firewall check is still pending. JS runs one firewall check and returns one reply for all N; C++ runs N firewall checks, builds N ServerConnections/rawStreams, and sends N different Noise msg2 replies to the client.
confirmed.consequence: Async-firewall servers are floodable and emit duplicate handshake replies for one client; contradicts the dedup invariant the JS comment marks as security-critical.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/server.js _onpeerhandshake stores the in-flight Promise into _connects synchronously ("MUST run within the same tick" comment) before _addHandshake's first await, so N duplicate noise messages share one firewall check and one reply. C++ side verified: src/server.cpp's only insert into handshake_dedup_ is line 879 inside on_handshake_result (grep confirmed; all other references are find/erase/clear). On the async-firewall path (server.cpp:493-547) on_peer_handshake returns at 547 without writing a dedup entry, so while firewall_async_ is pending the dedup lookup at 407-419 misses: each duplicate allocates a new hp_id (421), re-decodes Noise (484), re-invokes firewall_async_ (522), and each completion creates its own ServerConnection under a distinct hp_id and sends a distinct Noise msg2 reply. The sync path is safe (finalize + on_handshake_result run before return). Divergence is real and observable — N firewall checks / N sessions / N replies vs JS's 1 — and triggers benignly whenever a client handshakes via multiple relays against a set_firewall_async server, plus it is the flood vector the JS comment explicitly guards. Severity MED (not HIGH): confined to servers using set_firewall_async; typically degrades (resource waste, orphan sessions until 10s timers, DoS amplification, conflicting replies) rather than deterministically breaking every connection since the client consumes the first msg2 it receives.

confirmed.title: MAX_PENDING_HANDSHAKES=256 cap silently drops handshakes with no JS equivalent
confirmed.divergence: JS keeps pending handshakes in the unbounded `_holepunches` array with no cap on concurrent count. C++ on_handshake_result rejects when `connections_.size() >= 256` by `return`ing before `reply_fn` (server.cpp:596), so no Noise reply is sent and the client cannot complete.
confirmed.scenario: A popular topic / CGNAT reconnect storm drives >256 concurrent pending sessions (each punch session can live up to the 45s punch_clear_wait). A JS server keeps accepting and replying to new handshakes; this C++ server silently drops every new handshake for the duration, so legitimate clients that a JS server would serve get no reply and time out.
confirmed.consequence: Under load the C++ server refuses connections a JS server would accept, invisibly (no error to the client).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS server.js:211-212/235: _addHandshake reuses a null slot or pushes onto the unbounded _holepunches array; grep of the whole file shows no cap of any kind — JS replies to every handshake. C++ src/server.cpp:580-583: on_handshake_result returns when connections_.size() >= 256 BEFORE reply_fn at line 596, so the client gets no Noise reply — silent drop, exactly as the finder claimed. Verified connections_ holds only PENDING punch sessions (inserted server.cpp:878, erased on connect at 1329, on stream close via clear_session:1289, and on timer at 1121), so established connections don't count toward the cap — that rules out HIGH. But each pending session can live up to punch_clear_wait (45s), so 256 concurrent is reachable at ~5.7 handshakes/sec sustained (popular topic or CGNAT reconnect storm), and during that window JS serves clients this C++ server silently drops. Real, observable divergence — but a deliberate, commented DoS guard (H23) that never fires at normal load, hence MED not HIGH.

confirmed.title: No changeRemote relay→direct upgrade — a relayed connection never migrates to the direct path
confirmed.divergence: JS hs.onsocket checks `if (hs.rawStream.connected)` and, when the stream is already connected via relay, calls `hs.rawStream.changeRemote(socket, udx.id, port, host)` to migrate the live stream onto the direct (punched) address without re-emitting a connection (322-325). C++ on_socket has no changeRemote branch; moreover the relay-success path calls clear_session(hp_id) at server.cpp:825, so when the punch later lands the puncher on_connect (1107-1110) finds no session and returns — the connection stays on the relay forever.
confirmed.scenario: relayThrough (blind-relay) server; the relay pairs first and emits the connection, then holepunch succeeds. JS upgrades the stream to the direct socket (relay traffic stops); C++ keeps the stream pinned to the relay for the whole connection lifetime, permanently consuming relay bandwidth even though a direct path exists.
confirmed.consequence: Relayed connections never become peer-to-peer; relay carries all traffic for the connection's life. (Matches the documented CLAUDE.md changeRemote TODO and this branch's purpose.)
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/server.js:305-341: hs.onsocket has the `if (hs.rawStream.connected) { hs.rawStream.changeRemote(socket, udx.id, port, host) }` branch that migrates a live relayed stream onto the direct punched address. C++ working tree: grep for change_remote/changeRemote/udx_stream_change_remote across src/ and include/ returns nothing; the relay-success path (src/server.cpp ~801-846) detaches raw_stream and calls clear_session(hp_id) (destroying the puncher) before emitting the connection; puncher on_connect (src/server.cpp:1107-1110) returns when connections_.find(session_id) fails, so a later punch is discarded; on_socket (src/server.cpp:1218-1254) has no migration branch, only the fresh-connect emit. Once relayed, a C++ connection stays on the relay forever — genuine observable divergence. Not fixed in tree: the fix/relay-direct-upgrade branch contains only the design doc (commit 7e08839); no implementation exists in the uncommitted changes. HIGH: permanent relay dependence, and project memory ties this gap to a real connected-but-no-data failure in nospoon.

confirmed.title: neverPunch (opts.holepunch === false) is not implemented
confirmed.divergence: JS sets `_neverPunch = opts.holepunch === false` (38) and, when true, skips creating any Holepuncher — `if (ourRemoteAddr || this._neverPunch) { hs.prepunching = setTimeout(onabort, 10000); return hs }` (430-433) — so the server never emits probes but still accepts direct/relay/LAN connections. C++ has no neverPunch flag; on any holepunch round with punching it lazily creates a puncher and starts probing (1093-1196). The only C++ knob is set_holepunch(cb), and a veto-all cb clear_session()s the session (1071) rather than keeping it alive for a direct/relay connection — not equivalent.
confirmed.scenario: A JS deployment that sets `holepunch: false` (never send UDP probes, rely on relay/direct only) has no C++ equivalent; a C++ server will always start probing when a client declares punching.
confirmed.consequence: Cannot replicate a punch-disabled server; C++ always emits holepunch probes.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: server.js:38 sets `_neverPunch = opts.holepunch === false`, and server.js:430-433 skips puncher/payload creation entirely when set — the session waits 10s (prepunching→onabort) for direct/LAN/relay, and onabort (400-411) preserves the rawStream when a relay is engaged (relayToken !== null). Holepunch rounds with no puncher hit server.js:490 → _abort, which with null payload is a no-op (602-606). Also verified opts.holepunch===false makes the veto callback default to ()=>true (line 27), so the flag is not expressible via the callback in JS either. C++ side verified: grep for never_punch across src/ and include/ returns nothing; src/server.cpp on a should_punch round unconditionally creates a Holepuncher (~1094) and calls punch() (~1193) unless holepunch_cb_ vetoes; the veto path (~1066-1073) calls clear_session, which (1270-1290) erases the ServerConnection and its raw_stream — destroying the relay/direct fallback JS keeps alive. So the divergence is real and the proposed workaround (veto-all cb) is genuinely non-equivalent. Severity corrected to LOW: default behavior (punching enabled) matches JS; the gap only affects deployments explicitly setting holepunch:false, and no known consumer (nospoon, rustdesk fork) uses it.

confirmed.title: Relay pairing has no 15s abort timer — stuck relayThrough pairings held to the 45s backstop
confirmed.divergence: JS _relayConnection arms `hs.relayTimeout = setTimeout(onabort, 15000)` (646): if the relay pairing doesn't complete in 15s it aborts, tears down relaySocket, and (if aborted) destroys rawStream. C++ has no dedicated relay timeout; while a relay is engaged the session timer is re-armed to punch_clear_wait = 45s (903-909) and both the punch on_abort and the 10s timeout defer to it (session_relay_engaged).
confirmed.scenario: relayThrough server; the chosen relay node accepts the connect but never completes pairing (data event never fires). JS reclaims the session + rawStream at 15s; C++ holds them ~45s before clear_session.
confirmed.consequence: Stuck relay pairings hold a session and rawStream ~30s longer than JS, inflating resource use on relayThrough servers.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS server.js:646 arms hs.relayTimeout = setTimeout(onabort, 15000) when _relayConnection starts pairing; onabort (676-684) destroys relaySocket and (if aborted) rawStream at 15s, and _clear at 10s (445-462) is bookkeeping-only. C++ has no counterpart: BlindRelayClient::pair (src/blind_relay.cpp:216-247) sends the Pair message with no timer — on_error fires only on unpair/channel close; the server relay path (src/server.cpp:680-860) arms no timer; and the session timer at src/server.cpp:903-909 re-arms to punch_clear_wait=45000 (server.hpp:218) when session_relay_engaged (server.cpp:1262), so a stalled pairing's session/rawStream is reclaimed at ~55s (10s+45s) vs JS's 15s. The RELAY_TIMEOUT_MS=15000 constant (blind_relay.hpp:72) is used only on the client side (connect.cpp:822-832), never in server.cpp — the comment at server.cpp:900 claiming the relay's own bounded timeouts cover this is wrong for the server path. Additionally, clear_session (server.cpp:1269-1289) never destroys the relay-side duplex/mux/client (kept alive by shared_ptr captures in the pending pair request, with keep-alive on), so unlike JS the relay socket is not torn down on the stuck path at all. Severity MED: failure-path-only resource retention (no happy-path or crash impact), but ~4x longer holds plus a missing relay-socket teardown.

confirmed.title: Server-side same-host LAN match (server.js:414-426) not implemented
confirmed.divergence: JS, when `!direct && clientAddress.host === serverAddress.host`, filters the client's private addresses, calls Holepuncher.matchAddress against its own local addresses, and on a match sets only a prepunching timer and returns — skipping puncher creation, expecting a LAN-direct connection (414-426). C++ never receives serverAddress (req.to is not passed to on_peer_handshake — router.cpp only forwards client_addr) and has no matchAddress branch, so it always proceeds to arm the session and (on a punch round) build a puncher.
confirmed.scenario: Two peers behind the same NAT/LAN. Both still connect (the client-side LAN shortcut in connect.js hits the server's advertised LAN address, tripping the rawStream firewall), but the C++ server needlessly spins up a Holepuncher and emits probes JS would have avoided.
confirmed.consequence: Redundant puncher + probe traffic for same-LAN peers; functionally connects but less efficient.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS server.js:414-426 verified: same-host check (clientAddress.host === serverAddress.host), onlyPrivateHosts filter, Holepuncher.matchAddress, and on match sets only a prepunching timer and returns before puncher creation (line 436). C++ verified: router.cpp:100-111 forwards only client_addr (req.to never reaches the server handler), and server.cpp on_peer_handshake/on_handshake_result (391-917) has no same-host/match_address branch — grep confirms match_address is only called client-side (connect.cpp:720,728). So the branch is genuinely unimplemented. HOWEVER the finder's failure scenario is overstated: C++ creates the Holepuncher lazily on the first punch round (server.cpp:1093-1094, comment at 928), and a JS client on the LAN shortcut returns at connect.js:248-249 without ever sending a punch round — so against JS clients the C++ server creates no puncher and emits zero probes; behavior is observably identical. The real divergence only manifests when a punch round arrives for a same-host session (the C++ client always sends one because its LAN ping and holepunch deliberately race in parallel, connect.cpp:762-765): JS would have no puncher and abort at server.js:489-490, while C++ builds one and punches (server.cpp:1093-1196). Different wire behavior for identical inputs, hence CONFIRMED — but no traced path where a JS-successful connection fails in C++ (C++ is more resilient when the LAN ping fails, e.g. AP client isolation), so severity is LOW: extra punch traffic / different abort semantics in a same-LAN corner, partly a deliberate C++ design choice.

confirmed.title: OPEN-client shortcut targets self-reported addresses4[0] with a null socket, vs JS clientAddress + dht.socket
confirmed.divergence: JS OPEN-path onsocket uses the relay-observed `clientAddress` (peerAddress||req.from) as target and `this.dht.socket` as the socket (391-393). C++ requires `!remote_payload.addresses4.empty()` and connects to `addresses4[0]` — the client's self-reported address — passing udx_sock=nullptr into on_socket (861-866), so ConnectionInfo.udx_socket is null and the target is the client's claim rather than the relay's observation.
confirmed.scenario: A FIREWALL_OPEN client that reports no addresses4 (or a self-reported address differing from the relay-observed source, e.g. it advertised a LAN/pre-NAT address): JS still connects to the observed clientAddress via dht.socket; C++ either skips the shortcut entirely (empty addresses4) or targets a possibly-wrong self-reported address and relies on the caller to substitute a socket for the null udx_socket.
confirmed.consequence: OPEN-client fast-connect can target the wrong address or be skipped, and depends on the caller correctly defaulting a null socket.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Address half CONFIRMED, socket half REFUTED. JS (server.js:471) sets clientAddress = peerAddress||req.from (relay-observed) and the OPEN shortcut (server.js:390-394) calls hs.onsocket(this.dht.socket, clientAddress.port, clientAddress.host) — the observed address. C++ (src/server.cpp:861-866, working tree) gates on !remote_payload.addresses4.empty() and targets addresses4[0], the client's SELF-reported address. The relay-observed address IS computed in C++ (src/router.cpp:100, value_or(req.from)) and reaches on_peer_handshake (server.cpp:392), but is not passed to on_handshake_result and ServerConnection (server_connection.hpp:28-76) has no field for it, so the shortcut cannot use it — real structural divergence, not a misread. Observable effect: (a) client whose sampled remoteAddress is stale vs the relay's fresh observation (NAT remap, multi-homed egress) — JS connects to the working observed address, C++ connects to the wrong one; (b) OPEN client with empty addresses4 — JS still connects, C++ silently falls through to the holepunch-wait path an OPEN client never drives. Socket half refuted: on_socket's udx_sock=nullptr default (server.hpp:337) is the documented "use server socket" convention — ffi_stream.cpp:60-62 falls back to dht->socket().socket_handle(), which is exactly JS's this.dht.socket, so no observable difference there. Severity MED: common case matches (connect.js:391-396 shows a compliant OPEN client always populates addresses4[0] with its usually-correct remoteAddress); divergence only bites on stale-sample/non-compliant edge cases, but when it bites it kills an otherwise-valid direct connection.

confirmed.title: RANDOM+CONSISTENT birthday-paradox punch is non-functional (no pool wired; probes egress from the shared pool socket, not per-holder)
confirmed.divergence: JS `_openBirthdaySockets` acquires up to 256 fresh sockets and calls `holepunch(ref.socket, remoteAddr, HOLEPUNCH_TTL)` from EACH new socket, opening 256 distinct NAT holes (the whole point of the birthday paradox); `_keepAliveRandomNat` then cycles `this._allHolders[i++].socket`. In C++ the Holepuncher is constructed with pool_=nullptr in BOTH callers (holepunch.cpp:1361 `Holepuncher(loop, true)`, server.cpp:1094 `Holepuncher(loop, false)`), so punch()'s RANDOM+CONSISTENT branch hits `if (!vr || !pool_) return false` (holepunch.cpp:369) and never runs. Even if a pool were supplied, open_birthday_sockets (677-679) and keep_alive_random_nat (725-728) send via `send_ttl_fn_`, which holepunch_connect binds to the SINGLE shared pool socket (holepunch.cpp:1366-1368) — never from holders_[i]->socket() — so only one NAT hole is ever opened.
confirmed.scenario: Symmetric/CGNAT client (firewall RANDOM) connecting to a CONSISTENT server (or vice-versa on the server side): JS opens 256 birthday sockets and lands the punch; C++ punch() returns false and the connection stalls/aborts.
confirmed.consequence: Every RANDOM+CONSISTENT connection that JS can holepunch fails in C++ — exactly the CGNAT case the project targets.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: holepuncher.js:203-209 routes RANDOM+CONSISTENT to _openBirthdaySockets (271-276), which acquires up to 256 pool sockets and sends holepunch(ref.socket, remoteAddr, HOLEPUNCH_TTL) from EACH — 256 distinct source ports/NAT holes; _keepAliveRandomNat (258-264) cycles _allHolders[i++].socket. C++ side: holepunch.hpp:267-269 defaults pool=nullptr; both constructors pass no pool (holepunch.cpp:1361 client, server.cpp:1094 server), so punch()'s RANDOM+CONSISTENT branch (holepunch.cpp:366-369) hits `if (!vr || !pool_) return false` and never runs; the false return is unchecked at holepunch.cpp:1772 and server.cpp:1196 so the punch silently does nothing. Even if a pool were supplied, open_birthday_sockets (677-679) and keep_alive_random_nat (723-729) send via send_ttl_fn_, which holepunch_connect binds (1366-1368) to the single shared PoolSocket — acquired holders only get a receive handler (669-673), never transmit — so only one NAT hole would open. The server additionally never sets send_ttl_fn at all. Divergence is real and observable: JS lands direct connections for symmetric/CGNAT-vs-consistent peers; C++ deterministically cannot. Whole strategy (1 of 4 firewall combos) is non-functional; HIGH stands. No fix in the uncommitted working-tree changes (they touch tick/query/relay-upgrade code).

confirmed.title: Random-punch throttle: client neither serializes nor waits; server TRY_LATER is treated as fatal abort instead of retry-after-delay
confirmed.divergence: JS roundPunch, when either side is RANDOM, enters a WAIT loop: while `_randomPunches >= _randomPunchLimit || now-_lastRandomPunch < _randomPunchInterval` it sends a punching=false round then `await tryLater` (sleeps 10-20s) and retries until the throttle clears; a TRY_LATER reply likewise triggers `await tryLater(c); return roundPunch(...true)`. The C++ CLIENT Holepuncher is built with stats_=nullptr (holepunch.cpp:1361), so punch()'s throttle guards (holepunch.cpp:353,372) are skipped entirely — no global random-punch accounting/serialization — and the throttle guards, where they do fire (server puncher), RETURN FALSE (fail) rather than wait. Worse, the client Round-1 and Round-2 response handlers treat ANY server `error != NONE` (including ERROR.TRY_LATER=3) as fatal: `state->complete({})` (holepunch.cpp:1552-1556 and 1746-1749).
confirmed.scenario: Server is random-throttled (it does emit TRY_LATER via server.cpp:1018 -> handle_holepunch). The C++ client receives error=TRY_LATER and aborts; a JS client sleeps 10-20s and reconnects successfully once the server's random-punch slot frees.
confirmed.consequence: Under concurrent random-punch load the C++ client fails connections that JS eventually completes, and the C++ client can also stampede multiple concurrent random punches JS would serialize.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: connect.js:527-533 returns {tryLater:true} for ERROR.TRY_LATER on a punching round instead of throwing; roundPunch (connect.js:637-664) serializes random punches via dht._randomPunches/_lastRandomPunch with a wait loop (tryLater = 10-20s sleep, connect.js:713-715) and connect.js:698-701 retries roundPunch after a tryLater reply. C++: client Holepuncher is constructed at src/holepunch.cpp:1361 with stats defaulted to nullptr (holepunch.hpp:267-269), so the throttle guards at holepunch.cpp:353/372 are skipped — no client-side limit/spacing/serialization — and where guards fire they return false (fail) rather than wait. Both round handlers (holepunch.cpp:1552-1556 and 1746-1749) treat any error != ERROR_NONE, including ERROR_TRY_LATER=3 (peer_connect.hpp:44), as fatal via state->complete({}). The C++ server really emits TRY_LATER when random-throttled (server.cpp:1014-1018 + handle_holepunch), so the scenario is reachable: JS client sleeps and retries within the connect; C++ client aborts the connect. Divergence confirmed; not fixed in the working tree. Severity lowered to MED because it only manifests on RANDOM-firewall punches while a throttle window is active — mainline consistent/open connect paths are unaffected.

confirmed.title: punch() treats UNKNOWN local firewall as CONSISTENT and starts probing; JS refuses (returns false)
confirmed.cpp_ref: holepunch.cpp:336-348; caller gap src/server.cpp:1196 vs server.js:519-525
confirmed.divergence: C++ punch() computes `local_consistent = (local_firewall_ != FIREWALL_RANDOM)` (holepunch.cpp:338), so UNKNOWN(0) is treated as consistent and the CONSISTENT+CONSISTENT branch fires. JS `_punch` requires `local === FIREWALL.CONSISTENT` in every punchable branch; coerceFirewall only maps OPEN->CONSISTENT and leaves UNKNOWN=0, so with local UNKNOWN all branches fail and `_punch` returns false. The header comment at holepunch.cpp:336-337 ("Treat UNKNOWN as CONSISTENT") is contrary to JS. This is reachable because the C++ server calls puncher->punch() (server.cpp:1196) WITHOUT the JS analyze()-stability gate (server.js:519-525) that would otherwise catch UNKNOWN; the client path is gated by proceed()'s analyze so is mostly safe.
confirmed.scenario: Server NAT sampling has not reached a firm classification (firewall UNKNOWN) but the client is holepunching: JS aborts; C++ launches a consistent probe schedule.
confirmed.consequence: Wasted/incorrect probe schedule and misadvertised firewall on servers whose NAT is not yet classified; divergent success/failure vs JS.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: coerceFirewall (holepuncher.js:333-335) maps only OPEN→CONSISTENT, leaving UNKNOWN=0; every punchable branch in _punch (lines 188/196/203) requires local===CONSISTENT or >=RANDOM, so local UNKNOWN → _punch returns false → server.js:576-578 aborts the session (the 519-526 analyze gate is bypassed when remoteHolepunching, so the abort comes from punch()'s false return). UNKNOWN is reachable in JS: nat.js resolves `analyzing` even below minSamples (nat.js:70-76) and leaves firewall UNKNOWN when sampled<3 (nat.js:103). C++: holepunch.cpp:336-338 explicitly comments "Treat UNKNOWN as CONSISTENT" and computes local_consistent = (local_firewall_ != FIREWALL_RANDOM), so UNKNOWN(0) enters the CONSISTENT+CONSISTENT branch (341-348) and starts consistent_probe(), returning true. The server path (server.cpp:988→1100→1196) feeds nat_sampler().firewall() — which stays UNKNOWN below 3 deduped samples (nat_sampler.cpp add()) — straight into punch() with no stability gate; Holepuncher::is_unstable() (holepunch.cpp:540-551) correctly models the JS check but is never invoked on this path. So JS aborts where C++ punches: a genuine observable divergence, reachable on a freshly-started firewalled server. Not fixed in the working tree (the current code contains the contrary comment). Downgraded to MED: consequence is optimistic probing / delayed abort / different wire reply (punching=true vs ERROR.ABORTED) in an under-sampled-NAT edge window, not a crash or security issue.

confirmed.title: analyze() reopen path is a no-op — unstable NAT aborts instead of re-sampling on fresh sockets
confirmed.divergence: JS analyze(true) -> _reopen loops up to MAX_REOPENS(3) calling _reset() (release socket, acquire fresh one, re-run nat.autoSample) until `!_unstable()`, returning whether the final coerced firewall is CONSISTENT. C++ analyze() increments reopen_count_ once and invokes on_reset_ — but holepunch_connect never wires on_reset_ (no `puncher->on_reset(...)` call), so analyze(true) simply returns false and the connection aborts. The code comment at holepunch.cpp:1633-1635 admits the reopen path is unwired.
confirmed.scenario: Client/server whose first NAT sample batch yields UNKNOWN or double-RANDOM-unstable: JS recovers via reopen and connects; C++ aborts.
confirmed.consequence: Connections that JS salvages by re-sampling the NAT are dropped by C++.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS holepuncher.js:85-93 analyze(true) returns _reopen() (lines 152-159), which loops up to MAX_REOPENS=3 calling _reset() (104-116: fresh pool socket + new Nat + autoSample), awaits re-sampling, and returns whether the coerced firewall reached CONSISTENT; both call sites (connect.js:605-608, server.js:522-525) use it to recover and continue. C++ holepunch.cpp:570-605: analyze(true) on the unstable path increments reopen_count_, fires on_reset_ if set, then unconditionally on_done(false). Grep of src/ and include/ shows on_reset_ is NEVER wired — only the setter (holepunch.hpp:340), the member (:380), and the internal invocation exist; no caller sets it. Even if wired, analyze(true) reports false immediately without awaiting re-sampling, so the connect-path caller at holepunch.cpp:1629-1641 aborts (state->complete({})); the comment at 1633-1635 admits the reopen path is unwired. The C++ server never calls analyze at all (server.cpp:955 comment). So JS re-samples on fresh sockets and can recover an UNKNOWN/double-RANDOM-unstable first classification; C++ aborts. Divergence is real and not fixed in tree. Severity MED not HIGH: affects a recovery path for flaky/mobile NATs (JS's own comment: "Mostly relevant for mobile networks"), and C++ gates analyze behind completed pool sampling so transient-UNKNOWN is rarer than in JS; mainline connects on stable NATs are unaffected.

confirmed.title: CONSISTENT+RANDOM branch omits the verified-remote gate; probes remote_addresses_[0] unconditionally
confirmed.divergence: JS `_punch` has `if (!remoteVerifiedAddress) return false` before BOTH random branches and passes the verified address to `_randomProbes(remoteVerifiedAddress)` (so the host of a VERIFIED address is used). C++ punch()'s CONSISTENT+RANDOM branch (holepunch.cpp:350-364) has no verified-remote check, and random_probes() (holepunch.cpp:518-524) always uses `remote_addresses_[0]`, verified or not. (The RANDOM+CONSISTENT branch does gate on verified_remote() at 368, but CONSISTENT+RANDOM does not.)
confirmed.scenario: Remote addresses present but none verified (e.g. token never echoed): JS declines; C++ sprays random-port probes at addr[0]'s host. In current callers addr[0] is always marked verified, so impact is limited.
confirmed.consequence: Divergent behavior (and potentially wrong target host) on unverified remote sets; low in practice because callers verify addr[0].
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/holepuncher.js — `_punch()` builds `remoteVerifiedAddress` from the first addr with `verified` (lines ~181-187), returns true for CONSISTENT+CONSISTENT before the gate, then `if (!remoteVerifiedAddress) return false` (line 194) guards BOTH random branches, and `_randomProbes(remoteVerifiedAddress)` (line ~198, impl 234-244) sprays random ports at the VERIFIED address's host. C++ side verified: src/holepunch.cpp punch() CONSISTENT+RANDOM branch (lines 350-364) has throttle + increment_randomized + random_probes() with no verified check, while the RANDOM+CONSISTENT branch does gate via verified_remote() at line 368 — asymmetry confirming the omission is not a deliberate design. random_probes() (lines 507-530) targets `remote_addresses_[0].addr` unconditionally, not the verified entry. So the code-level divergence is real: with addresses present but none verified, JS returns false and sends nothing; C++ sprays 1750 probes at addr[0]'s host. However, I traced every production caller: src/server.cpp:1195 calls update_remote(valid_addrs, peer_address.host_string()) where valid_addrs[0] IS peer_address, and src/holepunch.cpp:1623-1624 and 1752-1755 both pass addrs[0].host_string() as verified_host — so in the current tree addr[0] is always verified at every production punch() call, and the verified addr is always index 0, making the wrong-host and no-verified scenarios unreachable today (set_remote_addresses, the all-unverified setter, is only used in test/test_holepunch.cpp). Latent divergence, zero observable effect through current callers → severity LOW. Not fixed in tree: the gate is genuinely absent from the CONSISTENT+RANDOM branch and random_probes() still indexes [0].

confirmed.title: SocketRoutes GC missing — cached routes are never removed on socket close or stream error
confirmed.js_ref: socket-pool.js:72-101 (_onconnect gc on 'close', setReusable(false)+gc on stream 'error')
confirmed.divergence: JS SocketRoutes installs a `gc` closure on the socket's 'close' event that deletes the route, and a rawStream 'error' handler that marks the socket non-reusable and gc's the route. C++ SocketPool::add_route (socket_pool.cpp:218-224) only inserts into routes_ and calls set_reusable(true); there is no close/error hook, and remove_route() is never called by live code. get_route (connect.cpp:475) then hands back the stale cached address after the underlying connection has closed or the peer's external address has changed.
confirmed.scenario: Client connects to a peer (route cached), the connection later closes or the peer NAT-remaps, then the client reconnects: JS skips the dead route; C++ wastes one handshake round-trip on the stale address every reconnect.
confirmed.consequence: Extra failed-handshake latency on reconnect after address change, plus unbounded routes_ memory growth on a long-lived node.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS socket-pool.js:79-100: SocketRoutes._onconnect installs gc on socket 'close' (deletes route) and rawStream 'error' handler (setReusable(false) + gc); connect.js:155-173 retryRoute additionally skips routes whose socket ref is gone. C++ socket_pool.cpp:218-224 add_route only inserts + set_reusable(true); no close/error hook exists, and remove_route (socket_pool.cpp:233-236) is called only from test/test_socket_pool.cpp:179, never live. connect.cpp:474-501 uses the stale cached address for a peer_handshake that must time out before falling through to start_find_peer. Divergence is real and observable: C++ wastes one handshake round-trip (RPC retry/timeout) per reconnect to a closed/remapped peer, JS goes straight to findPeer. Corrections: routes_ growth is bounded by distinct-peer count (entries overwritten per pubkey), not unbounded; no UAF since only route->address is read, never route->socket. Fallback works and the route self-heals on successful reconnect, so MED (latency penalty + slow per-peer leak), not HIGH.

confirmed.title: 'connected'/on_connect gated on REMOTE header + local write-ack, not on LOCAL handshake as in JS
confirmed.divergence: In JS with a precomputed handshake (the hyperdht case: opts.handshake={tx,rx,hash,...}), _setupSecretStream sets this._encrypt, so _open takes the `if (this._encrypt !== null) { this._resolveOpened(true); return cb(null) }` branch and fires connected=true + emit('connect') SYNCHRONOUSLY at construction — before our header write completes and before ANY remote data. C++ maybe_fire_connect() requires BOTH header_sent_ (set only in the UDX write ACK of our header frame) AND header_received_ (set only when the remote's header frame is parsed). So on_connect fires a full RTT later.
confirmed.scenario: Initiator creates the encrypted stream. JS emits 'connect' immediately and accepts write() right away (encrypt/Push state is ready; only the decrypt side needs the remote header). C++ ffi_stream write() is gated on is_connected() (src/ffi_stream.cpp:193,202) which returns false until the peer's header arrives — a write issued right after start() returns -1 and the caller's bytes are dropped, and the user's 'open'/on_connect event (ffi_stream.cpp:153-157) is delayed by one round trip.
confirmed.consequence: on_connect/connection-ready fires ~1 RTT late vs JS; early writes are refused (-1/-2) and silently lost if the caller ignores the return, where JS would have buffered/sent them. Observable timing and potential data-loss divergence against a JS peer.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS verified: with a precomputed handshake (hyperdht's only mode — connect.js:470, server.js:670 pass {handshake}), start() → _startHandshake (index.js:195-199) → _setupSecretStream sets _encrypt (line 377) and only QUEUES the header write (line 396); _open (lines 436-439) then hits the `_encrypt !== null` branch and _resolveOpened(true) (lines 510-518) sets connected=true and emits 'connect' with no dependency on write drain or remote data — the constructor's resume/pause wiggle (line 80-82) triggers open immediately. C++ verified: maybe_fire_connect (src/secret_stream.cpp ~734-736) requires header_sent_ (set only in the UDX write-ack callback of send_header_frame) AND header_received_ (set only when the remote 56-byte header is parsed in handle_frame); write() returns -2 when !connected_, and ffi_stream.cpp:193-194/202-203 reject writes with -1 until is_connected(). So on_connect/on_open fires one RTT later than JS and there is a write-rejection window JS doesn't have. Bounded severity: both peers send headers unconditionally over reliable UDX so connect always completes (~1 RTT, no deadlock), and early inbound data is queued in pending_messages_ and replayed — nothing received is lost. Real divergence (latency + write API behavior), not HIGH: no data corruption, no connection failure for callers that wait for on_open. Not addressed by any working-tree change.

confirmed.title: Keepalive/timeout timers are never uv_unref'd, so they hold the event loop open (JS unrefs both)
confirmed.divergence: JS unrefs both timeout-refresh handles so they never keep the libuv loop alive. C++ creates raw uv_timer_t handles and never calls uv_unref on them.
confirmed.scenario: A stream has keep_alive_ms or timeout_ms set (e.g. holepunch/DHT keepalive). All real work is done and the app expects uv_run to drain and return, but the ref'd repeating keepalive/timeout timer keeps the loop alive indefinitely.
confirmed.consequence: Event loop / process fails to exit naturally when only idle secret-stream timers remain, unlike JS where unref'd timers let the loop close.
confirmed.verify_reasoning:
JS side verified: .analysis/js/@hyperswarm/secret-stream/index.js has `this._timeoutTimer.unref()` (line 102, inside setTimeout) and `this._keepAliveTimer.unref()` (line 115, inside setKeepAlive) — both timers are unref'd so they never hold the Node loop. C++ side verified: src/secret_stream.cpp start_timeout_timer (lines 249-256) and start_keepalive_timer (lines 257-263) create uv_timer_t handles and call uv_timer_start with no uv_unref anywhere in the file (only uv_unref in the tree is the gc timer at src/rpc_handlers.cpp:92, confirming the pattern is used elsewhere but not here). So the mechanical divergence is real and not fixed in the working tree.

Severity corrected down to LOW, for two reasons I verified. (1) Both sides clear timers at destroy: JS _destroy (index.js:535-536) calls _clearKeepAlive/_clearTimeout; C++ destroy() (secret_stream.cpp:606-618) and ~SecretStreamDuplex (lines 359-369) call crypto_.stop_timers(), which uv_close's the handles. (2) While a secret-stream is open, its ref'd udx stream/socket handle holds the loop in BOTH implementations (udx-native has no unref anywhere in lib/*.js), so the timer's ref is almost never the sole thing keeping the loop alive — the finding's "all work done, loop expected to drain" scenario mostly requires a live un-destroyed stream, which pins the loop in JS too. The one genuinely divergent window: on natural remote close, on_udx_close (secret_stream.cpp:903-915) sets destroyed_=true and fires on_close WITHOUT stopping timers, and a later destroy() early-returns on destroyed_ — so between remote close and object deletion the C++ keepalive/timeout timers alone hold the loop (and keep firing), where JS would have both cleared them in _destroy and unref'd them. Fix is two uv_unref calls (plus arguably a stop_timers in on_udx_close), but the exposure in real usage — where apps delete the duplex in on_close — is narrow.

confirmed.title: end() does not clear the keepalive timer; JS _final does
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/secret_stream.cpp:578-595 (end() — sets ended_, issues write_end, never touches crypto_.set_keep_alive/stop timers)
confirmed.divergence: JS _final immediately clears the keepalive timer on graceful shutdown. C++ end() leaves the keepalive timer running. The timer keeps firing; its callback calls write() which now returns -1 (ended_), so no frame is sent, but the (non-unref'd) timer keeps waking the loop until the stream fully closes.
confirmed.scenario: Peer or app calls end() to half-close. Between end() and final UDX close, the keepalive timer keeps firing on interval and (per timers-1) holds the loop open, doing wasted work — JS has already torn the keepalive down.
confirmed.consequence: Wasted timer wakeups after graceful end and, combined with timers-1, the loop stays alive longer than in JS.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS index.js:503-508 _final() calls _clearKeepAlive() (destroys timer, lines 527-532). C++ end() at src/secret_stream.cpp:578-595 sets ended_ and issues write_end but never stops the keepalive timer; the timer (repeating, non-unref'd, started at line 262) is only stopped by destroy() (line 610) or the destructor (line 369) — on_udx_close (903-915) does not stop it. Keepalive is enabled on real paths (connect.cpp:859, server.cpp:733, dht.hpp:890, default 5000ms). However, after end() the keepalive callback's write(nullptr,0,nullptr) returns -1 at line 529 due to the ended_ guard, so no frame reaches the wire — protocol behavior is identical to JS. The only observable divergence is the timer continuing to wake the loop every 5s between end() and object destruction (potentially long in a half-close). No leak, no wire divergence, no crash: severity LOW. Fix is a one-liner (stop keepalive timer in end()); not yet present in the working tree.

confirmed.title: enable_send=false disables send_udp/try_send_udp; JS keeps the send path functional (enableSend only gates the receive listener)
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/@hyperswarm/secret-stream/index.js:37 (this.enableSend=opts.enableSend!==false), :434 (_open: if(this.enableSend) rawStream.on('message',...)), :391 (_setupSecretStream unconditionally calls _setupSecretSend), :564-579 (send/trySend only check _sendState)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/secret_stream.cpp:354-357 (setup_secret_send only if opts_.enable_send), :816-817 (send_udp requires send_state_ready_), :452-454 (recv_start only if enable_send)
confirmed.divergence: In JS, _setupSecretSend (which builds _sendState) runs unconditionally, and `enableSend` ONLY controls whether the inbound 'message' listener is installed — so send()/trySend() work even when enableSend=false. In C++, setup_secret_send() is skipped when enable_send=false, leaving send_state_ready_=false, so send_udp()/try_send_udp() return -1.
confirmed.scenario: A caller constructs the duplex with DuplexOptions.enable_send=false (intending 'don't receive unordered messages') and then calls send_udp(). JS would encrypt and send the secretbox datagram; C++ refuses with -1.
confirmed.consequence: Outbound unordered/secretbox messages are impossible in C++ under enable_send=false, diverging from JS where only the receive side is disabled.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: index.js:33 sets enableSend; its ONLY use is index.js:434 gating the inbound 'message' listener in _open. _setupSecretSend runs unconditionally in _onhandshakert (line ~388), so _sendState always exists and send()/trySend() (which check only _sendState and rawStream?.send) work even with enableSend=false. C++ side verified: src/secret_stream.cpp:354-356 gates setup_secret_send() on opts_.enable_send, so send_state_ready_ (set at :422) stays false and send_udp (:817) / try_send_udp (:848) return -1; recv gating at :452-454 matches JS :434. The behavioral divergence is real and observable for an API caller. Severity LOW, not MED/HIGH: no code in either tree ever sets the flag false (grep across src/include/test and .analysis/js — hyperdht never passes enableSend; only the secret-stream README mentions it), default=true on both sides so no hyperdht path is affected, wire format unaffected, and the JS README:67 documents enableSend:false as "disable the send API" — the C++ matches the documented semantics while the JS code diverges from its own docs.

confirmed.title: Inactivity timeout refreshed only on fully-decrypted data frames, not on every inbound raw chunk
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/secret_stream.cpp:183-186 (refresh happens inside decrypt()), :647-656 (process_incoming_bytes never refreshes), :694-708 (header-frame path never calls decrypt)
confirmed.divergence: JS refreshes the timeout on EVERY raw data event (_onrawdata), including the incoming header bytes and partial-frame chunks that don't yet complete a message. C++ refreshes the timeout only inside decrypt(), i.e. only when a complete DATA frame is processed — the header frame and any partial/trickled frame bytes do not refresh it.
confirmed.scenario: With a short timeout, a large data frame arrives split across several UDX reads spanning longer than the timeout, or the remote header is slow: JS keeps resetting the timeout on each chunk, C++ times out and destroys the stream because no full frame completed in the window.
confirmed.consequence: C++ can spuriously time out a stream that is actively receiving (partial frames / header) where JS would not.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: _onrawdata in .analysis/js/@hyperswarm/secret-stream/index.js refreshes _timeoutTimer as its first statement, before the length/body FSM — every raw chunk (header bytes, partial frames) resets the inactivity timeout. C++ side verified: the only receive-side refresh is uv_timer_again(timeout_timer_) at src/secret_stream.cpp:183-186 inside SecretStream::decrypt(); process_incoming_bytes (647-656) and try_extract_frame never touch the timer, and the header frame goes through receive_header (handle_frame 694-708) which bypasses decrypt entirely — secret_stream.hpp:113 even documents "Refreshed on every decrypt()". Refutation attempts failed: uv_timer_again's repeat interval doesn't substitute for per-chunk refresh, and peer keepalive frames (which would refresh via decrypt) queue behind an in-flight trickled frame on the ordered stream, so they can't mask it. Divergence is real and observable: with a timeout set, a frame trickled across chunks spanning > timeout_ms destroys the C++ stream while JS survives. Severity lowered to LOW: timeout_ms defaults to 0 and no in-tree or FFI caller invokes set_timeout (hyperdht JS only sets relay-socket keepalive, not stream timeouts), so shipped behavior cannot hit it — it only affects a future opt-in user under slow-frame-delivery timing. Fix when touched: call uv_timer_again in SecretStreamDuplex::process_incoming_bytes (raw-chunk level), matching JS.

confirmed.title: Bool::decode accepts any non-zero byte as true; JS accepts only exactly 0x01
confirmed.divergence: JS bool.decode returns `state.buffer[state.start++] === 1` — true ONLY for byte value 0x01, false for every other non-zero byte. C++ returns `Uint8::decode(s) != 0` — true for ANY non-zero byte (0x02..0xFF).
confirmed.scenario: A peer (buggy or adversarial) sends byte 0x02 in a bool field. JS decodes it as `false`; C++ decodes it as `true`. Conformant encoders only emit 0/1 so this never triggers on well-formed traffic, and no HyperDHT/dht-rpc message currently routes through compact::Bool (grep finds no callers of Bool::decode outside compact.cpp), so today it is a latent codec-level semantic gap rather than an active wire divergence.
confirmed.consequence: Different decoded boolean value for non-canonical bool bytes; would surface if any future message field is wired through compact::Bool and a peer emits a non-0/1 byte.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/compact-encoding/index.js line 463 (`decode(state) { ... return state.buffer[state.start++] === 1 }` inside `exports.bool`, lines 455-466) — strict equality with 1, so byte 0x02 decodes as false. C++ side verified: src/compact.cpp lines 203-205 — `bool Bool::decode(State& s) { return Uint8::decode(s) != 0; }` — byte 0x02 decodes as true. The divergence is exactly as claimed and real at the codec level. However, grep across src/, include/, test/ shows the only callers of Bool::decode/encode are test/test_compact.cpp (lines 397, 405, 428); no protocol message decoder routes through compact::Bool, so no observable wire behavior differs today. Real but latent → LOW, not fixed in the working tree.

confirmed.title: Array decode length cap lowered to 4096 vs JS 0x100000 (1M)
confirmed.divergence: JS array.decode rejects only arrays with more than 0x100000 (1,048,576) elements. C++ rejects anything above 4096 (deliberate hardening, comment 'H14: lowered from 0x100000').
confirmed.scenario: A peer sends a compact array with between 4097 and 1048576 elements. JS decodes it successfully; C++ sets s.error and fails the whole message. Deliberate and documented; against a conformant HyperDHT peer the on-wire arrays (closest-nodes k=20, peer lists) never approach 4096, so it does not trigger on real conformant traffic.
confirmed.consequence: C++ rejects large-but-legal arrays that JS accepts; only reachable via non-standard/oversized peers, not normal HyperDHT traffic.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both cited sites. JS .analysis/js/compact-encoding/index.js:498-503: array.decode throws only if len > 0x100000 (1,048,576). C++ include/hyperdht/compact.hpp:204 sets ARRAY_MAX_LENGTH = 4096 (comment 'H14: lowered from 0x100000') and compact.hpp:223-227 sets s.error for count > 4096. Divergence is real and wire-reachable: 1-byte elements let a single UDP datagram carry >4096 elements, which JS decodes and C++ rejects. But it never triggers against conformant HyperDHT peers (real arrays: relays ~3, closest nodes k=20), is deliberate documented hardening, and fails in the safe direction (rejects more, never accepts more). Working tree still has the 4096 cap, so not 'fixed'; nothing needs fixing unless strict byte-for-byte JS parity on adversarial input is a goal. Minor nit: the header comment at compact.hpp:202 still claims the cap is 0x100000 — stale doc, worth a one-line update.

confirmed.title: LOOKUP reply length-prefixes each peer record; JS uses c.raw (no prefix)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/dht_messages.cpp:281,291 (encode_lookup_reply uses compact::Buffer); src/rpc_handlers.cpp:486,495 (live LOOKUP handler); include/hyperdht/dht_messages.hpp:16 comment claims "array(raw)(peers)"
confirmed.divergence: JS lookupRawReply encodes the peers as c.array(c.raw): uint(count) followed by each pre-encoded peer record's bytes concatenated with NO per-element length prefix (each peer is self-delimiting: fixed32 pubkey + ipv4Array). C++ encodes uint(count) then compact::Buffer per peer, which writes a varint length prefix before every peer's bytes. The header comment says "array(raw)" but the code uses length-prefixed Buffer.
confirmed.scenario: A JS hyperswarm/hyperdht client sends a LOOKUP request for a topic a C++ node serves. C++ replies with value = uint(count) + [varint(len)+peerbytes]... . JS decodes via lookupRawReply.decode = c.array(peer): it reads count, then tries to decode the first element as a peer starting at the inserted length-prefix byte, so it reads the length varint as the first byte of the 32-byte publicKey and mis-parses the entire reply (garbage pubkeys/addresses or throw).
confirmed.consequence: C++ LOOKUP responses are unparseable by JS peers (and vice-versa) — topic-based peer discovery via LOOKUP is wire-broken across implementations.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/messages.js:317 `const rawPeers = c.array(c.raw)` and lookupRawReply.encode (:324-327) uses rawPeers — c.array writes uint(count) then each element via c.raw, whose encode (.analysis/js/compact-encoding/raw.js:9-12) is `state.buffer.set(b, state.start)` with NO length prefix. Decode side (messages.js:332-334) uses `peers.decode` = c.array(peer), each peer self-delimiting (fixed32 publicKey + ipv4Array, messages.js:298-312). C++ side: src/rpc_handlers.cpp:486-500 (live handle_lookup) encodes uint(count) then `compact::Buffer::encode` per peer, and Buffer::encode (src/compact.cpp:222-231) writes `Uint::encode(s, len)` before the bytes — a varint length prefix JS will not skip. Same pattern in encode_lookup_reply (src/dht_messages.cpp:275-295). So a JS client decoding a C++ LOOKUP reply reads the length varint as byte 0 of the 32-byte publicKey and mis-parses the whole array — exactly the claimed scenario. The finding actually UNDERSTATES it: JS stores/sends only the m.peer record (`record = encodeUnslab(m.peer, peer)`, persistent.js:129), while C++ stores the ENTIRE announce message value (`stored.value = *req.value`, rpc_handlers.cpp:581) — so even without the prefix the bytes are the wrong record (flags+peer+signature, not bare peer), and C++ also omits the trailing uint(bump) that JS encodes. Not fixed in the working tree (current rpc_handlers.cpp still uses Buffer). HIGH: wire-format incompatibility on a served command in a project whose stated goal is wire compatibility — any JS hyperswarm client doing a LOOKUP against a C++ persistent node gets garbage/undecodable peers.

confirmed.title: LOOKUP reply returns full announce-message bytes as peers; JS returns bare peer records
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc_handlers.cpp:581 (stored.value = *req.value — the full AnnounceMessage); :475-496 (handle_lookup returns stored.value as each peer entry)
confirmed.divergence: JS stores, per announcement, only the encoded peer record (fixed32 publicKey + ipv4Array relayAddresses) and returns those in the LOOKUP reply. C++ stores the entire ANNOUNCE request value (AnnounceMessage: flags byte + peer + refresh + fixed64 signature + bump) and returns those whole blobs as the LOOKUP peer entries.
confirmed.scenario: Even ignoring the framing bug, a JS client decoding a C++ LOOKUP reply expects each element to be a peer record but instead gets an AnnounceMessage: the first byte is the announce flags (e.g. 0x05 = peer|signature) which JS reads as the first byte of publicKey; the trailing 64-byte signature + bump are extra bytes. Peer records are corrupted and the stream desyncs.
confirmed.consequence: Peer entries returned by a C++ node's LOOKUP carry the wrong payload shape (announce envelope instead of peer record), breaking JS decode of publicKey/relayAddresses independently of the length-prefix bug.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: persistent.js:129 stores record = encodeUnslab(m.peer, peer) — bare peer record (fixed32 publicKey + ipv4Array relayAddresses per messages.js:298-313) — and persistent.js:36 replies with c.encode(m.lookupRawReply, {peers, bump}), where encode is c.array(c.raw) (raw concatenation) and client decode (index.js:524 mapLookup) is c.array(peer). C++ side: src/rpc_handlers.cpp:581 stores stored.value = *req.value (the entire AnnounceMessage: flags + peer + optional refresh + fixed64 signature + bump) and handle_lookup (rpc_handlers.cpp:467-503) returns those whole blobs (additionally length-prefixed) as the peer entries; src/dht_messages.cpp:298-319 decode_lookup_reply mirrors that C++-only format. git status confirms rpc_handlers.cpp and dht_messages.cpp have no uncommitted changes, so the divergence stands in the working tree. Observable effect: a JS client decoding a C++ LOOKUP reply parses the length varint/flags byte as the start of publicKey and hits the trailing 64-byte signature — c.array(peer) throws or yields corrupted peers, so mapLookup returns null and the C++ node contributes zero usable peers to JS lookups. handle_find_peer's store fallback (rpc_handlers.cpp:452) returns the same wrong content. Content divergence is real independent of the separate framing finding. HIGH because it breaks LOOKUP interop with JS entirely for records stored on C++ nodes.

confirmed.title: closest() traverses rows in the opposite primary direction and re-sorts by XOR distance; JS does neither
confirmed.divergence: JS closest() collects rows in order d, d-1, ..., 0 FIRST (loop `for (i=d; i>=0 ...)`), then d+1, ..., 255, and returns nodes in that raw traversal order with NO distance sort. C++ collect_from goes d, d+1, ..., 255 FIRST (loop `for (i=d; i<ID_BITS; i++)`), then d-1, ..., 0, and then std::sort()s the whole result by true XOR distance to target and resizes to count. So the primary fill direction is reversed AND C++ adds a sort JS never does.
confirmed.scenario: Table has >k candidate nodes spread across rows. Example: our id vs target gives d=100; row 99 holds 20 nodes, row 150 holds 20 nodes, row 100 empty, k=20. JS fills 20 from row 99 (i=100 empty, i=99 → 20 nodes, length==k, stops) and returns row-99's nodes, never inspecting row 150. C++ fills 20 from row 150 (i=100 empty ... i=150 → 20 nodes), stops, sorts, returns row-150's nodes. The two return entirely disjoint node sets. Even in the small-table case (all nodes fit), the SET matches but the ORDER differs: JS result[0] is the lexicographically-smallest id in row d, C++ result[0] is the genuine XOR-closest node.
confirmed.consequence: closest() feeds FIND_NODE/LOOKUP responses and the local query engine. C++ returns a different node list (different membership when near rows overflow k, different order always) than the JS reference would for the same table state, so responses to real JS peers and query traversal order diverge from spec. C++ is arguably more Kademlia-correct, but it is not JS-equivalent.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides in full. JS .analysis/js/kademlia-routing-table/index.js:65-78: closest() runs `for (let i = d; i >= 0 && result.length < k; i--) this._pushNodes(...)` FIRST (rows d, d-1, ..., 0), then `for (let i = d + 1; i < this.rows.length ...)` (d+1..255), and _pushNodes (lines 80-86) appends nodes in the row's stored lexicographic order — there is no distance sort anywhere in the file; the raw traversal order is returned. C++ src/routing_table.cpp:160-197 (current working tree): first loop line 177 `for (size_t i = d; i < ID_BITS ...)` goes d, d+1, ..., 255 FIRST, second loop lines 182-185 goes d-1 down to 0, then lines 188-191 std::sort by true XOR distance (compare_distance, lines 31-39) and resize. So the primary fill direction is genuinely reversed and C++ adds a sort JS never performs — exactly as the finding claims. The finder's scenario checks out: with d=100, row 99 full (20 nodes), row 150 full, row 100 empty, k=20 — JS fills entirely from row 99 (loop 1 hits i=99 before loop 2 ever runs) while C++ fills entirely from row 150 (loop 1 reaches i=150 before its downward loop runs); the returned sets are disjoint. Even when all nodes fit, the ordering differs (JS: row-traversal + lexicographic within row; C++: true XOR-distance order). This is observable on the wire in FIND_NODE reply composition and in query seeding. Severity MED, not HIGH: it is a real reference divergence in a core primitive, but not protocol-breaking — C++'s set is by construction at least as close to the target as JS's (sorted true-distance selection), receiving peers re-sort candidates anyway, and the DHT still converges; the harm is strict-parity/observability, not correctness or interop failure.

confirmed.title: Re-adding an already-present id: JS replaces node and returns true; C++ returns false and fires the bucket-full (ping-and-swap) callback
confirmed.divergence: JS Row.add, on a binary-search hit (c===0), overwrites nodes[m]=node and returns true; size delta is 0 and no 'full'/'add' event fires. C++ Bucket::add early-returns false when has_id() is true, so the node is NOT updated; RoutingTable::add then treats the false the same as a full bucket and invokes on_full_(idx, node), spuriously kicking off ping-and-swap for a node that already exists.
confirmed.scenario: Any direct table_.add(node) call for an id already in the bucket. In the current tree the main caller add_node_from_network (src/rpc.cpp:775-790) guards with get_mut() first, so this exact path is not reached today; but the routing-table contract itself diverges, and any future/other caller that add()s a known node will (a) fail to refresh host/port/metadata and (b) trigger an unwanted eviction ping instead of a no-op.
confirmed.consequence: Contract-level divergence: add() return value and side effects differ from JS for an existing id. Latent — reachable if any caller adds without a prior get() guard.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides. JS kademlia-routing-table/index.js Row.add lines 121-141: on binary-search hit (c===0, lines 125-127) it overwrites nodes[m]=node and returns true; the 'full' event (line 135) only fires when the id is absent and the row is at k. Outer add (lines 25-29) returns true, size delta 0. C++ src/routing_table.cpp Bucket::add lines 65-68 returns false on has_id() without updating the node, and RoutingTable::add lines 121-130 conflates that false with a full bucket and fires on_full_(idx,node). The divergence is exactly as described. Reachability: I checked all four callers. add_node_from_network (src/rpc.cpp:775-790) is guarded by get_mut() and matches JS _addNodeFromNetwork, so the main network path is unaffected. reping_and_swap's do_swap (rpc.cpp:848-850) never re-adds a duplicate in practice. But the public add_node() API (include/hyperdht/dht.hpp:832-847) has NO duplicate guard — JS addNode on a known address replaces/refreshes the table entry and returns true, while C++ leaves it stale and fires on_bucket_full post-bootstrap, sending a spurious eviction PING (and a completed swap re-adds the duplicate, returns false, and recursively re-enters on_bucket_full, bounded only by repinging_). The pre-seed loop (src/dht.cpp:178-186) also hits it with duplicate seed nodes, but bootstrapped_ is false there so on_full no-ops. Real contract divergence with a reachable-but-narrow trigger (duplicate add_node() calls / duplicate seeds), hot path guarded — hence LOW, not fixed in tree.

confirmed.title: No IO-layer token validation: bad-token requests are silently dropped instead of getting an INVALID_TOKEN error reply
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/rpc.cpp:700-716 (onmessage REQUEST path, no token check); src/rpc_handlers.cpp:525-528,614-617,668-671,788-791
confirmed.divergence: JS io.onmessage validates every request that carries a token (`req.token !== null`) against BOTH current and previous secrets at the IO layer, and on mismatch calls `req.error(INVALID_TOKEN, { token: true })` — sending an error response — then drops the request before it ever reaches the command handler. The C++ RPC receive path (rpc.cpp onmessage) performs no token check at all; it dispatches straight to on_request_. Token validation exists only inside individual handlers (announce/unannounce/mutable_put-equivalent/immutable_put-equivalent), and on failure those handlers just `return` with no reply.
confirmed.scenario: A client sends an ANNOUNCE (or UNANNOUNCE / PUT) whose token has expired past the ~7.5–15s overlap window, or is otherwise wrong. Against JS the client immediately receives a response with error=INVALID_TOKEN. Against C++ the server silently drops the request; the client gets no response and must wait for its request timeout, then retries with the same stale token until it happens to re-fetch a fresh one.
confirmed.consequence: Different client-observable outcome for stale/invalid tokens: prompt error vs. timeout. Slower announce recovery and wasted retries against a C++ server; a client relying on the INVALID_TOKEN code to trigger an immediate token refresh never receives it.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read JS io.js onmessage REQUEST_ID branch (~lines 91-103): if req.token is non-null and matches neither token(req.from,1) nor token(req.from,0), JS sends req.error(INVALID_TOKEN, {token:true}) — an error reply carrying a fresh token — and never dispatches to onrequest. Read C++ rpc.cpp:697-717 (handle_message REQUEST_ID branch): no token check at all; dispatches straight to on_request_. grep for INVALID_TOKEN across src/ and include/ returns zero hits — the error code does not exist in the C++ tree. Token validation lives only in four handlers (rpc_handlers.cpp:523-529 announce, ~612-617 unannounce, ~665-672 mutable put, ~785-792 immutable put) and each silently `return`s on mismatch with no reply. TokenStore::validate does check both current and previous secrets (tokens.hpp:43-44), so only the error-reply behavior diverges, not the acceptance window. Observable difference: JS client with a stale token gets an immediate INVALID_TOKEN response (with fresh token); against C++ it gets silence and a full request timeout. Real but non-fatal parity gap — no security weakening, self-heals on retry — hence MED.

confirmed.title: C++ NAT sampler dedups by source node and never evicts, vs JS this._nat 32-slot ring with no dedup — address estimate freezes and needs ≥3 distinct sources
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/nat-sampler/index.js:14-50 (add; no dedup, 32-entry ring with hit-decrement eviction at lines 18-36); consumed as dht-rpc/index.js:69 this._nat, fed un-deduped at index.js:118,467,480,500,533
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/nat_sampler.cpp:101-116 (add: visited_ dedup, early-return on repeat, no ring/eviction); src/rpc.cpp:725 (nat_sampler_.add fed on every response)
confirmed.divergence: dht-rpc's this._nat is the nat-sampler PACKAGE: add(host,port) takes no source id, so the same remote node's repeated observations all count, and a 32-slot ring evicts the oldest sample (decrementing its hits) to form a sliding window of the ~16 most recent observations. The C++ nat_sampler_ implements the hyperdht nat.js Nat algorithm instead: it dedups by source (`visited_.count(ref)` → return false) so each distinct node contributes exactly one sample forever, and it never evicts — samples_full_/samples_host_ accumulate and hit counts only ever grow. Because sampled_ only increments on a non-duplicate, classification (sampled_>=3) requires at least 3 DISTINCT source nodes.
confirmed.scenario: (a) Address change during the pre-persistent sampling window (NAT rebind, mobile handover, interface change): the already-visited nodes re-report the NEW `to` address, but C++ drops those samples (source already in visited_), so its winner stays the stale old address; JS ages the old samples out of the ring within ~16 adds and flips to the new address. (b) Sparse connectivity: a node that only knows 2 other DHT nodes gets repeatedly pinged by them — JS this._nat builds hits past threshold and determines host/port; C++ never reaches sampled_>=3, so firewall() stays UNKNOWN and the node can never begin the persistent transition.
confirmed.consequence: C++ can compute its address-based node ID from a stale external address (wrong ID → unreachable), or fail to ever classify/go-persistent on sparse networks, where JS adapts and succeeds.
