confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: nat-sampler/index.js:14-50 confirmed — 32-slot ring, oldest-sample eviction with hits-decrement (lines 24-32), adaptive threshold (line 20: size<4 → threshold=size, so 2 consistent samples suffice), no source dedup in the package. However the finder's "fed un-deduped" claim is wrong at the dht-rpc call sites: _sampleBootstrapMaybe (index.js:473-480) dedups by source and caps at bootstrap count; the known-node path (index.js:497-500) re-feeds only after OLD_NODE=360 ticks (~30 min); _addNode (index.js:533) feeds once per node. JS is effectively one-sample-per-distinct-node-per-30-min in a sliding window; _checkIfFirewalled (index.js:947) accepts count>=1 when <5 nodes known; the persistent transition swaps in a fresh NatSampler (index.js:818,842). C++ side: src/nat_sampler.cpp:97-123 confirmed — visited_ dedups each source FOREVER, MAX_VISITED=1024 hard cap after which add() returns false unconditionally (line 103, sampling permanently stops), no eviction, hits only grow, update_firewall (line 135) needs sampled_>=3 distinct sources. rpc.cpp:725 feeds every response but the sampler self-dedups; grep shows the RPC nat_sampler_ is never reset/replaced (only health_.reset() at rpc.cpp:992), and post-persistent it still feeds announce host/port (server.cpp:364-365) and holepunch strategy (server.cpp:988-989). Observable divergence is real on two axes: (1) address staleness — after a NAT rebind, JS converges to the new address via 30-min per-node refresh + ring eviction, while C++ drops re-reports from visited nodes and freezes entirely after 1024 distinct sources, so a long-lived node advertises a dead address indefinitely; (2) C++ requires >=3 distinct sources to classify/start the persistent path where JS needs 2 samples + 1 probe reply — real but only bites at <=2 reachable nodes, a marginal scenario. Severity corrected to MED: the finder's headline mechanism (same-node repeats counting in JS) is mischaracterized — short-term both dedup per node; the genuine gap is long-run refresh/eviction and the 1024 hard stop, which does not affect fresh-start operation but breaks address tracking on long-lived nodes behind rebinding NATs (CGNAT/mobile).

confirmed.title: Winner selection has no threshold gating: C++ sets host/port to the top sample unconditionally, never expressing the host-consistent/port-random (port==0) or no-winner (host==null) states
confirmed.divergence: JS nat-sampler computes a size-dependent `_threshold = size - (size<4?0:size<8?1:size<12?2:3)` and only publishes host+port when the full-address sample crosses it (`_a.hits >= _threshold`), else publishes host with port=0 (host consistent, port random) when only the host-only sample crosses, else host=null/port=0 (no winner). C++ add() unconditionally sets `host_ = samples_full_[0].host; port_ = samples_full_[0].port;` whenever any sample exists — it never yields port==0 (unless a real observed port were literally 0) and never yields an empty/null host once ≥1 sample is in.
confirmed.scenario: Host consistent but port varies (port-random NAT) with, e.g., ports A,A,B observed: JS reports host set, port=0 (correctly signalling port unpredictable); C++ reports host + port A. The port==0 divergence is largely masked downstream because C++ check_persistent (rpc.cpp:1073,1083) additionally gates the transition on firewall()==CONSISTENT, which such weak/conflicting samples do not satisfy — so both usually end up not transitioning — but the sampler's own host()/port() outputs differ and are also read at rpc.cpp:947 (re-ping host cache) and rpc.cpp:1190 (port-preservation check).
confirmed.consequence: nat_sampler_.port() reports a concrete port where JS reports 0; any consumer treating port==0 as the port-random signal (as JS _updateNetworkState/isPortConsistent do) would behave differently. In the primary persistent path the firewall() gate happens to mask it.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side (.analysis/js/nat-sampler/index.js:14-50): add() computes _threshold = size - (0/1/2/3 by size bracket) at line 20 and tri-states output at lines 38-47 — host+port only when _a.hits >= threshold (near-unanimity, since size caps at 16 and threshold at size-3), else host+port=0 when the host-only rank crosses, else host=null/port=0. C++ side (src/nat_sampler.cpp:117-120): add() unconditionally publishes samples_full_[0].host/.port whenever any sample exists — the tri-state is structurally inexpressible. The header comment (lines 12-20) documents swapping in hyperdht nat.js's _updateFirewall ladder (verified faithful against .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/nat.js:96-133), but that ladder is a different JS layer (holepunch classification over ~4 samples); JS dht-rpc's persistent transition (index.js:800-814 bails on host===null||port===0) and remoteAddress() (index.js:201-214, returns null unless threshold-crossed host/port match server local port) consume the threshold-gated tri-state. Observable divergences: (1) rpc.cpp:1073/1089 check_persistent substitutes fw==CONSISTENT (max_hits>=3 regardless of sample count, plus the 2+2-two-IP edge at nat_sampler.cpp:153-155 where JS publishes nothing) for JS's near-unanimity gate — with e.g. 12 samples and a 3-hit top port, C++ probes and can go persistent where JS bails; masked only partially by the live probe (rpc.cpp:1165) and port-preservation check (rpc.cpp:1191). (2) connect.cpp:191-196 mirrors remoteAddress() but with ungated host/port: after a NAT remap JS nulls the address quickly (ring eviction decays _a.hits below threshold) while C++ keeps advertising the stale top sample as FIREWALL_OPEN (no eviction; port==local guard passes on port-preserving NATs). (3) server.cpp:364-365 address info and rpc.cpp:947 recheck throttle inherit the same absence of the null state. The finder's mechanism description is accurate on both sides and it correctly flagged the downstream masking. Nothing in the working tree gates host_/port_ publication. Severity MED: real, reachable, multi-consumer divergence, but every path carries extra live-evidence guards, so worst effects are weaker-evidence persistent transitions and slow-to-invalidate stale address advertisement (degraded but recoverable), not hard failures.

confirmed.title: DATA frame encodes remote_id_ instead of local_id_ — unroutable when local/remote ids differ
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:270-278 (send encodes session._localId), :504-521 (_decode looks up _remote[remoteId-1] by sender's localId)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:250-270 (Channel::send encodes remote_id_), :711-714 & :763-766 (handle_data / try_pair key remote_to_channel_ by the peer's localId)
confirmed.divergence: JS puts the SENDER's own localId on every data frame; the receiver maps that id (its _remote table, keyed by the opener's localId) back to the session. C++ Channel::send() encodes remote_id_ (the PEER's localId). The C++ receiver keys remote_to_channel_ by the peer's localId (remote.remote_local_id), so it expects the incoming channel-id to equal the SENDER's localId — which is local_id_, not remote_id_. OPEN (cpp:224) and CLOSE (cpp:287) both correctly encode local_id_; only send() uses the wrong id.
confirmed.scenario: Two peers where the paired channel has different local ids on each side (asymmetric channel counts/creation order, or the blind-relay where the relay allocates ids independently). E.g. A's channel has local_id_=2, remote_id_=1 (peer B allocated 1). A.send() emits channel-id=1. B's remote_to_channel_ for this channel is keyed by A's localId=2, so lookup of id 1 misses and B drops the message. Symmetric-id cases (both sides' first channel → local_id_==remote_id_==1) hide it, which is exactly why test_protomux.cpp SendReceiveMessage/MultipleChannels pass.
confirmed.consequence: All data messages are silently dropped by the receiver whenever the two endpoints assigned different local ids to the paired channel — breaks C++<->JS AND C++<->C++ data flow (blind-relay pair/unpair messages included). Not caught by tests because every test allocates ids symmetrically.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side read directly: protomux/index.js m.send() (lines 270-281) encodes session._localId (also batch path line 266), _decode (504-521) routes by _remote[wireId-1], and _onopensession (611-636) keys _remote by the incoming OPEN's id (the peer/sender's localId) — so the wire id on JS data frames is always the SENDER's own localId. C++ side read directly: src/protomux.cpp Channel::send (250-270) encodes remote_id_ (lines 254/260), which try_pair (765) sets to the PEER's localId; yet the C++ receiver handle_data (711-714) looks up remote_to_channel_, keyed at line 766 by the sender's localId. So C++ transmits the receiver's id while both JS and C++ receivers expect the sender's id — internally inconsistent and JS-wire-incompatible whenever local/remote ids differ. OPEN (line 225) and CLOSE (line 287) correctly use local_id_, confirming send() is the outlier. git status shows src/protomux.cpp and protomux.hpp unmodified in the working tree — not fixed. Severity corrected to MED (not HIGH): every in-tree usage (blind_relay.cpp:334; JS blind-relay creates one channel per fresh per-stream mux) yields symmetric id 1 on both sides, so all current live paths and tests are unaffected; the bug fires only with multi-channel muxes, channel churn (C++ next_local_id_ is monotonic while JS free-lists ids, so re-created channels desync), or asymmetric creation order. Fix is a one-token change (local_id_ for remote_id_ in Channel::send).

confirmed.title: Pre-pairing data is dropped, not buffered/paused — no MAX_BUFFERED backpressure (JS pauses, never drops)
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:546-563 (_bufferMessage always pushes to r.pending, _pauseMaybe pauses the stream), :628/:636 (_remote slot with pending:[] created at OPEN time), :147-156 (_drain replays pending)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:711-714 (handle_data returns/drops when no remote_to_channel_ entry), :725-736 (buffering branch), :763-769 (try_pair sets remote_to_channel_ then synchronously fully_open→opened_=true)
confirmed.divergence: JS creates a _remote[rid]={pending:[]} slot the moment a remote OPEN arrives, buffers every subsequent data frame into pending (unbounded — MAX_BACKLOG=Infinity), and applies backpressure by pausing the underlying stream when _buffered>MAX_BUFFERED; it never drops. C++ only creates the remote_to_channel_ entry at try_pair, and try_pair immediately calls fully_open (synchronous, opened_=true). So (a) data for a remote-first OPEN that has no local channel yet hits handle_data with no map entry and is dropped (cpp:714); and (b) the pre-open buffering branch at cpp:725 is dead code (opened_ is always already true when the map hit occurs). There is no stream-pause backpressure at all, and the MAX_BUFFERED path (cpp:728) drops rather than pauses.
confirmed.scenario: Remote opens a channel and sends one or more data frames before the local app creates the matching channel (app accepts the OPEN asynchronously in its notify handler rather than synchronously). JS buffers those frames and replays them on pairing; C++ drops every one (map miss). Also, if buffered data ever did accumulate past 32KB, C++ drops it whereas JS pauses the stream.
confirmed.consequence: Silent loss of the first data message(s) on a channel for any app that doesn't create+open the channel synchronously inside the OPEN notify; loss of flow-control (no stream pause).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS (index.js:627-628) creates the _remote[rid]={pending:[]} slot at OPEN receipt, buffers every pre-pairing data frame (_decode:517 → _bufferMessage:546-549), pauses the stream past MAX_BUFFERED (554-557), and replays via _drain (147-156); JS m.send gates only on `closed`, so pre-pairing data is spec-legal and blind-relay's BlindRelayRequest._open actually sends it. C++ (src/protomux.cpp) creates the remote_to_channel_ entry only in try_pair (766), which synchronously calls fully_open (769) → opened_=true (304); therefore handle_data's map miss at 713-714 silently drops any data for a remote-first OPEN with no local channel, the buffering branch at 725-737 is unreachable dead code (a map hit always sees opened_=true, or destroyed_ returns at 717 first), and the MAX_BUFFERED check (728-729) drops instead of pausing — no backpressure exists. Divergence confirmed on both counts. Severity corrected to MED rather than HIGH: the only in-tree protomux consumer is blind-relay, where both C++ (blind_relay.cpp:213,334-340,443) and JS open channels eagerly and synchronously at mux construction, so the OPEN always pairs before data can arrive; the Mux::pair notify (async-accept) path that triggers the drop has zero in-tree callers. Real parity bug with silent data loss, but latent in the current codebase.

confirmed.title: C++ never sends REJECT — unaccepted incoming opens and control-session opens leak instead of being rejected
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:602-605 (control-session open → _rejectSession(0)), :683-695 (_requestSession rejects every still-incoming open after the notify round), :697-722 (_rejectSession emits a [0,2,remoteId] frame)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:599-635 (handle_open: raw_id==0 → silent return; unpaired open stored in pending_remote_ and never rejected), grep: no CONTROL_REJECT is ever encoded anywhere
confirmed.divergence: JS emits a REJECT control frame in two cases: a remote OPEN on the control session (remoteId=0), and any incoming OPEN that no local channel claimed after the pair/notify round. C++ contains no code path that encodes a REJECT at all — it only decodes them (handle_reject). Unclaimed opens sit forever in pending_remote_.
confirmed.scenario: Peer opens a channel whose (protocol,id) the local side declines (notify handler creates no channel). JS sends REJECT → peer's open() resolves false and the peer tears down its half-open channel. C++ sends nothing → the peer's channel stays half-open indefinitely, and the local pending_remote_ entry leaks.
confirmed.consequence: Peer-side channel/resource leak and no negative acknowledgement; local pending_remote_ map grows unbounded with declined opens.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:602-605 sends _rejectSession(0) for control-session opens; :683-695 (_requestSession) rejects every still-incoming open after the notify round; :697-722 (_rejectSession) encodes [0,2,remoteId] and writes it via _write0. C++ side: src/protomux.cpp:604 returns silently on raw_id==0 (comment at :596 explicitly admits 'C++ silently ignores'); :626-634 stores unclaimed opens in pending_remote_ and only fires dispatch_notify (:799-825), which never emits anything on decline; pending_remote_ is erased only when a local channel later claims the key (:237-240) or via remove_channel (:855). Grep confirms CONTROL_REJECT appears only in decode dispatch (:570, :697) — no encode path anywhere in src/ or include/. Divergence is real and observable: a peer whose open is declined never gets REJECT, so its open() never resolves false and its channel stays half-open, while the C++ pending_remote_ entry persists indefinitely. Severity corrected to MED: in hyperdht's own use protomux runs only over blind-relay where both sides proactively open the same channel, so the decline path rarely triggers; impact is limited to external protomux users or misbehaving peers, with a bounded per-key leak.

confirmed.title: Missing invalid-open sequence validation — JS destroys the stream on out-of-sequence/duplicate remote id, C++ silently accepts
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:610-617 (remote ids may only grow by one; reusing a non-null _remote slot → throw 'Invalid open message' → _safeDestroy), :638-640 (backlog cap → throw)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:599-635 (handle_open validates only 0<raw_id<=UINT32_MAX; no sequence/duplicate check; try_pair overwrites remote_to_channel_[id])
confirmed.divergence: JS enforces that incoming remote ids are allocated strictly sequentially and that a slot is not reused while live, treating a violation as a fatal protocol error that destroys the stream. C++ accepts any id and, on a duplicate id, overwrites the existing remote_to_channel_ mapping (or the pending_remote_ entry for the same key).
confirmed.scenario: A malformed or malicious peer sends an OPEN reusing an already-live remote id, or a wildly non-sequential id. JS tears the connection down; C++ continues, silently rebinding the id to a different channel (misrouting subsequent data) or clobbering a pending open.
confirmed.consequence: Robustness/security divergence: C++ tolerates and misroutes on protocol violations that JS rejects fatally.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js _onopensession — `if (this._remote.length === rid) this._remote.push(null)` (grow-by-one only), then `if (rid >= this._remote.length || this._remote[rid] !== null) throw new Error('Invalid open message')`, and `if (++this._remoteBacklog > MAX_BACKLOG) throw new Error('Remote exceeded backlog')`. The throw is fatal: _decode is called inside try/catch at index.js:486-489 which calls `this._safeDestroy(err)`, destroying the stream. C++ side verified: src/protomux.cpp handle_open (lines 599-635) validates only `raw_id != 0 && raw_id <= UINT32_MAX`; there is no sequence, duplicate, or backlog check anywhere (grepped for backlog — absent). On a reused live id, try_pair (line 763-770) does `remote_to_channel_[remote.remote_local_id] = local`, silently overwriting the existing mapping so handle_data/handle_close (lines 659, 713) misroute to the new channel; worse, when the clobbered old channel is later destroyed, line 849 `remote_to_channel_.erase(ch->remote_id_)` erases the LIVE channel's entry — state corruption. pending_remote_[key] similarly overwrites, and pending_remote_ has no cap (JS caps at MAX_BACKLOG), allowing unbounded per-connection growth from unique protocol strings. Divergence is real and observable: JS tears down the connection on protocol violation; C++ silently continues in a corrupted state. Not fixed in working tree. Severity MED rather than HIGH: requires a malicious/broken authenticated peer (a conforming JS peer never reuses a live id, so no interop impact), damage is confined to that one connection, and in this codebase protomux is only exercised on the blind-relay path.

confirmed.title: Batch decode caps at 1024 entries and silently stops — JS processes unbounded entries
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:565-593 (_onbatch loops over the whole frame with no entry cap)
confirmed.divergence: JS decodes every message in a batch frame. C++ stops (returns) after 1024 sub-messages, silently discarding the remainder of the frame.
confirmed.scenario: A peer (JS) corks and sends >1024 small messages, packed into one batch frame within the 8MB MAX_BATCH limit. C++ delivers the first 1024 and drops the rest.
confirmed.consequence: Silent data loss on large legitimate batches.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS _onbatch (.analysis/js/protomux/index.js:565-592) loops `while (state.end > state.start)` with no entry cap, decoding every sub-message; the JS sender (lines 418-421) only flushes on byte size (MAX_BATCH=8MB, line 9), never on entry count, so >1024-entry batches are legitimately produced by a corked JS peer. C++ handle_batch (src/protomux.cpp:669-677, current working tree) has `constexpr size_t MAX_BATCH_ENTRIES = 1024` and `if (++entries > MAX_BATCH_ENTRIES) return;` — the 1025th entry silently discards the rest of the frame, including any control OPEN/REJECT/CLOSE sub-messages. Real observable divergence: silent message loss on a reliable stream. The cap is a deliberate hardening (M5 comment) still present in the tree, so not already fixed. Severity MED rather than HIGH: requires a peer corking >1024 messages in one uncork window, uncommon in this library's blind-relay protomux usage, but consequences (dropped data/control messages, channel desync) are serious when triggered.

confirmed.title: uncork() never splits at MAX_BATCH (8MB) — emits one oversized frame
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:417-422 (_pushBatch flushes and starts a new batch once state.end>=MAX_BATCH=8MB)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:484-539 (uncork builds a single frame from the entire batch_ with no size split)
confirmed.divergence: JS bounds each emitted batch frame at ~8MB, flushing mid-accumulation. C++ accumulates the whole corked batch and emits it as one frame regardless of size.
confirmed.scenario: An application corks and sends more than 8MB of messages before uncork. JS produces multiple <=8MB frames; C++ produces one frame larger than the SecretStream/udx max message size, which the peer may reject or fail to decode.
confirmed.consequence: Oversized single frame on very large batches; differs from JS framing and can exceed transport limits. The cpp comment block (lines 441-450) claiming byte-level parity with _sendBatch omits this split.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:9 defines MAX_BATCH = 8*1024*1024, and _pushBatch (lines 417-429) starts with `if (this._batchState.end >= MAX_BATCH) { this._sendBatch(...); this._batch = []; this._batchState = {...end:1} }` — so JS flushes the accumulated batch mid-cork and starts a new frame once the pre-encoded size reaches 8MB. C++ side: src/protomux.cpp Mux::write_frame (lines 453-478) under cork_count_>0 unconditionally appends to batch_ with no size check, and Mux::uncork (lines 484-539) computes the total size of the entire batch and emits it as ONE frame via write_fn_ — grep confirms no MAX_BATCH constant or split logic anywhere in src/protomux.cpp or include/hyperdht/protomux.hpp (the only MAX_BATCH_* there is MAX_BATCH_ENTRIES=1024 on the RECEIVE path, handle_batch line 669, which is unrelated). So the divergence is real and not fixed in tree. Severity corrected to LOW: (a) grep shows no cork() callers anywhere in src/ or include/ outside protomux.cpp itself — the DHT's own flows (blind-relay control messages, all tiny) never cork, so the oversized-frame path is reachable only if application/FFI code corks and queues >8MB before uncorking; (b) even JS's bound is soft — the check runs before pushing, so a JS batch can also exceed 8MB by up to one message; the parity gap only becomes observable well past 8MB of corked data. Real parity gap in the public Channel::cork/Mux::cork API, but no internal code path can trigger it.

confirmed.title: Multi-message incoming batch does not cork the reply — C++ emits N reply frames vs JS one batched frame
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:578-592 (_onbatch corks the mux when a batch has >1 message so all replies are re-batched, then uncorks after processing)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:665-709 (handle_batch dispatches each sub-message with no surrounding cork/uncork)
confirmed.divergence: When an incoming batch triggers side-effect replies (e.g. an OPEN whose handler sends messages), JS batches all those replies into a single control frame; C++ writes each reply as its own frame.
confirmed.scenario: Peer sends a corked batch containing multiple opens/messages that provoke replies. JS answers with one batched frame; C++ answers with several separate frames.
confirmed.consequence: Different on-wire framing (more frames, more packets) though each frame is individually valid/decodable.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS protomux/index.js:565-593 (_onbatch) corks the mux when a batch has >1 message (line 579-582) and uncorks after Promise.all of the handlers (line 591), so all handler replies coalesce into one CONTROL_BATCH frame. C++ src/protomux.cpp:665-709 (handle_batch) dispatches each sub-message (handle_open/reject/close/data) with no cork/uncork bracket, even though Mux::cork exists (line 480) and write_frame honors cork_count_ (line 457). So C++ genuinely emits N separate frames where JS emits one batched frame — divergence confirmed and not fixed in tree. Severity corrected to LOW: both framings are valid on the wire and decode identically on the peer (its _ondata accepts single or batch frames), so the effect is packet-count/efficiency only, with no correctness or interop failure; protomux here is only used via blind-relay, further narrowing exposure.

confirmed.title: pending_remote_ holds only one open per (protocol,id) key — concurrent second OPEN clobbers the first
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:643 (info.incoming is a queue: push, multiple pending), :690-692 (drains all incoming)
confirmed.divergence: JS queues all incoming opens for a key in info.incoming[]. C++ stores a single PendingOpen per key in an unordered_map, so a second remote OPEN for the same (protocol,id) before the first is paired overwrites the first.
confirmed.scenario: Peer opens two non-unique channels with the same (protocol,id) before the local side pairs either. C++ loses the first open's remote id (it never pairs; leaks), JS pairs both in order.
confirmed.consequence: Lost/leaked channel pairing for non-unique multi-channel (protocol,id) usage.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:643 pushes each unpaired remote OPEN onto info.incoming (an array — line 461 initializes it as []), line 405 shifts one per createChannel (non-unique channels are legal: line 397 only blocks duplicates when unique=true), and lines 690-692 REJECT-drain whatever remains after notify. So JS queues N concurrent opens per (protocol,id) and pairs or rejects every one. C++ side verified: src/protomux.cpp:599-635 handle_open — when no local channel matches (find_by_pair_key at :620 returns null both times since no channel exists yet), line 631 `pending_remote_[key] = std::move(pending)` on a std::unordered_map<std::string, PendingOpen> (protomux.hpp:274) assigns through operator[], so a second OPEN for the same key before pairing destroys the first PendingOpen. The first remote channel is never paired and never rejected (C++ has no _rejectSession equivalent on this path), so it hangs open on the remote side — observably different from JS. The mechanical divergence is real and I could not refute it. However, severity is LOW, not higher: (a) it requires the remote to open two non-unique channels with the identical (protocol,id) before the local side pairs either — hyperdht's only protomux consumer (blind-relay) uses one unique channel per stream, so the honest-peer path never hits this; (b) the C++ failure mode is a silently dropped open (old PendingOpen is destructed on overwrite — no memory leak or corruption locally, just a stalled channel on the remote); (c) protomux runs over a reliable encrypted stream, so no duplicate-delivery path triggers it accidentally. The code comment at src/protomux.cpp:592-595 claims the flat map is "equivalent state" to JS's incoming queue — that claim is wrong for this corner, so nothing in the working tree already handles it.

confirmed.title: ondrain only fires on fully-opened channels — JS fires on every live local channel
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:492-498 (_ondrain iterates _local and calls ondrain on every non-null session, opened or not)
confirmed.divergence: JS puts a channel into _local as soon as open() is called and fires ondrain on it thereafter regardless of pairing state. C++ only invokes on_drain when opened_ is true.
confirmed.scenario: A channel has sent OPEN but not yet paired when the underlying stream drains. JS delivers a drain event; C++ suppresses it.
confirmed.consequence: Missed drain/backpressure-release callback during the open-but-not-yet-paired window.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: index.js:492-497 _ondrain calls s.ondrain(s) on every non-null entry of _local with no opened check, and open() (index.js:73-78) inserts the session into _local the moment OPEN is sent — before _fullyOpen (index.js:119-124) sets opened=true. C++ side verified: src/protomux.cpp:834-844 on_stream_drain gates on ch->opened_, which only flips in Channel::fully_open (protomux.cpp:303-304, both sides opened). So an OPEN-sent-but-unpaired channel receives drain in JS and not in C++ — the divergence is real and unfixed. Severity downgraded to LOW: grep shows no ondrain consumer anywhere in .analysis/js/hyperdht or .analysis/js/blind-relay (nobody passes ondrain to createChannel), no C++ code sets a protomux Channel::on_drain outside protomux.cpp itself, and C++ send() (protomux.cpp:251) already blocks writes until opened_, so a pre-pair channel cannot be waiting on backpressure from channel messages. Observable only to a hypothetical external user of the C++ protomux API, never in the hyperdht pipeline.

confirmed.title: Stray REJECT handling differs — JS treats reject-after-open/unknown-id as fatal, C++ closes/ignores
confirmed.js_ref: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js:648-668 (_onrejectsession searches outgoing lists; not found → throw 'Invalid reject message'; only closes channels still awaiting open)
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/protomux.cpp:637-649 (handle_reject looks up local_to_channel_ and calls remote_close(); unknown id → silent return)
confirmed.divergence: JS only accepts a REJECT for a channel still in its outgoing (not-yet-opened) list and destroys the stream for any REJECT it can't match; a REJECT for an already-opened channel is unmatched → fatal. C++ closes whatever local channel matches the id (even a fully opened one) and silently ignores unmatched rejects.
confirmed.scenario: Peer (or attacker) sends a REJECT for a channel id that is already fully opened. JS destroys the connection (protocol error); C++ closes that live channel.
confirmed.consequence: A late/forged REJECT tears down a live channel in C++ instead of being rejected as a protocol error.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: _onrejectsession (.analysis/js/protomux/index.js:648-668) only matches localIds still in info.outgoing (pending opens); _onopensession shifts a channel out of outgoing when it pairs, so a REJECT for an opened channel is unmatched and hits `throw new Error('Invalid reject message')` (line 667). _ondata (482-489) catches it and calls _safeDestroy — the whole mux/stream dies. C++ side: handle_reject (src/protomux.cpp:637-649) looks up local_to_channel_, which is filled at channel creation (line 415) and erased only on channel destroy (line 851), so fully-opened channels ARE matched and get remote_close(); unmatched ids silently return. Divergence is real and observable: reject-after-open closes one live channel in C++ vs destroying the connection in JS; unknown-id reject is ignored in C++ vs fatal in JS. The legitimate pending-open reject path matches. Severity LOW: protomux runs over an authenticated secret-stream (blind-relay only in this codebase), so only the peer itself can send stray rejects; no crash or memory issue, just lenient vs fatal handling of peer misbehavior.

confirmed.title: Relay streams are never connect()-ed to peers: firewall/connect(remoteId) wiring is entirely absent
confirmed.divergence: JS BlindRelayLink.createStream() creates each relay stream with a per-link firewall callback `_createStream({ firewall: this._onfirewall })`, and `_onfirewall(socket, port, host)` calls `this.stream.connect(socket, this.remoteId, port, host); return false` — this is the ONLY mechanism by which a relay stream learns the peer's observed UDP source and binds to it using that peer's stream id (remoteId). C++ `CreateStreamFn` is typed `std::function<udx_stream_t*()>` (no firewall parameter, and no way to pass the per-link remote_id), and blind_relay.cpp calls `server_.create_stream_fn()` with no firewall and never calls udx_stream_connect on any relay stream. udx_stream_relay_to only cross-links two local streams' data flow; each still needs connect() to have a remote to send to.
confirmed.scenario: Two peers pair through a relay built on BlindRelayServer. Relay creates stream_a/stream_b and relay_to's them, but neither is connected to either peer's address/stream-id. No relayed bytes can ever be delivered.
confirmed.consequence: The relay-node data path is non-functional as implemented. Currently latent: no BlindRelayServer is instantiated anywhere in the DHT (grep-confirmed dead code), so not observable against a live peer today — but any relay node built on this code would fail to route.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS (.analysis/js/blind-relay/index.js:268-279): BlindRelayLink.createStream() passes a per-link firewall cb to _createStream, and _onfirewall calls this.stream.connect(socket, this.remoteId, port, host) — the sole mechanism connecting relay streams to peers. C++ (include/hyperdht/blind_relay.hpp:205, src/blind_relay.cpp:512-540): CreateStreamFn is std::function<udx_stream_t*()> (no firewall/remoteId/addr args); on_pair only calls create_stream_fn() + udx_stream_relay_to(); zero udx_stream_connect/udx_stream_firewall calls in blind_relay.cpp. libudx confirms total breakage: udx.c:1466 skips the firewall branch when on_firewall==NULL, and udx.c:1373 relay_packet requires relay->socket!=NULL plus remote_id/remote_addr (set only by udx_stream_connect) — otherwise the packet is silently dropped. So a C++ BlindRelayServer can pair peers but never deliver a single relayed byte, in both directions. Divergence CONFIRMED. Severity corrected to MED, not HIGH: BlindRelayServer is instantiated nowhere in production code (only test/test_blind_relay.cpp with nullptr-returning factories), and the hyperdht peer paths (connect.cpp:869/942, server.cpp:718/794) use BlindRelayClient and connect their raw streams correctly — matching JS hyperdht, which likewise only uses relay.Client. Only the unused act-as-relay-operator role is broken. Note docs/archive/JS-PARITY-GAPS.md:87 wrongly marks E3 as complete. Not fixed by the uncommitted working-tree changes (blind_relay.* untouched).

confirmed.title: Server sends pair confirmation AFTER closing the channel (ordering inverted vs JS)
confirmed.divergence: JS _onpair pass 3 does, per link and in order: `session._pair.send(confirmation)` (175) THEN `session._endMaybe()` (182, which may close the channel) THEN emit. C++ performs the _endMaybe-equivalent close in the stream-tracking loop (`if (link.session->pending_close_ && link.session->pairing_tokens_.empty()) link.session->channel_->close();`, lines 545-547) which runs BEFORE the confirmation-send loop (553-576). So C++ closes the channel first, then calls channel_->send() on the closed channel where the message is dropped.
confirmed.scenario: A relay session had close() called while a pairing was still in-flight (sets pending_close_=true). When that last pairing completes, C++ closes the channel at line 546 and then tries to send the pair confirmation at line 563 on the now-closed channel — the peer never receives its assigned relay stream id.
confirmed.consequence: Peer never learns its relay stream id → relay setup fails for that peer on a graceful-close race. Latent (relay-node server unused), but a direct violation of the send-before-close ordering the JS relies on.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides. JS (.analysis/js/blind-relay/index.js): pass 3 (174-185) per link sends the pair confirmation (175) BEFORE session._endMaybe() (182); _endMaybe (230-234) is the only place the channel closes on the end() path, so JS always delivers the confirmation first. C++ (src/blind_relay.cpp): the stream-tracking loop (540-549) performs the _endMaybe-equivalent — channel_->close() at 546 when pending_close_ && pairing_tokens_.empty() — BEFORE the confirmation-send loop (553-576) calls channel_->send() at 563. Ordering is genuinely inverted. Impact is worse than claimed: protomux Channel::close() (src/protomux.cpp:278-297) calls destroy(), which via mux_.remove_channel(this) frees the channel (comment at protomux.cpp:351-353: "this pointer becomes dangling"), so the later send at blind_relay.cpp:563 is a use-after-free, not just a dropped message; even if it survived, send() returns false when closed_ (protomux.cpp:251) and the result is unchecked. Severity downgraded to MED, not HIGH, because the trigger is unreachable through current production wiring: pending_close_ is set only in BlindRelaySession::close() (446-455), whose sole caller BlindRelayServer::close() (344-350) immediately destroys all sessions via sessions_.clear(), and BlindRelayServer itself is instantiated nowhere in production src/ (only test/test_blind_relay.cpp) — production code uses only BlindRelayClient. Latent real bug in public library API; not fixed in tree. Fix is trivial: move the pending_close_ check after the confirmation loop, matching JS pass-3 ordering.

confirmed.title: BlindRelayServer::close() is not graceful — destroys sessions immediately instead of awaiting end()
confirmed.divergence: JS Server.close() calls `session.end()` for every session and `await Promise.all(ending)` — end() resolves only on the session 'close' event, i.e. after in-flight pairings complete and the channel actually closes (via _endMaybe). C++ close() calls `session->close()` (which for a session with in-flight pairings merely sets pending_close_=true and returns WITHOUT closing) and then immediately runs `sessions_.clear()`, destroying every session unique_ptr synchronously, and `pairings_.clear()`.
confirmed.scenario: Relay server shuts down while a session has a pairing in-flight. JS lets that pairing finish and confirmations go out before closing. C++ destroys the session object right away (~BlindRelaySession nulls channel callbacks and destroys streams), abandoning the in-flight pairing.
confirmed.consequence: In-flight pairings are dropped on shutdown rather than drained; pending_close_ path is effectively dead because sessions_.clear() runs unconditionally right after. Latent (server unused).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS Server.close() (blind-relay/index.js:36-45) awaits session.end() for every session; end() (221-228) resolves on 'close', and _endMaybe (230-234) closes the channel only when _pairing.size === 0 — in-flight pairings drain first (finalize calls session._endMaybe() at line 182), and _pairing.clear() runs only after all sessions closed. C++ BlindRelayServer::close() (src/blind_relay.cpp:344-350) calls session->close() — which for sessions with in-flight pairings merely sets pending_close_=true (446-455) — then immediately sessions_.clear() + pairings_.clear(). sessions_.clear() destroys every session unique_ptr synchronously; ~BlindRelaySession (425-440) nulls channel callbacks and udx_stream_destroys relay streams, so the deferred-close machinery (545-546, 589-590) never fires on this path and in-flight pairings are abandoned. Divergence is real. Severity downgraded to LOW because BlindRelayServer is unreachable from any product path in the C++ tree (only test_blind_relay.cpp uses it), and JS hyperdht itself never instantiates relay.Server either (connect.js:764, server.js:645 use only relay.Client) — the impact is confined to graceful shutdown of a hypothetical future relay node, where peers would time out instead of receiving their final pair confirmation.

confirmed.title: No per-relay-stream 'close'/'error' handlers on the server side
confirmed.divergence: JS pass 2 wires each relay stream with `.on('error', session._onerror)` (re-emits stream errors on the session) and `.on('close', () => session._streams.delete(keyString))` (removes the stream from _streams when it closes), before relayTo. C++ calls udx_stream_relay_to both directions and stores the stream in streams_[key] but attaches no close or error callback.
confirmed.scenario: A paired relay stream closes because one peer disconnects. JS removes the entry from _streams immediately, so a later unpair(token) or session _onclose is a no-op for it. C++ keeps the (now-closed) pointer in streams_ and will later call udx_stream_destroy on it from on_unpair or the channel on_close handler; stream errors are also silently swallowed rather than surfaced.
confirmed.consequence: Stale streams_ entries after remote close, no error propagation, and a destroy call on an already-closed stream. Latent (server unused).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/blind-relay/index.js pass 2 (_onpair) does `stream.on('error', session._onerror).on('close', () => session._streams.delete(keyString)).relayTo(remote.stream)`, where `_onerror = (err) => this.emit('error', err)` (index.js:87). C++ side verified: src/blind_relay.cpp:523-549 (working tree) calls udx_stream_relay_to both directions and stores `link.session->streams_[key] = link.stream` with NO close or error callback attached anywhere (also checked session ctor at 385-423, dtor at 425-440, on_unpair at 580-604 — none install per-stream callbacks). So the code divergence is real: after a relay stream closes on its own, C++ keeps a stale entry in streams_ forever (JS deletes it), so streams_ grows unboundedly on a long-lived session, and stream errors are never surfaced session-level. HOWEVER the finding's failure scenario overstates the consequence in two ways I verified: (1) deps/libudx/src/udx.c:2698-2702 — udx_stream_destroy on an already-CLOSED stream is an explicit safe no-op (`if (stream->status & UDX_STREAM_CLOSED) return 0`), so the later destroy from on_unpair/on_close/dtor does NOT crash as long as the udx_stream_t memory is still alive; a use-after-free only materializes if the create_stream_fn provider frees the stream in its own close callback — a latent lifetime-contract hazard, not a demonstrated bug. (2) Reachability: grep shows BlindRelayServer/BlindRelaySession are referenced ONLY in src/blind_relay.cpp, include/hyperdht/blind_relay.hpp, and test/test_blind_relay.cpp — production code (connect.cpp, server.cpp) uses only BlindRelayClient, and nothing in src/ or the C FFI constructs a BlindRelayServer or supplies a create_stream_fn. The relay-hosting role is test-only library code today. Real parity gap (stale map entries + unsurfaced errors) but unreachable in production and non-crashing given libudx's closed-stream guard → LOW, not fixed in tree.

confirmed.title: Client registers an inbound-unpair handler; JS client ignores inbound unpair
confirmed.divergence: JS BlindRelayClient registers the unpair message with NO onmessage handler (`this._unpair = this._channel.addMessage({ encoding: m.unpair })`) — the client never acts on an inbound unpair; the unpair type exists only for sending. C++ registers on_unpair_response which, on any inbound unpair matching a pending token, erases the request and fires on_error(PAIRING_CANCELLED).
confirmed.scenario: A relay (or a peer able to inject on that channel) sends an unpair for a token the client is actively pairing. JS ignores it and keeps pairing; C++ cancels the pairing and reports PAIRING_CANCELLED to the caller.
confirmed.consequence: C++ can abort a live client pairing on an inbound unpair that JS would ignore. Not triggered by a stock JS relay (which never sends unpair to clients), so effectively a robustness/attack-surface divergence rather than a normal-path one.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: .analysis/js/blind-relay/index.js:319-321 registers the client's unpair message with encoding only, no onmessage; protomux/index.js:240 shows onmessage defaults to noop, so JS clients silently ignore inbound unpair. The only client-side use is sending (index.js:397-404); the _onunpair handler at lines 76-78/188 belongs to the server session class. C++ side: src/blind_relay.cpp:176-181 registers on_unpair_response for message type 1, and lines 308-319 erase the matching pending request and fire on_error(PAIRING_CANCELLED). So an inbound unpair cancels an active pairing in C++ but is a no-op in JS — a genuine observable divergence, still present in the working tree. Severity corrected to LOW: no conforming relay ever sends unpair to a client (only client→server in the reference), so it only triggers with a malicious/buggy relay, which could already deny pairing by simply not relaying — no meaningful security or interop delta in normal operation.

confirmed.title: Malformed pair/unpair messages are silently accepted instead of tearing down the connection
confirmed.divergence: JS decodes via compact-encoding (flags.decode + c.fixed32.decode + c.uint.decode); a truncated/short message makes fixed32/uint decode read past the buffer and throw, which propagates through protomux and tears down the channel/connection. C++ decode_pair/decode_unpair never throw: on truncation they hit guards (`if (ptr + 32 > end) return m;`, `if (ptr < end) ...`) and return a partially/zero-filled message (e.g. all-zero token), which on_pair/on_pair_response then processes as a real message.
confirmed.scenario: A peer sends a truncated pair frame (e.g. <33 bytes). JS throws in fixed32.decode and destroys the mux. C++ returns a PairMessage with token=all-zeros and processes it (client: key miss → silent ignore; server: creates/looks up a pairing under the zero token).
confirmed.consequence: C++ does not perform the malformed-message teardown JS does and can act on a garbage (zero-token) message. Robustness/parity gap; requires a malformed frame a stock peer won't send.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS: blind-relay/index.js:495-504,516-522 decode via c.fixed32/c.uint, which throw 'Out of bounds' on truncation (compact-encoding/index.js:46,463,479). The throw propagates from the protomux onmessage wrapper (protomux/index.js:249-251) through Channel._recv into Muxer._ondata's try/catch (482-490), whose catch calls _safeDestroy → stream.destroy(err) (737-740) — connection torn down. C++: src/blind_relay.cpp decode_pair (98-121, guards at 103/110/115/118) and decode_unpair (138-154, guards 143/149) return a zero-filled message on truncation (Token token{} in blind_relay.hpp:90-99 zero-initializes, so no UB). Handlers then process it: client on_pair_response (284-305) silently ignores on key miss; server on_pair (464-479) registers a pairing under the all-zero token via get_or_create_pair. No teardown path exists in C++ protomux for payload decode failure. Divergence is real and observable, but only under malformed/malicious input, with no crash/memory unsafety and pairing map capped at 1024 — a strictness/robustness gap, not an interop or safety bug, hence LOW.

confirmed.title: C++ adds a 30s unpaired-pairing TTL and a 1024-pairing cap that JS does not have
confirmed.divergence: JS keeps a server pairing entry alive until the owning channel closes — no TTL, no count cap. C++ get_or_create_pair() evicts any unpaired pairing older than 30s on every new pair attempt, and on_pair rejects new tokens once pairing_count() >= 1024.
confirmed.scenario: On a relay, peer A sends its pair and peer B arrives >30s later (slow/congested holepunch). JS still has A's half and completes the pairing; C++ has already evicted A, so B's arrival creates a fresh half-pairing that never matches A → relay fails. Separately, >1024 concurrent unpaired tokens are rejected outright (JS is unbounded).
confirmed.consequence: Divergent success for slow second-arrivers and under high concurrency. Defensible DoS hardening, but a behavioral divergence from the JS spec. Latent (server unused). Note: the client side correctly has no pairing TTL, matching JS.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read JS .analysis/js/blind-relay/index.js:137-186 (_onpair) plus _onclose (113-115) and _onunpair (188-211): pairing entries in _server._pairing have no TTL and no count cap; they persist until paired, unpaired, or the owning channel closes. Read C++ src/blind_relay.cpp:352-371 (get_or_create_pair sweeps and erases unpaired entries older than 30s on every call, PAIRING_TTL, comment H25) and 464-478 (on_pair silently drops new tokens when pairing_count() >= 1024, MAX_PAIRINGS). Both mechanisms are real, present in the working tree, and absent from JS, so the divergence is confirmed: a pair half arriving >30s after its counterpart completes in JS but fails in C++, and >1024 concurrent unpaired tokens are rejected in C++ but accepted in JS. Severity corrected to LOW: these are deliberate H25 DoS-hardening additions, and in the actual hyperdht holepunch flow both pair messages arrive within seconds (the punch session and relay paths have ~10-15s bounds), so a >30s skew means the connect already failed for other reasons; the 1024 cap only bites at attack scale where C++'s bounded behavior is safer than JS's unbounded map. Real code divergence, negligible effect on legitimate traffic, intentional rather than a misport.

confirmed.title: LOOKUP reply uses a non-JS wire format (per-record length prefix, no bump, full-announce elements) — JS lookup misdecodes every C++ node's records
confirmed.divergence: JS onlookup replies with c.encode(m.lookupRawReply,{peers,bump}) = uint(count) + concat(raw peer records, each self-delimiting publicKey(32)+ipv4Array) + uint(bump). C++ handle_lookup encodes uint(count) + for-each[ uint(len) + value ] and appends NO bump. Additionally each stored `value` is the FULL announce message (flags+peer+signature, from handle_announce storing *req.value at line 581), not a bare m.peer record. JS mapLookup does c.decode(m.lookupRawReply,...) which reads count then decodes `count` m.peer structs; the extra per-element length varint (and the announce flags byte) misalign fixed32 publicKey reads.
confirmed.scenario: A non-ephemeral C++ node holds a topic's announce records and a JS client's lookup query lands on it. c.decode(m.lookupRawReply, value) reads a wrong/oversized varint or runs past buffer end → throws → mapLookup returns null → that node contributes zero peers. JS discovery silently loses every peer stored on C++ nodes.
confirmed.consequence: JS clients cannot discover peers whose announces are held by C++ DHT nodes; lookup returns empty for those records. bump signalling is also lost.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides. JS: persistent.js:36 replies c.encode(m.lookupRawReply,{peers,bump}); messages.js:317-334 defines lookupRawReply as c.array(c.raw) + uint(bump) — verified in compact-encoding that c.raw has no per-element length prefix (raw.js:6-17) and c.array prefixes only the count (index.js:488-505); stored records are bare m.peer (fixed32 pubkey + ipv4Array), persistent.js:129/142; index.js:520-536 mapLookup strictly decodes lookupRawReply in try/catch → null on failure. C++: src/rpc_handlers.cpp:480-501 encodes uint(count) + per-record compact::Buffer (varint length prefix, verified src/compact.cpp:222-232) with no bump; rpc_handlers.cpp:581 stores the FULL announce wire message (flags+peer+signature) as the record value. So a JS client's fixed32 publicKey read lands on the length varint + announce flags byte — misaligned → throw or garbage → mapLookup returns null → every peer stored on a non-ephemeral C++ node is invisible to JS lookups. Minor correction to the finding: the missing bump alone is harmless (JS decode treats bump as optional, messages.js:331), but the per-element length prefix and full-announce payload each independently break decoding. dht_messages.cpp:275-320 has a same-wrong-format codec with no production callers, so nothing in the tree emits the JS format; not fixed. HIGH: silent peer-discovery loss in a project whose stated goal is wire compatibility.

confirmed.title: FIND_PEER served from the announce store returns the full announce message, not the m.peer record — JS mapFindPeer misdecodes
confirmed.divergence: JS onfindpeer replies with fwd.record (only), where record=encodeUnslab(m.peer,peer)=publicKey(32)+relayAddresses; and JS returns NULL when there is no router forward entry. C++ handle_find_peer falls back to the AnnounceStore and returns peers.back().value, which is the entire announce message (*req.value: flags byte + peer + 64-byte signature). JS mapFindPeer does c.decode(m.peer, node.value): it reads the announce flags byte as the first byte of publicKey and the signature/relay bytes as ipv4Array → wrong publicKey or a decode throw.
confirmed.scenario: A peer self-announces (target=hash(pubkey)) to a non-ephemeral C++ node (stored in store_, since C++ never routes remote self-announces to the router). A JS client's findPeer(pubkey) reaches that C++ node; mapFindPeer decodes a corrupt m.peer (wrong publicKey/relayAddresses) or returns null.
confirmed.consequence: findPeer answers served by C++ store nodes are unusable to JS clients (wrong publicKey/relay data), and C++ also answers findPeer where JS would return null. The router-served path (Server's own key) is correct; only the store fallback is malformed.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: persistent.js:39-43 onfindpeer replies ONLY `fwd.record` from dht._router (or null); that record is built at persistent.js:129 as encodeUnslab(m.peer, peer) = fixed32 publicKey + ipv4Array relayAddresses (messages.js:298-313) — no flags byte, no signature. index.js:538-551 mapFindPeer does c.decode(m.peer, node.value) inside try/catch→null; compact-encoding's decode (index.js:986-988) does not reject leftover bytes. C++ side verified: rpc_handlers.cpp:430-456 handle_find_peer first checks router_ (this path is FINE — server.cpp:211 stores announcer record_ = encode_peer_record, matching m.peer format), then falls back to store_ and returns peers.back().value. That stored value is the raw ANNOUNCE message: handle_announce line 581 stores `stored.value = *req.value`, whose wire format per messages.js:336-362 is flags(uint) + peer + optional refresh32 + signature64. And C++ handle_announce has no announceSelf branch — the header comment at rpc_handlers.cpp:516-517 explicitly admits "JS treats announceSelf by populating _router; C++ uses the AnnounceStore unconditionally" — so a remote peer's self-announce to a persistent C++ node lands only in store_, and every subsequent JS findPeer hitting that node gets announce-formatted bytes where m.peer bytes are expected. Observable effect: mapFindPeer reads flags+31 pubkey bytes as publicKey and pubkey[31] as the relayAddresses array length — usually an out-of-bounds throw → null → the JS query silently drops that node (client never uses it as relay in connect.js:350-361), occasionally a garbage publicKey/relayAddresses record reaches the API. Divergence is real on both claimed axes: wrong payload format AND replying-from-store where JS replies null-or-router-record. Not fixed in working tree (rpc_handlers.cpp store fallback unchanged; file not among the branch's modified files). Severity HIGH for a wire-compatibility project: any persistent C++ node serving announces poisons JS findPeer, and in C++-heavy meshes (nospoon) discovery via those nodes fails outright; note handle_lookup (rpc_handlers.cpp:467-504) ships the same raw-announce values in its peers array, so the defect also corrupts LOOKUP replies.

confirmed.title: C++ DHT nodes never relay third-party PEER_HANDSHAKE/PEER_HOLEPUNCH (router else-branch unimplemented) — silent black hole
confirmed.divergence: JS onpeerhandshake/onpeerholepunch have an else-branch for nodes that are NOT the server host: FROM_CLIENT relays FROM_RELAY to relayAddress||relay (or req.reply(null,{closerNodes:true})), FROM_RELAY relays FROM_SECOND_RELAY, FROM_SERVER converts to a REPLY toward peerAddress. C++ handle_peer_handshake/handle_peer_holepunch return false whenever get(target) has no local ForwardEntry with an on_peer_* handler (i.e. the node is only a relay/storage node, not the server host). The caller ignores the false return, so no reply and no relay is emitted.
confirmed.scenario: A client (JS or C++) routes a handshake/holepunch through a C++ non-ephemeral node that stored the target's announce but does not host the server. C++ returns false → emits nothing → the requester times out on that relay. JS would have relayed onward using the stored `relay` address.
confirmed.consequence: C++ nodes acting as DHT relays cannot forward connect/holepunch traffic for other peers; connections routed through them stall. (In practice partially masked for JS peers by finding announce-2, which makes JS discard the C++ node before choosing it as relay.)
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side, read in full: .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/router.js:128-171 — onpeerhandshake has an explicit non-server else-branch: FROM_CLIENT relays a FROM_RELAY message to `relayAddress || relay` (or replies `closerNodes: true` at line 135 if neither is known); FROM_RELAY relays FROM_SECOND_RELAY to `relay` (149-160); FROM_SERVER converts to a REPLY sent toward peerAddress (162-168). onpeerholepunch:212-219 relays FROM_CLIENT to `peerAddress || relay` with no isServer requirement, and 239-245 converts FROM_SERVER to REPLY unconditionally. Crucially, .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/persistent.js:131-135 (onannounce, announceSelf path) does `dht._router.set(k, { relay: req.from, record, onconnect: null, ... })` — so every JS storage node that accepts a self-announce registers a relay-only forward entry and will relay third-party handshakes/holepunches toward the announcing server. C++ side: src/router.cpp:79-84 (handle_peer_handshake) and :179-180 (handle_peer_holepunch) return false whenever `get(target)` has no entry with a local on_peer_* handler — there is no else/relay branch at all. The only caller of Router::set in the tree is src/server.cpp:212 (local Server::listen); handle_announce in src/rpc_handlers.cpp:521-596 stores into AnnounceStore only and never registers a relay entry. The dispatch sites at src/rpc_handlers.cpp:134 and :147 discard the boolean return and emit nothing on false — verified silent drop, no closerNodes fallback either. Observable divergence: a persistent C++ node that stored a server's announce is listed as a relay in that record (handle_find_peer at rpc_handlers.cpp:430-456 happily serves it), then black-holes the client's FROM_CLIENT PEER_HANDSHAKE/PEER_HOLEPUNCH and also cannot perform the FROM_SERVER→REPLY return-path conversion; JS relays or replies in every one of these modes. Not fixed in tree: git status shows router.cpp and rpc_handlers.cpp unmodified. Severity HIGH stands: default C++ nodes go persistent after the firewall probe, and a node that advertises itself as a relay via findPeer and then drops the handshake actively degrades connects (masked today only because public-network relays are JS and clients try up to 3 relays).

confirmed.title: Announcer embeds relay addresses in the SIGNED announce record; JS announces an empty relayAddresses list
confirmed.divergence: JS _commit signs and sends a peer record with relayAddresses:[] (line 243-245); the actual relay addresses are delivered separately in the handshake reply (server.js:368), never in the stored/announced record. C++ commit() populates peer.relay_addresses from relays_ (the active relay list) before signing, so the announced/stored record carries up to 3 relay addresses.
confirmed.scenario: A C++ server announces to a JS storage node. JS onannounce verifies the signature (valid over C++'s own record) and stores encodeUnslab(m.peer,peer) WITH the relay addresses. A JS client's findPeer/lookup then sees relay addresses in the record that a JS server would never have advertised there.
confirmed.consequence: Different (larger) stored record than JS; wire-valid and signature-consistent, but semantically divergent — clients receive relay hints via the record instead of only via the handshake reply.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS announcer.js:240-249 signs relayAddresses:[] in every _commit and this.record (line 21) is never re-encoded; relays are advertised only via server.js:368 handshake reply. C++ src/announcer.cpp:290-296 signs peer.relay_addresses from relays_, and build_relays() (announcer.cpp:388-423) deliberately re-announces once with relay addresses ('Re-announce ONCE with relay addresses' comment) and re-encodes record_ with them. So stored/announced records from a C++ server genuinely carry up to 3 relay addresses that a JS server never stores — divergence is real and still in the working tree. Severity corrected to LOW: JS mapFindPeer (index.js:538-546) decodes the field but connect.js never consumes the record's relayAddresses (only reply.from, handshake payload, and _relayAddressesCache), and messages.js:301-310 encodes the field natively, so nothing breaks; the effect is wire-visible extra (possibly stale) data in the signed record, not a functional failure.

confirmed.title: FROM_SECOND_RELAY handshake reply is sent to req.from instead of the embedded relayAddress
confirmed.divergence: JS onpeerhandshake isServer FROM_SECOND_RELAY: `if (!relayAddress) return; req.relay(FROM_SERVER..., relayAddress, opts)` — the reply is relayed to the first-relay address embedded in the message. C++ accepts FROM_SECOND_RELAY but treats it identically to FROM_RELAY, sending the FROM_SERVER reply to req.from (the second relay) and ignoring relayAddress; it also does not drop when relayAddress is absent.
confirmed.scenario: A JS client connects to a C++ server through two relays, producing a FROM_SECOND_RELAY handshake at the C++ server. C++ sends the reply back to the immediate sender (second relay) rather than the first relay named in relayAddress; the double-relay return path breaks.
confirmed.consequence: Two-hop-relay handshakes to a C++ server are misrouted on the return path. Rare (single-relay is the common case).
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS router.js onpeerhandshake isServer FROM_SECOND_RELAY case: 'if (!relayAddress) return' then req.relay(FROM_SERVER..., relayAddress) — reply is relayed to the embedded first-relay address, dropped if absent. C++ src/router.cpp accepts MODE_FROM_SECOND_RELAY (line 97) but the reply lambda (lines 128-152) has one non-FROM_CLIENT branch that sets relay_req.to.addr = req_from (the second relay); hs_msg.relay_address is never consulted and there is no drop-if-absent. The file's own comments (lines 13-14 and 134-137) explicitly acknowledge the divergence as a TODO. Observable effect: FROM_SERVER goes to the wrong node; a JS second relay would then reply directly to the client from an address the client never contacted, which restricted-cone NATs drop — the double-relay handshake path fails for NATed clients. Severity corrected to MED: the path only occurs when the handshake traverses two relays (uncommon; the code comment calls it rare), but when hit the connection setup breaks entirely. Not fixed in the current working tree.

confirmed.title: Holepunch server handler is invoked for all incoming modes, not only FROM_RELAY
confirmed.divergence: JS onpeerholepunch only calls state.onpeerholepunch in the FROM_RELAY case; FROM_CLIENT relays onward and FROM_SERVER converts to a REPLY, neither invoking the server handler. C++ handle_peer_holepunch calls entry->on_peer_holepunch unconditionally for any mode (as long as a local ForwardEntry exists), then branches in the callback (FROM_CLIENT→REPLY, else→FROM_SERVER). It also lacks JS's FROM_RELAY guard `if (!isServer || !peerAddress) return`.
confirmed.scenario: A client sends a FROM_CLIENT holepunch directly to a C++ node that hosts the target server (self-relay). JS would relay it onward to peerAddress||relay; C++ instead runs the server holepunch handler locally and replies REPLY.
confirmed.consequence: Direct FROM_CLIENT holepunch to a self-hosting server is handled locally instead of relayed, diverging from JS routing. Edge case; normal flow delivers FROM_RELAY to the server.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/router.js:202-247 — onpeerholepunch switches on mode; state.onpeerholepunch is invoked ONLY in the FROM_RELAY case, guarded by `if (!isServer || !peerAddress) return`. FROM_CLIENT relays onward (`req.relay(..., peerAddress || relay)`), FROM_SERVER converts to a REPLY sent `to: peerAddress` — neither touches the server handler. C++ side verified: src/router.cpp:172-232 — handle_peer_holepunch calls `entry->on_peer_holepunch` unconditionally for ANY mode once a local ForwardEntry exists, then branches only in the reply callback (FROM_CLIENT→REPLY response, else→FROM_SERVER relay request). The `!peerAddress` guard is also absent — C++ substitutes `req.from.addr` (router.cpp:186) where JS drops the message. So the code divergence is real, not a misread. Severity correction to LOW because it is unreachable in the standard protocol: JS clients always send FROM_CLIENT to a third-party relay (connect.js:506→router.js:174-181 peerHolepunch always encodes FROM_CLIENT and sends to relayAddr), so a server node only ever receives FROM_RELAY — where C++ behavior matches JS exactly (run handler, send FROM_SERVER back to req.from with peerAddress=client). The finder's self-relay scenario additionally requires the server node to appear in its own announce relay set, which the JS announcer never produces (relays are the third-party nodes that stored the announce; C++'s only Router::set is the local server in src/server.cpp:212 — there are no relay-only entries at all, per the acknowledged 'relay-only forward case is not yet implemented' comment at router.cpp:167-169, a separate known gap). Divergent observable behavior therefore only occurs for crafted/out-of-spec packets (FROM_CLIENT or FROM_SERVER sent directly at a server node, or FROM_RELAY missing peerAddress): JS ignores or relays; C++ runs the local holepunch handler with a wrong client_addr (for FROM_CLIENT, hp.peerAddress is the SERVER's address, not the client's) and answers REPLY. Real divergence, negligible real-world impact; worth a mode guard when the relay path is implemented.

confirmed.title: Refresh-chain announce not honored: refresh token never stored and _onrefresh not implemented
confirmed.divergence: JS onannounce stores refresh preimage-hashes (refreshes.set at 145-147) when a full announce carries a refresh field, and _onrefresh re-adds the record (router.set or records.add) and rotates the token when a peer-null announce presents the refresh preimage. C++ handle_announce ignores the refresh field on full announces (no refresh store) and, on the peer-null path, merely replies without performing any refresh work (TODO at 539).
confirmed.scenario: A client that uses the refresh-chain optimization (full announce with refresh=hash(preimage), then cheap peer-null announces with the preimage) refreshes against a C++ storage node. The C++ node does not extend/re-add the record, so it expires at TTL despite the refreshes.
confirmed.consequence: Refresh-based re-announcement silently fails on C++ nodes. Not exercised by the current JS reference (announcer.js:246 and index.js:470 set refresh:null; refresh-chain.js is never required), so latent unless an older/other client uses refresh.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Both sides verified. JS (.analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/persistent.js): onannounce stores the refresh hash into this.refreshes at lines 145-147 on full announces, and routes peer-null announces to _onrefresh at lines 109-112; _onrefresh (lines 72-98) hashes the presented preimage, looks up the stored entry, re-adds the record (router.set for announceSelf at 83-88, records.add otherwise at 91), rotates the token (94-95), and replies. C++ (src/rpc_handlers.cpp): the full-announce path (lines 550-590) never reads ann.refresh — nothing is stored; the peer-null path (lines 536-548) has an explicit 'TODO: implement full refresh token handling (_onrefresh)' and just replies, doing no record work. The header comment at lines 514-515 admits the gap. So the divergence is real and unfixed: a refresh-chain client gets a success-looking reply from a C++ storage node while its record silently expires at TTL. Severity downgraded to LOW, however, because I verified that every announce sender in the current JS hyperdht codebase sends refresh: null — announcer.js:211 (_unannounce), announcer.js:246 (_commit), and index.js:470 (_requestAnnounce). The refresh-chain is server-side support for legacy/third-party clients only; no modern JS peer ever exercises this path against a C++ node, so the observable impact is confined to old or custom clients using the deprecated cheap-refresh optimization.

confirmed.title: Client-side unannounce() sends no UNANNOUNCE — it is a plain lookup stub
confirmed.divergence: JS lookupAndUnannounce runs a LOOKUP whose per-reply map detects nodes still holding our announcement and, via the commit fn, signs and sends an UNANNOUNCE (dht._requestUnannounce) to each such node; it also unlinks self from the local persistent store (index.js:203-206). C++ lookup_and_unannounce delegates to dht_ops::lookup with NO commit/unannounce, and unannounce() just wraps that. Zero UNANNOUNCE requests are ever transmitted. The comment at dht_storage.cpp:53-56 admits 'Not yet fully ported'.
confirmed.scenario: A client announces a key via the client-side announce path, then later calls dht.unannounce(publicKey, keyPair) to withdraw it. JS immediately removes the record from every node that holds it; C++ sends nothing, so the announcement lingers on the network until its TTL (~20 min / configured maxAge) expires and continues to be returned to other peers' lookups.
confirmed.consequence: Withdrawn peers remain discoverable/advertised for the full record TTL; callers relying on unannounce for prompt removal (e.g. going offline, key rotation) get silent no-op behavior.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS index.js:197-246: lookupAndUnannounce unlinks self from the persistent store and runs a LOOKUP whose map fn sends dht._requestUnannounce (signed) to every replying node whose peer list contains our publicKey (or is full at 20); unannounce() wraps it. C++ src/dht_storage.cpp:59-94: lookup_and_unannounce delegates to dht_ops::lookup with no map/commit; the comment at lines 53-56 admits 'Not yet fully ported' and claims the commit 'happens in dht_ops (TODO)' — grep of src/dht_ops.cpp and include/hyperdht/dht_ops.hpp shows only comments mention unannounce, no implementation. HyperDHT::unannounce (dht_storage.cpp:86-94) and FFI hyperdht_unannounce (ffi_storage.cpp:340-354) therefore transmit zero UNANNOUNCE messages — a silent no-op. Not masked elsewhere: Announcer::stop/unannounce_node (announcer.cpp:89-126) does send real UNANNOUNCEs but only on the server-close path with cached relays, not the standalone client API. Divergence is real. Severity corrected to MED: records self-heal via TTL expiry and the common server-close path unannounces correctly; the gap is the standalone client unannounce API being a no-op.

confirmed.title: ConnectionPool is unusable: pool() returns a detached copy and there is no attachServer
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/dht.cpp:638-640 (pool); src/connection_pool.cpp / include/hyperdht/connection_pool.hpp (no attach_server, no dht binding, no connection event)
confirmed.divergence: JS pool() returns `new ConnectionPool(this)` bound to the dht; the pool exposes _attachServer(server) which subscribes to the server's 'connection' events and funnels inbound streams through _attachStream so inbound (server) connections are deduped against outbound (client) ones and re-emitted via a unified pool 'connection' event. C++ HyperDHT::pool() returns a fresh ConnectionPool by VALUE (a disconnected empty object, not bound to the dht), and the C++ ConnectionPool class has no attach_server, no dht reference, and emits no connection event.
confirmed.scenario: User does `auto p = dht.pool(); server.on(connection -> p.attach_stream(...))` expecting inbound+outbound dedup like JS `pool.attachServer(server)`. In C++ there is no attach_server hook, and each pool() call yields a distinct detached object, so cross-direction dedup and the unified connection event never happen.
confirmed.consequence: The documented JS ConnectionPool contract (dedup a simultaneous inbound+outbound to the same peer, single 'connection' event) is not achievable via the C++ API.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side read: index.js:92-94 `pool() { return new ConnectionPool(this) }`; connection-pool.js:15-27 `_attachServer(server)` subscribes to the server's 'connection' event and funnels inbound sockets through `_attachStream(socket, true)`; lines 63-97 populate _connecting/_connections and emit the unified pool 'connection' event; server.js:30+193 (`if (this.pool) this.pool._attachServer(this)`) and connect.js:35+54 (`if (pool && pool.has(publicKey)) return pool.get(publicKey)`; `pool._attachStream(encryptedSocket, false)`) show the JS library itself wires both directions into the pool. C++ side read: src/dht.cpp:638-640 `HyperDHT::pool()` returns `connection_pool::ConnectionPool{}` by value — a fresh standalone container with no dht binding; include/hyperdht/connection_pool.hpp:85-124 has attach_stream/mark_opened/has/get but NO attach_server and no pool-level connection event (only per-ConnectionRef caller-set callbacks). Grep across src/, include/, test/ shows ZERO callers of attach_stream/mark_opened outside connection_pool.cpp itself: connect (dht.cpp:568-573) only checks `opts.pool->has()` to reject duplicates with -7 and never attaches the resulting stream (the comment at dht.cpp:541-542 even admits 'C++ leaves pool wiring to ConnectionPool helpers'), and server.cpp never references the connection pool at all. So a user-supplied pool is never populated by the library — has() is permanently false, dedup (in either direction) and the unified connection event never happen, and JS's return-existing-stream behavior on duplicate is replaced by an error. Two quibbles with the finding's framing: (a) the JS `this._dht` binding is stored but never used inside connection-pool.js, so 'not bound to the dht' is cosmetic, and (b) JS pool() also returns a distinct new object per call — the real defect is not the by-value return but that nothing in the C++ library ever feeds the pool. The substantive divergence (no attach_server, no inbound/outbound dedup, no connection event, outbound streams never attached) is confirmed by code on both sides. Severity MED not HIGH: opts.pool is an optional feature off the core connect/server path; nothing crashes — the pool is silently inert, which is a real API trap but not core-path breakage. Working tree has no fix (no attach_server anywhere, uncommitted changes don't touch connection_pool).

confirmed.title: Default HyperDHT never bootstraps the public network
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/dht.cpp:279-281 (bind: walk only if opts.bootstrap non-empty); include/hyperdht/dht.hpp:76-77 (default bootstrap vector empty)
confirmed.divergence: JS defaults opts.bootstrap to the 3 public BOOTSTRAP_NODES and auto-runs the bootstrap query at construction, driving NAT detection and the persistent transition. C++ leaves opts.bootstrap empty by default and bind() runs start_bootstrap_walk() only when it is non-empty, so a default-constructed node performs no background bootstrap walk, no NAT sampling from bootstrap, and never transitions to persistent unless the caller explicitly passes default_bootstrap_nodes(). (dht_ops::add_default_bootstrap still injects the 3 nodes into individual queries, so lookups reach the network, but the node itself does not join.)
confirmed.scenario: Construct HyperDHT with default options and call bind(). JS would join the public DHT and eventually flip to persistent/server-capable; C++ stays ephemeral with an empty routing table until an outbound query happens to seed it.
confirmed.consequence: Behavioral participation divergence: default C++ node does not self-bootstrap or reach persistent state; documented as intentional (dht.cpp:216-223) but observably different from JS default.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/hyperdht/index.js:28 reads `const bootstrap = opts.bootstrap || BOOTSTRAP_NODES` and constants.js:16-20 defines the 3 public nodes, so a default-constructed JS HyperDHT bootstraps automatically (dht-rpc ctor kicks off `_bootstrap` per index.js:82-84, cited in the C++ comment itself). C++ side verified: include/hyperdht/dht.hpp:76-77 declares `std::vector<compact::Ipv4Address> bootstrap;` with empty default, and src/dht.cpp:279-281 runs `start_bootstrap_walk()` only `if (!opts_.bootstrap.empty())`. Nothing anywhere substitutes the defaults into opts_.bootstrap: `default_bootstrap_nodes()` (dht.cpp:299) is only injected by the FFI layer (src/ffi_core.cpp:106-109, gated on `use_public_bootstrap`) and per-query by dht_ops.cpp:31 `add_default_bootstrap` — the latter seeds individual lookups but not the background walk/NAT sampling/persistent transition. So the divergence is REAL and observable exactly as the scenario describes. HOWEVER, it is a documented, deliberate design decision, not an oversight: src/dht.cpp:215-222 explicitly says "C++ instead keeps the bootstrap list empty by default (preserving the contract that existing offline tests rely on)... Callers who want JS's default behaviour pass opts.bootstrap = HyperDHT::default_bootstrap_nodes() explicitly." Severity corrected to MED (not HIGH): the primary consumer path (C FFI / wrappers) has the opt-in `use_public_bootstrap` flag, and the escape hatch is documented. Residual real problem: the dht.hpp:76 comment "Empty = use public defaults" is WRONG for the bootstrap walk (empty means no walk; only per-query injection uses defaults) — that misleading comment is worth fixing even if the intentional default stays.

confirmed.title: filterNode composes the caller filter; JS discards opts.filterNode (false parity comment)
confirmed.divergence: JS passes only the built-in `filterNode` function to super({...opts, filterNode}); the trailing `filterNode` key overwrites any opts.filterNode, so the user's filter is silently DISCARDED and only the 3-entry testnet blocklist applies. C++ ANDs the built-in blocklist with the caller-supplied filter_node. The comment at dht.cpp:116-122 claims this 'matches JS ... single-filter semantics where the built-in filter composes' — JS does not compose, it replaces.
confirmed.scenario: Caller passes opts.filter_node (C++) / opts.filterNode (JS) to reject some address range. In C++ that filter is honored (nodes dropped); in JS it is ignored and those nodes are accepted into the routing table / query results.
confirmed.consequence: Different set of accepted nodes when a caller supplies a filter; the C++ parity comment is inaccurate.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side: hyperdht/index.js constructor calls `super({ ...opts, port, bootstrap, nodes, filterNode })` — the trailing `filterNode` key (the module-level function at index.js:585-592, the 3-host testnet blocklist) overwrites any caller-supplied opts.filterNode after the spread. dht-rpc/index.js:80 then stores that single function as `this._filterNode` (used at index.js:484 and lib/query.js:275). So in JS a user's filterNode passed to HyperDHT is silently discarded; only the built-in blocklist runs. C++ side: src/dht.cpp:126-152 captures `opts_.filter_node` and installs a lambda that checks the built-in blocklist AND then the user filter (line 150: `if (user_filter) return user_filter(id, addr);`). Observable divergence is real: a caller supplying a node filter has it honored in C++ but ignored in JS, so routing-table/query-result membership differs. The comment at dht.cpp:114-125 claiming this "matches JS" is wrong — JS replaces, not composes. Not fixed in tree (the cited code IS the current working tree). Severity LOW, not higher: it only fires when a caller uses the filter_node option, has zero wire/interop impact (purely local node-acceptance policy), and the C++ behavior is a strict superset of JS filtering that matches what a caller would expect — arguably JS's discard is the bug. Fix is either matching JS (drop user filter) or keeping the AND and correcting the comment to say it's a deliberate deviation.

confirmed.title: announce opts.clear (clear-before-announce) not supported
