confirmed.js_ref: hyperdht/index.js:244-264 (announce routes to lookupAndUnannounce when opts.clear)
confirmed.divergence: JS announce with opts.clear=true runs lookupAndUnannounce (removing stale records for this key) instead of a plain lookup before committing the new ANNOUNCE. C++ announce always does a plain lookup+ANNOUNCE commit; there is no clear path (dht_ops.cpp:102-103 comment notes it is unported).
confirmed.scenario: Client re-announces a key with clear=true expecting old/duplicate records at other nodes to be withdrawn first. C++ leaves the stale records in place.
confirmed.consequence: Stale/duplicate announcements are not cleared on re-announce; peers may see multiple/old records until TTL.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: JS side verified: .analysis/js/hyperdht/index.js:244-250 — announce() returns `opts.clear ? this.lookupAndUnannounce(target, keyPair, opts) : this.lookup(target, opts)`, and lookupAndUnannounce (index.js:197-238) sends signed UNANNOUNCE to each replying node before the commit phase installs the new announce. C++ side verified: src/dht_ops.cpp:106-139 — announce() always builds a plain lookup query with an ANNOUNCE commit; no clear/opts parameter exists, and the comment at dht_ops.cpp:102-103 explicitly says the opts.clear route is "Not yet ported". The higher-level wrapper HyperDHT::announce (src/dht_storage.cpp:38-44) just delegates to dht_ops::announce with no clear path, and HyperDHT::lookup_and_unannounce itself is a stub that delegates to plain lookup (comment at dht_storage.cpp:52-57). So the divergence is real and unhandled in the working tree. Severity corrected to LOW: opts.clear is a user-facing option only — grep shows the single use in the whole JS package is index.js:250; hyperdht's own server/Announcer path never passes clear (announcer.js uses its own per-relay unannounce diff instead), stale records expire naturally via dht-rpc record TTL, and re-announcing overwrites records at the nodes it reaches. It is a missing optional API feature, not a protocol-correctness or connectivity bug.

confirmed.title: DUPLICATE_CONNECTION keep-new swaps synchronously instead of after the old stream closes
confirmed.js_ref: hyperdht/lib/connection-pool.js:37-55 (keepNew: destroy existing, re-_attachStream only on existing 'close')
confirmed.cpp_ref: src/connection_pool.cpp:57-77 (attach_stream keep-new: remove old + insert new synchronously)
confirmed.divergence: In JS keepNew, the new stream is NOT placed in the pool immediately; existing is destroyed with DUPLICATE_CONNECTION and only when existing's 'close' event fires (and the new one hasn't itself closed) does _attachStream(stream) run to insert the new stream and emit 'connection'. C++ synchronously removes the old ref, fires its on_destroy, and inserts the new ref in the same call, returning DUPLICATE_KEPT_NEW.
confirmed.scenario: Two connections to the same peer arrive; the new one wins the tie-break. Between old.destroy() and old's async close, JS get(remoteKey) still returns the OLD stream and no 'connection' fires; C++ has already swapped to the new. If the old stream's close never completes, JS never surfaces the new connection whereas C++ already did.
confirmed.consequence: Different observable pool state and 'connection' emission timing during the swap window; different failure mode if the losing stream hangs on close.
confirmed.verify_reasoning: Read both sides. JS .analysis/js/hyperdht/lib/connection-pool.js _attachStream keepNew branch: existing.on('close', () => { if (closed) return; ... this._attachStream(stream, opened) }).destroy(DUPLICATE_CONNECTION()) — the new stream is inserted and 'connection' emitted only AFTER the old stream's close event, and a closed-flag on the new stream drops it entirely if it dies while waiting. C++ src/connection_pool.cpp attach_stream keep-new path: remove(existing key), existing->on_destroy(), then synchronously inserts the new ref into connections_/connecting_ and returns DUPLICATE_KEPT_NEW — no deferred swap, no new-closed-while-waiting guard. Observable differences: (1) between old.destroy and old close, JS get() returns the old stream while C++ returns the new; (2) JS never surfaces a new stream that closed before the old finished closing, C++ already attached it. So the divergence is real, not a misread. Severity corrected down to LOW: dht.cpp integration is minimal — HyperDHT::pool() (line 638) returns a fresh ConnectionPool by value and connect() only calls opts.pool->has() (line 569), so the pool's keep-new path is not on the live connect/server pipeline today; impact is confined to unit tests (test_connection_pool.cpp) and future wiring. Not fixed in the current working tree — the code read IS the working tree.

coverage:
[dhtrpc-tick] Read in full on both sides: dht-rpc/index.js, dht-rpc/lib/io.js, nat-sampler/index.js, hyperdht/lib/nat.js (JS); src/rpc.cpp, include/hyperdht/rpc.hpp, src/dht_network.cpp, plus src/nat_sampler.cpp, include/hyperdht/nat_sampler.hpp, relevant slices of src/dht.cpp and src/server.cpp, include/hyperdht/messages.hpp (C++).

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT (no finding): CongestionWindow is_full/send/recv/drain/clear (rpc.cpp:87-116 vs io.js:556-591; C++ adds a harmless total<0 clamp). _onwakeup tick arithmetic tick+=2*OLD_NODE then tick+=8-(tick&7)-2 sets phase to 6 — C++ unsigned modular math yields the same result even for (tick&7)==7 (rpc.cpp:986-999 vs index.js:552-573). STABLE_TICKS=240 / MORE=720 / REFRESH=60 / SLEEPING_INTERVAL=15000 / RECENT_NODE=12 / OLD_NODE=360 constants all match. _ontick structure/order (wakeup-vs-bump, !bootstrapped early return, stable-tick recheck with last-host skip, ping_some on tick&7, refresh on (tick&63 && size<K)||--refresh<=0 with correct short-circuit, health last) matches (rpc.cpp:928-979 vs index.js:764-799). ping_some cnt=inflight>2?3:5, RECENT_NODE→cnt=2, the 'already-pinged-this-tick burns a slot without advancing' loop quirk, and empty-table→refresh all match (rpc.cpp:1004-1044 vs index.js:715-735). _check keep/remove-on-stale semantics and add-before-callback ordering match (rpc.cpp:877-911, handle_message add_node_from_network before on_response at 731-755, vs io.js:141-142). refresh target selection (random node else table id) matches (dht_network.cpp:126-171 vs index.js:435-438). validateId on both request and response, id-suppression note, and token-rotation/drain cadence (750ms, rotate every 10) match. Generic requests correctly use DEFAULT_RETRIES=3 matching JS Request.retries=3.

NOT FULLY COVERED / out of these files: health::HealthMonitor internals and the network-update mapping (separate subsystem); the JS global _ontimeout (index.js:709-713) that removes a table node on ANY request timeout carrying to.id — C++ has no such global hook in rpc.cpp, so query-target eviction depends on query.cpp (verify there). DELAYED_PING server-side handler and _pendingTimers (JS _ondelayedping index.js:693-703) not present in these files. server.cpp:978 feeds the SAME RpcSocket nat_sampler_ from PEER_HOLEPUNCH requests, whereas JS uses a separate per-puncher Nat (hyperdht/lib/nat.js) distinct from dht._nat — this cross-mixing (holepunch samples influencing the persistent-transition sampler) is a plausible further divergence worth a dedicated server.cpp audit. Also note check_persistent only starts the probe for CONSISTENT/OPEN firewall (rpc.cpp:1089) whereas JS runs _checkIfFirewalled whenever firewalled regardless of NAT type (index.js:821); the end state (stays ephemeral) matches so not reported, but the probe-traffic differs. delayed_ping uses retries=0 while JS delayedPing keeps default retries=3 unless opts.retry===false — a minor client-send divergence outside the tick focus.

[dhtrpc-io] Read in full on both sides: dht-rpc/lib/io.js, peer.js, session.js; src/rpc.cpp, src/rpc_handlers.cpp, include/hyperdht/rpc.hpp; plus supporting messages.hpp/messages.cpp, tokens.hpp/tokens.cpp, dht-rpc/lib/errors.js, dht-rpc/index.js, hyperdht/index.js and hyperdht/lib/persistent.js/constants.js for reply-token and error-code parity.

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: wire byte layout of request/response (type byte, flag bit assignments id=1/token=2/internal|closer=4/target|error=8/value=16, field order flags→tid→to→id→token→command/closerNodes→target/error→value) exactly matches io.js _encodeRequest/_sendReply/decode. peer.id / compute_peer_id (BLAKE2b over 6-byte host-LE+port-LE) match; validateId behavior matches for responses (resp.id cleared on mismatch, rpc.cpp:736) and is harmless-but-not-cleared for requests (req.id unused downstream). Token create/validate/rotation match JS token(addr,i): host-only input, current=index1/previous=index0, swap+hash-in-place rotation, both secrets accepted (tokens.cpp:36-62 vs io.js:148-159,297-304). Rotation cadence 10×750ms=7.5s matches. CongestionWindow (is_full 2×maxWindow OR per-bucket, send/recv/drain 4-bucket rotate, maxWindow=80) matches io.js:556-591; C++ adds a harmless underflow clamp. Retry count math matches (4 total sends: 1 + 3 retries; timeout fires when sent>retries). RTT recorded only when sent<=2 matches io.js:116. delayed_ping timeout=delay+1000 and retries=0 matches index.js:297. ID-suppression on replies (!ephemeral && from_server) matches io.js:488. Reply `to`/socket selection (reply on receiving socket) matches. PING_NAT reply-to-specified-port matches. Announce/unannounce token=false, mutable_get/find_peer/lookup token=true, SEQ_REUSED=16/SEQ_TOO_LOW=17 all match.

MINOR / NOT SEPARATELY FILED: (a) JS SEQ error replies include closerNodes (target present, error() closerNodes default true) whereas C++ mutable_put SEQ error omits closer_nodes — persistent.js scope, cosmetic. (b) MUTABLE_PUT/IMMUTABLE_PUT success replies: JS reply(null) sends a token, C++ omits it — same class as finding-4 but persistent.js scope. (c) io.js:84 rejects source port not in (0,65536) and drops all messages while suspended; C++ handle_message has neither guard — port-0 is not deliverable in practice, and message-drop-on-suspend belongs to the higher-level dht suspend() which I did not fully trace.

NOT FULLY TRACED: HyperDHT-level suspend()/resume() (dht.cpp) — rpc-level stop_tick/start_tick alone do not clear inflight with IO_SUSPENDED, close/rebind sockets, or drop inbound messages the way io.js suspend()/resume() (io.js:173-222) does, but dht.cpp may implement those; header comment rpc.hpp:269 claims dht.suspend/resume parity for what is only a timer stop. The query-engine's use of CMD_ANNOUNCE/CMD_MUTABLE_GET etc. as the *query* command (dht_ops.cpp:110) vs JS LOOKUP-then-commit is a query-subsystem concern, out of io scope, not evaluated here.

[query] Read in full on both sides: dht-rpc/lib/query.js (all 410 lines), src/query.cpp, include/hyperdht/query.hpp, src/dht_ops.cpp; plus dht-rpc/lib/io.js (retry/oncycle/congestion), dht-rpc/index.js (_request, _downHints* rate limit, retries defaults, map wiring), hyperdht/index.js (findPeer/lookup/announce/immutable*/mutable* opts, map fns, finished()), src/rpc.cpp (request()/send_now()/on_request_timeout()), routing_table.hpp/.cpp (K, compare_distance).

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT (no finding):
- push_closest insertion-sort, equal-id dedup (splices the NEW copy, keeps existing), and cap-at-k — matches query.js:334-351 exactly; the const-ref parameter does not alias into the vector so the swap loop is correct.
- is_closer (size<k OR compare<kth) == query.js:133-138. routing::compare_distance sign matches _compare (query.js:353-360); JS returns the raw numeric diff but only its sign is used.
- Missing is_closer() gate before push_closest (JS query.js:270 has it; C++ query.cpp:322 omits it) is HARMLESS: push_closest's cap-at-k pops a non-closer reply back off immediately, and an equal-id dup is spliced out — net result identical.
- Slowdown engage timing (set true AFTER the pop loop on the first tick when !from_table_ && successes==errors==0) and disengage (in on_visit_response only, using BASE concurrency, never in the timeout path) both match query.js:189-191,283-285 and the comment claims there are accurate.
- successes_<K/4 table-retry path, from_table_ flag semantics (set only when pending<k, even if table empty), and the bounded read_more recursion match query.js:196-205 and _addFromTable 111-120.
- seen=DONE set before the closerNodes loop; closerNodes own-id skip and filter_accept both applied; port-0 skip (H13) is extra but benign (invalid addr). The JS `break` on !_addPending vs C++ full-iterate+filter-at-pop yields the SAME visited set because closerNodes are distance-sorted and the k-th threshold is monotonic.
- Commit decrement-on-both (finish(true) on response, finish(false) on timeout, both decrement commit_inflight_) matches query.js:236 and the query.hpp:56-62 comment — accurate.
- inflight decrement ordering (before read_more) matches; single query-level timeout after RPC-layer retry exhaustion matches JS (oncycle retries internally, query._onerror fires once).
- closest_nodes() flattening == get closestNodes() (address list).
- The do_commit comment at query.cpp:465-467 ACCURATELY self-documents the empty-closest divergence (comment_claim_false=false); no false parity claims found in query.cpp/query.hpp.

NOT FULLY EXERCISED / downstream: map()-returning-null filtering (JS filters value-less replies via mapLookup/mapImmutable) is a hyperdht-layer concern, not query.cpp; only the query-level error!=0 gate (query-2) is reported. seen_ MAX_SEEN=4096 cap and pending MAX_PENDING=640 caps are C++-only safety limits, far above any real query fan-out, so not observable. I did not audit the higher-level HyperDHT::mutable_get/immutable_get aggregation (dht.cpp, out of scope) beyond the dht_ops early-destroy path, which correctly mirrors the JS for-await return/destroy.

[connect] Read in full on both sides: connect.js (851 lines), peer_connect.cpp/.hpp, connect.cpp; plus supporting JS truth router.js, messages.js, errors.js, constants.js, crypto.js, and C++ dht.hpp (ConnectResult/ConnectError/ConnectOptions), holepunch.hpp (try_direct_connect/holepunch_connect/HolepunchResult), dht_ops.hpp, and dht.cpp connect()/do_connect wiring. NOT FULLY AUDITED (separate subsystem — flagged for the holepunch auditor): the holepunch DRIVER logic that JS keeps inside connect.js — probeRound (555-629), roundPunch (631-711), updateHolepunch (505-553), tryLater backoff 10000+rand*10000 (713-716), TRY_LATER handling (527-533), random-punch throttle (639-668), openSession/analyze/nat.freeze — all live in src/holepunch.cpp in this port, not in the two assigned files. I confirmed the throttle exists (holepunch.cpp:353-565) but did not diff its timing/branches. Also NOT fully traced: blind_relay.cpp channel/pair internals (connect-11 consequence), query.cpp findPeer map/filter (connect-9 confidence medium), and the exact observable of the LAN path using local_addresses(0) + null udx_socket vs JS io.serverSocket (folded into connect-6).

Cross-checked router.js/router.cpp (handshake+holepunch dispatch, peerAddress/direct derivation) and secure-payload.js vs holepunch.cpp for the token; also skimmed holepunch.cpp punch() to confirm its bool return can be false.

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT (no finding): token(addr) hashes host-only with the local secret (secure-payload.js:47-51 == holepunch.cpp:140 called with client_address.host_string()); has_same_addr host+port membership (server.js:697-704 == server.cpp:114-122); NAT sampler add(to,from) argument order and the documented single-socket reason for dropping JS's `req.socket === p.socket` gate (server.js:509-510 == server.cpp:978); NAT freeze when firewall!=UNKNOWN before the reply is built (server.js:582-584 == server.cpp:982-984); fast-mode ping condition isConsistent && remoteAddress && hasSameAddr, pinging peerAddress (server.js:528-537 == server.cpp:1039-1046); TRY_LATER random-throttle wire result and `punching` field in that reply (server.js:553-574 == server_connection.cpp:388-421); version-mismatch/no-udx handshake error replies ARE sent by both (server.js:263-268 == server_connection.cpp:171-179); client-error holepunch → ABORTED reply (server.js:499-503 == server_connection.cpp:341-361); decrypt-failure → no reply (server.js:493 == server.cpp:1027-1029 guard on empty value); relay role/initiator selection and relayKeepAlive=5000 on the relay control connection (server.js:632-645 == server.cpp:653-668,733); the sync-path dedup IS same-tick (server.cpp:407-419 set at 879 within the synchronous call); prepunching→punch_clear_wait re-arm and the puncher-abort relayToken gate match the JS intent (server.js:548-551,401-412 == server.cpp:1083-1091,1156-1167); token only returned when is_server_relay (server.js:596 == server_connection.cpp:426-428); reply addresses = nat.addresses, remote_address=null (server.js:594-595 == server_connection.cpp:423-424).

NOT fully verified (out of assigned scope): Announcer stop() vs JS announcer.suspend()/resume() semantics on server.suspend/resume (announcer.cpp not read) — flagged only as a possible difference, not a finding; the FFI/wrapper caller's handling of ConnectionInfo.udx_socket==nullptr for the OPEN/direct path (feeds finding server-11); Holepuncher internals beyond punch()'s return value; socket-pool routes/reusableSocket route-add (server.js:315-317) noted absent in on_socket but reusable_socket defaults false so not raised as a standalone finding. </invoke>

[holepuncher] VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: (1) consistent_probe (holepunch.cpp:463-498) vs _consistentProbe (holepuncher.js:215-231): 10 rounds punch_round_ 1..10, non-initiator 1s lead-in that does NOT consume a round, unverified addrs probed only on rounds 4 & 8 ((n&3)==0), trailing 1000ms pause before autoDestroy — all match. (2) random_probes (holepunch.cpp:506-530) vs _randomProbes (holepuncher.js:234-244): 1750 budget, 20ms interval, port range [1000,65535] (randombytes_uniform(64536)+1000 == (1000+rand*64536)|0), single socket — match. (3) keep_alive_random_nat TTL cycling (first full cycle low TTL=5, then default=64; birthday_index_ reset decrements low_ttl_rounds once) matches _keepAliveRandomNat lines 247-269 EXCEPT the egress bug in finding 1. (4) is_unstable (holepunch.cpp:540-551) matches _unstable (holepuncher.js:95-102). (5) increment/decrement_randomized ordering and last_random_punch-on-connect (holepunch.cpp:441,553-568) match holepuncher.js:144,282-295. (6) non-initiator probe-echo (holepunch.cpp:420-423) matches holepuncher.js:125-128 for the single-socket server (ref.socket == socket_). (7) initiator winner-selection release-all-but-winner (holepunch.cpp:433-439) matches holepuncher.js:135-142. (8) local_addresses/match_address (holepunch.cpp:1834-1906) match holepuncher.js:337-386. (9) coerceFirewall OPEN->CONSISTENT is honored for OPEN (both direct-connect and treated-as-consistent); the only coercion divergence is UNKNOWN (finding 3).

NOT FULLY CHECKED / FLAGGED FOR SEPARATE AUDIT: (a) nat.js (add/_updateFirewall/_updateAddresses/autoSample) — the C++ counterpart is src/nat_sampler.cpp (NOT one of my assigned files); discover_pool_addresses (holepunch.cpp:1053-1138) only feeds it. One nuance to check there/here: JS nat.autoSample resolves EARLY when firewall becomes CONSISTENT/OPEN even with sampled<4, whereas the C++ run_round1 sampling-wait fast-path only checks `sampled()>=4` (holepunch.cpp:1794) plus a 2s cap — so C++ can wait up to ~2s longer before Round 2 when 3 consistent samples would already decide in JS. Timing-only; recommend confirming nat_sampler.cpp firewall-decision parity. (b) Sleeper overlap semantics differ: JS sleeper.js pause() while a prior pause is pending resolves the NEW promise immediately and leaks the OLD (executor overwrites _resolve), while C++ Sleeper::pause fires the OLD callback then arms the new (async_utils.cpp:30-39). Not reported as a finding because the punch flow is deliberately structured to avoid overlap (PunchState uses separate `sleeper` and `wait_sampler` instances precisely so pause() never overlaps). (c) SocketRef busy/idle/LINGER reuse machinery (socket_pool.cpp:81-102) is effectively DEAD CODE in the current wiring: SocketPool::acquire() is only reachable through Holepuncher::pool_, which is nullptr in both live callers, so SocketRefs are never created for live traffic and never carry udx streams. The C++ SocketRef also never subscribes to udx 'idle'/'busy' events, so was_busy_ stays false (linger-for-reuse never triggers) and close_maybe() doesn't gate on socket.idle — but since the class isn't exercised for streams, this produces no observable behavioral difference today and is not reported as a finding (it WOULD matter if the birthday-socket path from finding 1 is ever wired). (d) I did not exercise the code at runtime; all findings are from reading both sources with cross-referenced callers (connect.cpp:140/475, server.cpp:1010-1196, dht.cpp:259).

[secret-stream] Read both JS files (@hyperswarm/secret-stream/index.js, sodium-secretstream/index.js) and both C++ files (src/secret_stream.cpp, include/hyperdht/secret_stream.hpp) in full, and checked caller usage in src/ffi_stream.cpp, src/connect.cpp, src/server.cpp.

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: uint24_le framing (write/read_uint24_le); header frame layout 3+56 (uint24=56 + stream_id(32) + secretstream header(24)); NS_INITIATOR/NS_RESPONDER/NS_SEND stream-id derivation (BLAKE2b-256(ns, key=handshakeHash)); receive_header stream-id check + init_pull; encrypt = uint24(len+ABYTES)+push(TAG_MESSAGE), ABYTES=17; decrypt len<ABYTES reject; empty-keepalive swallow (plain empty && keepAlive!=0) — matches on the RECEIVER's keepAlive setting on both sides; keepalive refresh-on-write (encrypt→uv_timer_again) and swallow; box_message/unbox_message (8-byte counter prefix + 16 zero pad nonce, counter-exhaustion→destroy, 8+MB min length, crypto_secretbox_easy/open_easy); _onmessage/on_udx_recv_message drop-before-sendState; _setupSecretSend key derivation (crypto_generichash_batch over [NS_side, NS_SEND] keyed by hash); MAX_ATOMIC_WRITE bound; both sides send their header immediately regardless of initiator.

NOT FULLY EXERCISED: streamx read-side backpressure (_read pause/resume, push()===false → rawStream.pause()) — C++ has no read backpressure, only a 64-message pending cap; this is a known documented simplification, not reported as a wire divergence. Did not runtime-test udx's actual err code on peer hard-destroy (ended-1 confidence is medium for that reason). Did not audit the streamx 'handshake' event emission (C++ Duplex does not expose a separate handshake event vs connect) since it is not on the wire.

[noise] Read in full on both sides: JS noise-handshake/{noise,symmetric-state,cipher,hkdf,hmac,dh}.js, noise-curve-ed/index.js, hyperdht/lib/noise-wrap.js, hyperdht/lib/crypto.js; C++ src/noise_wrap.cpp + include/hyperdht/noise_wrap.hpp (plus a spot-check of src/peer_connect.cpp for the wrapper logic that C++ places outside noise_wrap.cpp).

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT:
- Protocol name / initial digest: JS builds 'Noise_IK_'+curve.ALG('Ed25519')+'_'+CIPHER_ALG('ChaChaPoly')+'_BLAKE2b' = "Noise_IK_Ed25519_ChaChaPoly_BLAKE2b" (35 bytes <= HASHLEN) → digest.set(protocol), rest zero (symmetric-state.js:13, noise.js:110-127). C++ noise_wrap.cpp:21,337-341 does fill(0)+memcpy of the same string. (C++ comment at :338 says "37 < 64"; actual strlen is 35 — harmless off-by-two in a comment, memcpy uses strlen so behavior is correct.) chaining_key = copy of digest BEFORE mixHash(prologue) on both sides (noise.js:130-132 vs noise_wrap.cpp:341-344).
- IK pre-message: initiator mixHash(rs), responder mixHash(own pub) (noise.js:126-152 vs noise_wrap.cpp:348-358). Match.
- Message token expansion IK msg1=[E,ES,S,SS] msg2=[E,EE,SE], and keyPattern() local/remote resolution for ES/SE/EE/SS on both initiator and responder recv/send paths — traced every mix_key(remote,local) call (noise.js:39-43,167-255,270-295 vs noise_wrap.cpp:361-516). All local/remote key selections match.
- mixKey/hkdf: dh→hkdf(chainingKey,dh)→[ck, temp32] (symmetric-state.js:37-42 vs noise_wrap.cpp:278-295). Match.
- HMAC-BLAKE2b 128-byte block, inner/outer pad 0x36/0x5c, key>BLOCKLEN hashed else zero-padded (hmac.js vs noise_wrap.cpp:55-105). Match, including empty-message-slot skipping.
- HKDF extract+expand counter scheme: T1=HMAC(PRK,0x01), T2=HMAC(PRK,T1||0x02), empty info (hkdf.js:14-42 vs noise_wrap.cpp:111-132). Match. split() uses hkdf(ck, empty) (symmetric-state.js:64-67 vs noise_wrap.cpp:310-318). Match.
- Ed25519 DH: SHA512(secretKey[0:32] = seed) → lower-32 scalar → clamp(248/127/64) → scalarmult_ed25519_noclamp (noise-curve-ed:43-71 vs noise_wrap.cpp:138-163). Exact match including that the SHA512 input is the 32-byte seed, not the full 64-byte secret key.
- Cipher nonce layout: 12-byte IETF nonce with LE uint32 counter at offset 4 (cipher.js:70-72 vs noise_wrap.cpp:169-178). Match. Nonce reset to 0 on initialise_key (cipher.js:11-14 vs CipherState::initialise_key). Nonce advances only on success on both sides.
- final()/split key assignment: tx=initiator?k1:k2, rx=initiator?k2:k1, hash=digest, and finalize timing (initiator finalizes on recv of msg2, responder on send of msg2) (noise.js:155-165,208,253 vs noise_wrap.cpp:424-430,507-513). Match.
- decrypt_and_hash length for TOK_S: JS hasKey?PKLEN+16:PKLEN → 48 in IK; C++ hardcodes 48 (noise.js:182-186 vs noise_wrap.cpp:464). Match for IK.
- Wrapper (out of assigned C++ file scope but in JS scope): prologue = BLAKE2b(BLAKE2b("hyperswarm/dht")||0x00) built in peer_connect.cpp:29-42 (= NS.PEER_HANDSHAKE), consistent with noise-wrap.js:11. holepunchSecret/remoteId derivation (noise-wrap.js:36-45) is intentionally moved out of the noise module into holepunch_connect per the documented note at peer_connect.cpp:304-309 — NOT verified byte-for-byte here since it lives outside the assigned noise_wrap.cpp/.hpp files.

THEORETICAL-ONLY DIFFERENCES (no observable divergence vs a real/legitimate peer, so not reported as findings):
1. Low-order DH rejection: C++ mix_key explicitly sets corrupt_ and aborts when scalarmult yields all-zero (noise_wrap.cpp:154-158,278-285); noise-curve-ed:64 does not check the scalarmult return. Only matters under an active low-order-point attack, and sodium-native almost certainly throws on the -1 return anyway (making JS also abort). Legitimate handshakes never produce a zero DH → no observable difference.
2. decrypt_and_hash mixes ciphertext into the digest even on auth failure (noise_wrap.cpp:303-308, comment says "Per Noise spec" — NOT a JS-parity claim); JS throws before accumulateDigest so it does not mix. Unobservable: C++ sets corrupt_ and the handshake object is discarded with no state reuse on either side.
3. C++ CipherState nonce-exhaustion guard (>= UINT32_MAX → empty, noise_wrap.cpp:243) and JS 65535-ciphertext-length throw (cipher.js:25,34) have no counterpart on the other side, but neither is reachable in a handshake (<=2 encrypts, tiny payloads).

NOT GOT TO / caveat: Did not independently confirm sodium-native's throw-vs-return behavior for crypto_scalarmult_ed25519_noclamp on the zero-output case (would settle item 1 definitively). Wrapper-level holepunchSecret and NoiseSecretStream.id derivations in C++ (holepunch_connect / secret_stream) were not byte-diffed since they fall outside the assigned noise_wrap.cpp/.hpp scope.

[compact] Read both JS (compact-encoding/index.js, compact-encoding-net/index.js, compact-encoding-bitfield/index.js, raw.js) and both C++ files (src/compact.cpp, include/hyperdht/compact.hpp) in full.

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: (1) Varint boundaries exact match on all three sides — preencode sizes (1/3/5/9), encode tag bytes 0xFD/0xFE/0xFF + LE payload, decode dispatch (compact.cpp:66-134 vs index.js:11-34). (2) Fixed ints Uint8/16/32/64 little-endian read/write match JS byte-order (JS uint16 decode `a + b*0x100` == C++ read_le16). (3) Bool ENCODE matches (0/1). (4) Buffer null/empty semantics: JS `if(b)` treats zero-length Buffer as truthy → encodes varint(0)=0x00 identical to null; decode of len==0 → null; C++ treats len==0 as null on both encode and preencode — byte-for-byte identical round-trip. (5) IPv4: 4 host octets network-order + port uint16 LE, 6 bytes fixed — matches. (6) IPv6: 16 host bytes big-endian group order + port uint16 LE, 18 bytes fixed — matches; Ipv6Address::host_string() reproduces JS ipv6.decode format exactly (per-group toString(16), no leading zeros, no :: compression, e.g. 'fe80:0:0:0:0:0:0:1') — this matters because host strings feed BLAKE2b token computation. (7) IPv6 from_string '::' compression verified by hand-tracing '::1' and 'fe80::1' — produces same 16 bytes as JS encode's copyWithin/split path. (8) Frame preencode/encode/decode match including trailing-byte skip (s.start=s.end) for forward-compat; C++ adds an early bounds guard (compact.hpp:264) but JS reaches the same error outcome via a later OOB throw. (9) Fixed32/Fixed64/Raw match. (10) has_bytes uses subtraction to avoid size_t overflow (correct).

NOT FULLY AUDITED / HANDOFF: compact-encoding-bitfield has NO counterpart in compact.cpp — it is hand-inlined in src/blind_relay.cpp (bitfield(7)→1 flags byte, bit0=isInitiator; JS blind-relay/index.js:480-517 uses `flags.encode(state, bits.of(m.isInitiator))`). I confirmed the JS bitfield(7) semantics (length<8 → byteLength 1, no length-prefix byte) but did NOT diff the C++ blind_relay.cpp encoder since that file is outside this assignment's file pair — recommend a dedicated blind_relay parity pass. Also: C++ compact does not port many JS codecs (uint24/40/48/56, int/zigzag, float32/64, bigint/biguint, utf8/ascii/hex/base64 strings, typedarrays, any, date, json, record) — none are used by HyperDHT/dht-rpc wire messages (grep confirms no uint24 usage in hyperdht/dht-rpc), so their absence is not a wire divergence, but note string fields in messages must go through Buffer (a messages.hpp concern, not audited here). JS uint64 decode returns a float (precision loss >2^53) while C++ returns exact uint64 — only diverges for absurd/adversarial >2^53 values, not reported.

[messages] Read in full both sides: JS hyperdht/lib/messages.js (all codecs), hyperdht/lib/encode.js (encodeUnslab helper — no wire content), dht-rpc/lib/io.js (RPC frame encode/decode, Request/decodeReply, CongestionWindow), plus compact-encoding raw.js/index.js (raw, buffer, array) and dht-rpc/lib/peer.js and hyperdht/lib/persistent.js to confirm wire semantics. C++: src/messages.cpp, include/hyperdht/messages.hpp, src/dht_messages.cpp, include/hyperdht/dht_messages.hpp, plus compact.hpp Buffer/Raw/Array and rpc_handlers.cpp handle_lookup/handle_announce to confirm the live paths.

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: RPC request frame (REQUEST_ID=0x03, flags id1/token2/internal4/target8/value16, order type→flags→uint16 tid→ipv4 to→id→token→uint command→target→value) matches io.js:520-553. RPC response frame (RESPONSE_ID=0x13, flags id1/token2/closer4/error8/value16, order type→flags→uint16 tid→ipv4 from→id→token→ipv4Array closerNodes→uint error→value) matches io.js:485-518 and decodeReply:608-625. Response error only flagged when >0 in both (C++ callers only set error to 16/17, never 0 — checked rpc_handlers.cpp:711,721). peer record (fixed32+ipv4Array), announce (flags peer1/refresh2/signature4/bump8, order + optionality), mutableSignable (uint seq+buffer value), mutablePutRequest (fixed32 pk+uint seq+buffer value+fixed64 sig), mutableGetResponse (uint seq+buffer value+fixed64 sig) all match messages.js byte-for-byte. Ipv4 6-byte layout (dht-rpc peer.ipv4 == compact-encoding-net) matches.

DID NOT AUDIT (not in assigned C++ files): the handshake, noisePayload, holepunchInfo/udxInfo/secretStreamInfo/relayThroughInfo, holepunch, and holepunchPayload codecs from messages.js — their C++ counterparts live in peer_connect/holepunch sources, outside src/messages.cpp and src/dht_messages.cpp. NOTE: C++ decode_request/decode_announce_msg reject flags>0xFF where JS tolerates them (forward-compat only; not observable with current flag bits, not reported). decode_lookup_reply/decode_peer_record are defined but unused in src/ (no C++ client LOOKUP-reply parse path), so the confirmed break is the C++-server→JS-client encode direction.

[routing] Read both JS (kademlia-routing-table/index.js, 203 lines) and C++ (routing_table.cpp + routing_table.hpp) in full, and the relevant rpc.cpp callers (add_node_from_network, on_bucket_full/ping-and-swap, rebuild_with_id trigger). VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: bucket_index/_diff for differing bytes (i*8+countl_zero(uint8) == i*8+Math.clz32(a^b)-24, both give bit-in-byte 0..7); Bucket binary-search add/remove/get insertion point and lexicographic compare (JS Row.compare starts at byteOffset for speed; C++ compares from byte 0 — same total order, no divergence); random() distribution (both floor(rand*size) over [0,size-1], both null/nullptr on empty, both skip empty rows/buckets); size accounting on add/remove. The closest() find (routing-1) is the substantive divergence. NOT fully covered by this file's scope: rebuild_with_id (routing_table.cpp:217-250) — its correctness depends on the node-enumeration ORDER used to re-add during the ephemeral→persistent transition and on on_full suppression matching JS dht-rpc index.js:854-864; the JS side lives in dht-rpc, not kademlia-routing-table, so I did not diff the enumeration order here — flag for the rpc audit. Also note: K is compile-time reduced to 10 under HYPERDHT_EMBEDDED (routing_table.hpp:20-24) vs JS's fixed 20 — intentional embedded tradeoff, changes bucket capacity/eviction only on embedded builds, not on normal builds. Bucket::oldest() is unused by rpc (rpc scans nodes itself) and has no JS counterpart.

[tokens-nat] TOKENS — Verified EQUIVALENT: token = BLAKE2b-256(host_string, secret) with secret as key/host as message (tokens.cpp:18-24 vs io.js:139-149); create uses host-only string with no port (rpc_handlers.cpp:202/526/615/669/789 pass req.from.addr.host_string(), matching io.js `b4a.from(addr.host)`); dual-secret accept-both on validate (tokens.cpp:53-61 vs io.js:95-97); 7.5s rotation = 10 * 750ms drain ticks (rpc.hpp:41-42, rpc.cpp:551-553 vs io.js `_rotateSecrets=10` at 750ms interval). I traced the rotation secret sequence on both sides across two rotations: JS ([S0,S1] creates with S1) and C++ ([current,previous] creates with current) produce DIFFERENT secret sequences, but tokens are node-local (created and validated only by the same node) and BOTH implementations preserve the identical one-rotation overlap window (the previous creating secret remains accepted for exactly one rotation, ~7.5–15s), so there is no wire divergence — not reported. NAT — Verified EQUIVALENT against hyperdht/lib/nat.js (which the C++ class actually implements, despite the assigned reference being the nat-sampler package): _updateFirewall ladder max>=3→CONSISTENT, max==1→RANDOM, and all three max==2 edge cases (samplesHost==1&&sampled>3→RANDOM; samplesHost>1&&samplesFull[1].hits>1→CONSISTENT; sampled>4→RANDOM) match (nat_sampler.cpp:139-171 vs nat.js _updateFirewall); the extra C++ guard `samples_full_.size()>1` at nat_sampler.cpp:161 is a safe no-op (samplesHost>1 implies samplesFull>=2). addSample bubble-sort matches nat.js addSample. _updateAddresses RANDOM (host,port0) and CONSISTENT (hits>=2 or first-2) match except the MAX_ADDRESSES cap (nat-3). The core mismatch is that C++ plugs a nat.js-style (deduping, non-evicting, top-sample-winner) sampler into dht-rpc's this._nat slot, whose JS spec is the nat-sampler PACKAGE (non-deduping, 32-ring-evicting, threshold-winner) — captured in nat-1 and nat-2. NOT fully traced: the firewall-probe path in JS (_checkIfFirewalled uses a SEPARATE fresh NatSampler and its port becomes the final peer.id) vs C++ finish_firewall_probe (counts replied hosts, reuses nat_sampler_.port() for the port-preservation check) — this is a known architectural difference and outside the tokens/nat-sampler file scope; I did not audit rpc.cpp's probe logic line-by-line. Also did not verify holepunch.cpp's consumption of nat_sampler addresses()/freeze()/unfreeze() timing against holepuncher.js.

[protomux] Read both sides in full: .analysis/js/protomux/index.js (768 lines) and src/protomux.cpp + include/hyperdht/protomux.hpp. VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: varint encode/decode boundaries (0xFC/0xFD/0xFE/0xFF) match compact-encoding exactly (cpp:22-85); buffer/string length-prefix encode/decode; OPEN frame layout [0,1,localId,protocol,id,handshake] uses local_id_ correctly (cpp:204-245 vs js:70-101); CLOSE frame [0,3,localId] uses local_id_ correctly (cpp:278-297 vs js:217-232, and close encodes localId not remoteId — unlike send); batch WIRE encoding in uncork (header 0x00 0x00 + first localId + per-entry [0x00 newId] switch + len+payload) matches _sendBatch byte-for-byte for sub-8MB batches (cpp:494-539 vs js:432-453); batch decode msg_len==0 channel-switch marker matches (cpp:679-680 vs js:573-576); control dispatch table 0/1/2/3 = batch/open/reject/close matches; pair/unpair/dispatch_notify key format 'protocol##hex(id)' and protocol-only fallback match (js:379-385,683-684); fully_open ordering (opened→onopen→drain) matches js _fullyOpen. The line-712 comment ('channel_id is the remote local ID = our remote_id') is CORRECT and is itself the proof that send()'s use of remote_id_ (finding protomux-1) is wrong. VERIFIED DIVERGENT: the 10 findings above. NOT fully chased: the JS Promise/queueTick async ordering (_fullyOpenSoon queueTick vs C++ synchronous try_pair) — C++ fires onopen synchronously where JS defers to next tick (js:112-115,412); this is a timing/ordering difference (createChannel returns before onopen in JS, after in C++) that I judged LOW and did not file. Also did not file: cork() underflow clamp (cpp:487 clamps to 0 vs JS leaving corked negative) — API-misuse edge, C++ comment is accurate. send() return value: C++ returns true on success (cpp:269) while JS returns mux.drained backpressure state (js:281) — LOW API difference, not filed. Did not exercise a live C++<->JS run; findings are from static code+wire analysis with line refs on both sides.

[blind-relay] Read both files in full plus errors.js, and traced client wiring in connect.cpp/server.cpp. VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: (1) wire format of pair/unpair — flags bitfield(7)/bitfield(0) as 1 byte with bit0=isInitiator, fixed32 token, c.uint id/seq; C++ encode_pair/encode_unpair match byte-for-byte (blind_relay.cpp:71-154 vs index.js:482-523). (2) link index mapping: JS links[+isInitiator] (0=non-initiator,1=initiator) matches C++ idx = is_initiator?1:0 and remote() = links[is_initiator?0:1] (hpp:233-235). (3) relay_to cross-connect direction (stream_a<->stream_b) matches JS relayTo(remote.stream). (4) client ALREADY_PAIRING / CHANNEL_DESTROYED guards and error codes match errors.js and hpp:76-82. (5) server duplicate-slot guard (pair.has vs `else if pair.links[+isInitiator] return`) equivalent; pairing_tokens_ inserted only after the guard, matching JS _pairing.add ordering. (6) NO client-side pairing TTL in C++ — matches JS (the added TTL/cap are server-side only, reported as blind-relay-7). (7) client on_pair delivers remoteId then removes request; JS keeps the request (Readable) until stream destroy — architectural adaptation, same observable remoteId delivery, not reported as it produces no wire/behavior difference against a normal relay. IMPORTANT REACHABILITY CAVEAT: BlindRelayServer/BlindRelaySession are never instantiated anywhere in the DHT (grep across src/include/examples/test), so all server-side findings (1,2,3,4,7) are latent — correct against the JS spec as divergences but not observable against a live peer until a relay node is built on this code. Client-side findings (5,6) are reachable but require a non-conforming/malicious relay. NOT fully traced: exact protomux behavior for send-on-closed-channel (assumed drop, standard protomux) and udx_stream_relay_to internals / udx destroy-after-remote-close semantics — these affect the precise consequence wording of findings 2 and 4 but not the existence of the divergence.

[router-announce] Read in full both sides: JS router.js/announcer.js/persistent.js/refresh-chain.js and C++ router.cpp/announcer.cpp/announce.cpp/announce_sig.cpp/rpc_handlers.cpp, plus messages.js (lookupRawReply/announce/peer encodings), index.js (mapLookup/mapFindPeer, findPeer/lookup), server.js (relayAddresses usage), and dht_ops.cpp/connect.cpp to trace how replies are produced and consumed.

VERIFIED EQUIVALENT: (1) annSignable / signAnnounce / signUnannounce — C++ incremental BLAKE2b over target||id||token||encode(peer)||refresh with NS prefix matches JS crypto_generichash_batch layout (announce_sig.cpp vs persistent.js:269-284); node id used = local node id on verify, storage-node id on sign, both sides. (2) verifyMutable / signMutable layout matches (persistent.js:236-267). (3) Relay-address cap of 3 matches on both the announcer commit (announcer.cpp:338) and the onannounce handler (rpc_handlers.cpp:552-554 vs persistent.js:121-123). (4) Router isServer FROM_CLIENT reply (mode REPLY, token:false, closerNodes:false) and FROM_RELAY relay (FROM_SERVER to req.from, peerAddress=client) match JS, with tid preserved. (5) FIND_NODE/PING/PING_NAT/DOWN_HINT/DELAYED_PING reply assembly and ID-suppression (!ephemeral && from_server) match JS io.js/index.js. (6) MIN_ACTIVE relay-health refresh threshold matches (announcer.js:119-121). (7) FIND_PEER via the router forward path returns the correct peer-only record (Server sets entry.record = announcer record).

Comment-claim check: no false parity comments found — the C++ comments in router.cpp (FROM_SECOND_RELAY not implemented), announcer.cpp (unannounce diff / pickBest), and rpc_handlers.cpp (lookup "we don't track bumps", findPeer store fallback, refresh TODO) all accurately disclose their divergences; findings announce-1/2/7 formalize the observable impact of those disclosed gaps.

NOT fully chased: the exact C++ client-side decode of LOOKUP replies (dht_ops/query custom decoder) — I confirmed C++ connect ignores findPeer reply CONTENT (connect.cpp:296-319 uses only reply.from_addr), which is why announce-1/2 do not break C++↔C++ connect but do break JS interop; I did not fully trace whether ffi_storage lookup consumers self-decode the custom format. Also did not exhaustively verify announcer background timing (5s ping / 5min reannounce vs JS 3s/~5min Sleeper) for behavioral impact — noted as an intentional, disclosed timing choice, not reported.

[dht-top] Read in full on both sides: connection-pool.js, constants.js, errors.js, hyperdht/index.js (JS) and src/dht.cpp, src/dht_ops.cpp, src/dht_storage.cpp, src/connection_pool.cpp, plus the relevant option/const sections of include/hyperdht/dht.hpp and connection_pool.hpp.

Verified EQUIVALENT (no finding): COMMANDS enum values 0-9 (constants.js:3-14 vs messages.hpp:50-59); FIREWALL UNKNOWN/OPEN/CONSISTENT/RANDOM = 0/1/2/3 (constants.js:24-29 vs peer_connect.hpp:35-38, dht.hpp:524-526); default bootstrap addresses (constants.js:16-20 vs dht.cpp:299-306 and dht_ops.cpp:33-35); connectionKeepAlive default 5000 (index.js:22 vs dht.hpp:63,107); randomPunchInterval default 20000 and deferRandomPunch last_random_punch=now seeding (index.js:24,57-58 vs dht.cpp:190-195); resume re-seeds last_random_punch under defer (index.js:96-98 vs dht.cpp:698-700); suspend/connectable gating — C++ suspended_ conflates JS _connectable + dht.suspended but net connect-blocking window (suspend-start .. resume) is equivalent (index.js:106-120, connect.js:49-51 vs dht.cpp:656-715, dht.cpp:561-565/601-605); destroy force skips UNANNOUNCE emission on both sides (index.js:122-133 vs dht.cpp:725-794, server force plumbing); mutableGet/mutablePut seq encoding (userSeq query value + seq<userSeq filter; mutablePut always seq=0 query) (index.js:312-330,383-386 vs dht_ops.cpp:266-298,314-373); mutableGet latest=true picks highest verified seq, latest=false returns first via query destroy (index.js:319-328 vs dht_storage.cpp:288-326 + dht_ops.cpp:338-368); immutableGet BLAKE2b(value)==target check then short-circuit destroy (index.js:266-279 vs dht_ops.cpp:196-228); commit uses reply token + reply.from targeting for immutable/mutable put (index.js:288-293,374-379,479-487 vs dht_ops.cpp:115-134,161-176,278-293); relay cache empty-guard and hex keying (connect.js:464-466 vs dht.cpp:337-357); server-side Announcer DOES emit signed UNANNOUNCE on close (announcer.cpp:353-372) — confirming finding dhttop-1 is specifically the client-side dht.unannounce() path, not the server path.

NOT fully traced: hypercore-id-encoding decode() at JS connect entry (index.js:81) — C++ connect() takes a strongly-typed noise::PubKey (raw 32 bytes) so there is no string-decoding surface; treated as an API-type difference, not a wire divergence, so not reported. The errors.js DHTError code strings are not surfaced as strings by the C++ ConnectError integer codes (dht.hpp:184-193) — a mapping/shape difference only, error semantics (which failures map to which codes) were spot-checked and left unreported as they are not wire-observable. Did not exercise runtime; findings are from static JS↔C++ diff with line refs on both sides.
